presentations.arrival: 55M, acute onset substernal pressure, diaphoretic, clutching chest

diagnostics.lookingFor: ST elevation in 2+ contiguous leads with reciprocal changes
Rising pattern confirms myocardial necrosis; may be normal early

differentials.distinguishing: ST elevation + reciprocal changes on ECG; acute onset with diaphoresis; rising troponin

treatments.intervention: Activate cath lab — PCI within 90 min of first medical contact
treatments.why: Mechanical reperfusion physically opens the blocked artery; time is muscle
Irreversible COX-1 inhibition prevents platelet aggregation at the clot; chewing speeds absorption
Prevents clot extension and new thrombus formation while awaiting cath lab
Dual antiplatelet therapy — blocks the second major platelet activation pathway (ADP receptor)

treatments.med: Nitroglycerin SL (if not inferior MI, not hypotensive)
treatments.why: Coronary vasodilation + venodilation decreases preload and myocardial oxygen demand

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect in pulmonary artery confirming clot
Elevated fibrin degradation products; sensitive but not specific — useful to rule OUT PE
Sinus tach most common; S1Q3T3 pattern (classic but uncommon); right heart strain

differentials.distinguishing: Pleuritic chest pain (worse with breathing); acute dyspnea out of proportion to exam; risk factors (immobility, surgery, OCP, cancer); tachycardia with clear lungs

treatments.why: Prevents clot propagation; does not dissolve existing clot but lets body's fibrinolysis work

treatments.intervention: tPA (alteplase) for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
treatments.why: Fibrinolytic directly dissolves clot when patient is in shock from PE

treatments.intervention: Supplemental O2 and hemodynamic support
treatments.why: V/Q mismatch causes hypoxemia; may need fluids cautiously (RV is preload-sensitive but can fail with overload)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Intimal flap separating true and false lumen
Widened mediastinum (>8cm); not always present
>20mmHg difference suggests dissection extending to subclavian artery

differentials.distinguishing: Sudden tearing/ripping pain radiating to back; maximal at onset (not crescendo); BP differential between arms; pulse deficits; hypertension

treatments.why: Rate AND BP control — must reduce aortic wall shear stress; target HR <60, SBP 100-120

treatments.intervention: Emergent surgical consult for Type A (ascending)
treatments.why: Type A dissections have high mortality without surgery; Type B may be managed medically

treatments.intervention: Pain control (morphine/fentanyl)
treatments.why: Pain drives sympathetic response which worsens dissection; controlling pain helps control BP

diagnostics.test: Clinical diagnosis (do NOT delay for imaging)
diagnostics.lookingFor: Absent breath sounds unilaterally, tracheal deviation, JVD, hypotension, tachycardia
Absent lung sliding on affected side; barcode sign on M-mode
Absent lung markings, mediastinal shift away from affected side

differentials.distinguishing: Acute dyspnea + unilateral absent breath sounds + hemodynamic instability; often post-trauma or post-procedure; tracheal deviation is a late sign

treatments.intervention: Needle decompression (14g needle, 2nd ICS midclavicular or 5th ICS anterior axillary)
treatments.why: Immediately releases trapped air under pressure that is compressing the heart and great vessels

treatments.intervention: Chest tube placement (follows needle decompression)
treatments.why: Definitive management — continuous drainage prevents reaccumulation

presentations.arrival: 72F, progressive dyspnea x 3 days, worse lying flat, can't walk to bathroom without stopping

diagnostics.lookingFor: BNP >400 strongly suggests HF; helps differentiate cardiac vs pulmonary dyspnea
Cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema (cephalization, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions)
Afib, LVH, ischemic changes — identify the trigger

differentials.distinguishing: Orthopnea, PND, bilateral crackles, JVD, peripheral edema, S3 gallop. History of HF, missed meds, dietary indiscretion (salt). BNP markedly elevated.

treatments.why: Loop diuretic removes excess volume — works in 5 min IV. Goal: net negative fluid balance
Venodilation decreases preload rapidly — redistributes fluid out of lungs. First-line for acute pulmonary edema

treatments.intervention: BiPAP (non-invasive ventilation)
treatments.why: Positive pressure splints open alveoli, reduces work of breathing, decreases preload and afterload. Buys time while diuretics work

diagnostics.lookingFor: Hypercapnia (elevated PaCO2), respiratory acidosis — indicates ventilatory failure
Hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms. Rule out pneumonia, PTX as triggers

differentials.distinguishing: Known COPD, barrel chest, prolonged expiratory phase, diffuse wheezing or diminished breath sounds, pursed-lip breathing. Triggered by infection, missed inhalers, cold air.

treatments.why: Beta-2 agonist + anticholinergic = dual bronchodilation. Give together for first 3 treatments in severe exacerbation
Systemic corticosteroids reduce airway inflammation — takes 4-6 hours for full effect. 5-day course, no taper needed

treatments.intervention: BiPAP if hypercapnic/tiring
treatments.why: Reduces work of breathing, supports ventilation, avoids intubation. Target SpO2 88-92% — don't over-oxygenate COPD patients

diagnostics.lookingFor: Lobar consolidation, infiltrate, air bronchograms
Elevated suggests bacterial infection; helps guide antibiotic decisions
Identify organism and sensitivities for targeted therapy

differentials.distinguishing: Productive cough, fever, focal crackles on auscultation, pleuritic chest pain. CXR shows infiltrate. May have leukocytosis.

treatments.why: Standard CAP coverage: ceftriaxone for typical organisms (Strep pneumo, H. flu), azithromycin for atypicals (Mycoplasma, Legionella)

treatments.intervention: Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%
treatments.why: Pneumonia causes V/Q mismatch → hypoxemia. Supplement oxygen as needed.

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect in pulmonary artery
If low clinical suspicion, negative D-dimer rules out PE

differentials.distinguishing: Acute onset dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + tachycardia with CLEAR lungs. Risk factors: immobility, recent surgery, OCP, cancer, prior DVT. Hypoxemia out of proportion to exam.

treatments.why: Prevents clot propagation. Start if high clinical suspicion — don't wait for CT

treatments.intervention: tPA for massive PE with hemodynamic instability
treatments.why: Fibrinolytic dissolves clot directly when patient is in obstructive shock from PE

presentations.arrival: 38F, acute onset RLQ pain x 12 hours, nausea, one episode of vomiting, low-grade fever

diagnostics.lookingFor: Enlarged appendix >6mm, periappendiceal fat stranding, appendicolith
Leukocytosis with left shift (elevated WBC, bandemia)
Rule out UTI/kidney stone as mimic. Mild pyuria can occur with appendicitis (inflamed appendix near ureter)

differentials.distinguishing: Classic migration: periumbilical pain → RLQ (McBurney's point). Anorexia, low-grade fever, rebound tenderness, guarding. Pain worsens with movement/coughing.

treatments.intervention: Surgical consult for appendectomy
treatments.why: Definitive treatment is surgical removal — laparoscopic preferred. Do not delay for imaging if clinical picture is clear
Empiric coverage for GI flora. Given pre-op and if perforation suspected

treatments.intervention: NPO, IV fluids, pain management
treatments.why: Prep for OR. Don't withhold pain meds — treated pain doesn't mask surgical exam (evidence-based)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Positive pregnancy test — ALWAYS check in reproductive-age females with abdominal pain
Empty uterus + adnexal mass or free fluid with positive hCG = ectopic until proven otherwise
Baseline H/H for bleeding risk. T&S in case of rupture needing transfusion/surgery

differentials.distinguishing: Reproductive-age female, missed period (may not realize she's pregnant), unilateral pelvic/lower abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding. If RUPTURED: peritoneal signs, tachycardia, hypotension — surgical emergency.

treatments.intervention: Emergent OB/GYN consult if ruptured or hemodynamically unstable
treatments.why: Ruptured ectopic = surgical emergency. Hemoperitoneum can be rapidly fatal
Medical management — inhibits rapidly dividing trophoblastic cells. Avoids surgery in stable, early ectopic

treatments.intervention: 2 large-bore IVs, Type & Crossmatch, RhoGAM if Rh-negative
treatments.why: Prepare for hemorrhage. RhoGAM prevents Rh sensitization in Rh-negative mothers

diagnostics.lookingFor: Elevated ≥3x upper limit of normal = diagnostic. More specific than amylase

diagnostics.test: CT abdomen (with contrast if no AKI)
diagnostics.lookingFor: Pancreatic inflammation, necrosis, peripancreatic fluid collections. Not needed for diagnosis if lipase + clinical picture are clear

differentials.distinguishing: Epigastric pain radiating to back, worse after eating, better leaning forward. Often alcohol or gallstone-related. Severe nausea/vomiting. Lipase markedly elevated.

treatments.intervention: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (LR preferred)
treatments.why: Pancreatitis causes massive third-spacing. Early aggressive hydration (250-500 mL/hr initially) improves outcomes

treatments.intervention: Pain control (IV opioids — fentanyl or hydromorphone)
treatments.why: Pancreatitis pain is severe. Morphine is acceptable despite old teaching about sphincter of Oddi spasm (debunked)

treatments.intervention: NPO initially, early enteral feeding when tolerated
treatments.why: Old practice was prolonged NPO. Current evidence supports early oral feeding (within 24-48 hrs) if tolerated — improves gut barrier, reduces infection

presentations.arrival: 78M, found confused and agitated by family, normally alert and oriented, no witnessed event

diagnostics.lookingFor: Glucose <70 mg/dL. ALWAYS check glucose first in AMS — takes 5 seconds and is immediately treatable
Confirm glucose, check electrolytes, renal function

differentials.distinguishing: Diabetic on insulin or sulfonylureas, tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, confusion, seizure. May mimic stroke perfectly. FASTEST reversible cause of AMS.

treatments.med: D50 (dextrose 50%) 25g IV push if IV access
treatments.why: Immediate glucose correction. Onset in seconds. Recheck glucose in 15 min — may need repeat

treatments.med: Glucagon 1mg IM if no IV access
treatments.why: Stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis. Slower onset (10-15 min). Won't work if glycogen depleted (alcoholics, liver failure)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Rule out hemorrhage. Ischemic stroke may be CT-negative early
Large vessel occlusion for potential thrombectomy
Rule out hypoglycemia first — it mimics stroke

differentials.distinguishing: Focal neurological deficits: face droop, arm drift, speech changes (BE-FAST). Sudden onset. LKN time is critical. Posterior strokes may present as isolated AMS/vertigo.

treatments.why: Fibrinolytic dissolves clot. 0.9 mg/kg, 10% bolus, rest over 60 min. Strict contraindication checklist

treatments.intervention: Thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion (up to 24 hrs)
treatments.why: Mechanical clot retrieval. Dramatically better outcomes for LVO strokes. Requires transfer to comprehensive stroke center if not available

diagnostics.lookingFor: ≥2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion
Identify source and organism
Pneumonia as source

differentials.distinguishing: AMS may be the ONLY sign in elderly sepsis — they may not mount fever or tachycardia. Look for infection source: UTI (most common in elderly), pneumonia, skin/soft tissue. SIRS criteria.

treatments.intervention: SEP-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics within 1 hr, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive
treatments.why: Every hour of delay in antibiotics increases mortality. Start the clock at recognition
Cover gram-positive, gram-negative, and anaerobes until cultures return

diagnostics.lookingFor: Electrolyte derangements, renal failure, hepatic encephalopathy (ammonia)
UTI — most common cause of delirium in elderly
New medications, anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids — all cause delirium in elderly

differentials.distinguishing: Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention (can't spell WORLD backward, can't do months backward). DIFFERENT from dementia (chronic, stable). Always has an underlying CAUSE — find it.

treatments.intervention: Treat the underlying cause
treatments.why: Delirium is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Fix the UTI, correct the sodium, stop the offending med, treat the pain

treatments.intervention: Non-pharmacologic measures first: reorient, glasses/hearing aids, mobilize, sleep hygiene
treatments.why: Antipsychotics (haloperidol) only for severe agitation threatening safety. Benzos worsen delirium (except in alcohol withdrawal)

presentations.arrival: 45M, T 39.2°C, rigors, tachycardia, looks sick, immunocompetent

diagnostics.lookingFor: ≥2 = tissue hypoperfusion, ≥4 = severe sepsis/septic shock
Pan-culture to identify source. Don't delay antibiotics for cultures
Leukocytosis or leukopenia, bandemia, organ dysfunction (creatinine, bilirubin)

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + SIRS criteria + suspected infection. Tachycardia, tachypnea, hypotension, AMS. Source may not be obvious initially — pan-culture and treat empirically.

treatments.intervention: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg fluid if hypotensive or lactate ≥4
treatments.why: Time-to-antibiotics is the most modifiable factor in sepsis survival

treatments.med: Norepinephrine drip if MAP <65 after fluid resuscitation
treatments.why: First-line vasopressor for septic shock. Alpha-1 vasoconstriction restores SVR

diagnostics.lookingFor: Elevated WBC (neutrophils = bacterial, lymphocytes = viral), elevated protein, decreased glucose (bacterial), gram stain + culture

diagnostics.test: CT head before LP if indicated
diagnostics.lookingFor: Only if: AMS, focal neuro deficits, papilledema, immunocompromised, seizure. Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT/LP
Positive in ~50% of bacterial meningitis — get before antibiotics but don't delay

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + headache + neck stiffness (nuchal rigidity). Photophobia, AMS, petechial rash (meningococcal). Kernig and Brudzinski signs (low sensitivity but specific). Can deteriorate rapidly.

treatments.why: Empiric bacterial coverage. Dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose — reduces inflammation, improves outcomes (especially Strep pneumo). Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP or CT

treatments.intervention: Droplet precautions until meningococcal ruled out
treatments.why: N. meningitidis is transmitted via respiratory droplets. Close contacts need prophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin)

diagnostics.lookingFor: Pyuria, bacteriuria, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase
Leukocytosis, assess renal function (may have AKI from obstruction or sepsis)

differentials.distinguishing: Fever + flank pain + costovertebral angle tenderness (CVA tenderness). Dysuria/frequency may or may not be present. More systemic than simple UTI — rigors, nausea, vomiting.

treatments.med: Ceftriaxone 1g IV (if admitting) or fluoroquinolone PO (if discharging)
treatments.why: Cover gram-negative uropathogens (E. coli most common). Check local antibiogram for resistance patterns

treatments.intervention: IV fluids + antipyretics
treatments.why: Dehydration from fever/vomiting. Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and comfort

presentations.arrival: 62M, witnessed collapse at church, brief LOC (~30 seconds), now alert and oriented, no seizure activity

diagnostics.lookingFor: Bradycardia, heart block, prolonged QTc, Brugada pattern, WPW (delta wave), VT
Intermittent arrhythmias that caused the syncope — may be paroxysmal
ACS as cause of arrhythmia

differentials.distinguishing: No prodrome (sudden), exertional syncope, palpitations before event, syncope while sitting/supine, family history of sudden cardiac death. ECG abnormalities. HIGH-RISK — admit for monitoring.

treatments.intervention: Continuous cardiac monitoring + cardiology consult
treatments.why: Cardiac syncope has significant mortality risk. Need to identify and treat the arrhythmia

treatments.intervention: If bradycardic: atropine → pacing per ACLS bradycardia algorithm
treatments.why: Symptomatic bradycardia causing syncope needs immediate rate support

diagnostics.lookingFor: Normal — rules out cardiac cause
Drop ≥20 systolic or ≥10 diastolic on standing

differentials.distinguishing: Clear prodrome: lightheadedness, warmth, nausea, tunnel vision, diaphoresis BEFORE the event. Triggered by prolonged standing, emotional stress, pain, heat. Brief LOC with rapid full recovery. BENIGN — but must rule out cardiac causes first.

treatments.intervention: Reassurance + education on triggers and counterpressure maneuvers
treatments.why: Vasovagal syncope is benign. Teach: sit or lie down at prodrome onset, leg crossing with tensing, adequate hydration/salt intake

treatments.intervention: Safe for discharge if ECG normal, no cardiac risk factors, classic prodrome
treatments.why: Low-risk syncope doesn't need admission. Follow up with PCP. Consider cardiology if recurrent.

diagnostics.lookingFor: Filling defect — syncope from PE indicates large/central clot with transient drop in cardiac output
Sinus tach, S1Q3T3, right heart strain, new RBBB

differentials.distinguishing: Syncope + dyspnea + tachycardia. Risk factors for VTE. Syncope from PE = significant hemodynamic compromise from a large clot. High mortality if missed.

treatments.med: Heparin drip immediately if high suspicion
treatments.why: Don't wait for CT if clinical picture strongly suggests PE with hemodynamic compromise

diagnostics.test: ALT >150 IU/L — 95% positive predictive value for gallstone etiology
Elevated direct bilirubin, ALP, GGT — suggest biliary obstruction
If cholangitis suspected (Charcot triad: fever + jaundice + RUQ pain), urgent ER

differentials.distinguishing: Epigastric abdominal pain — sudden onset, severe, constant, boring; radiates to the back in ~50% of ; Pain often worse in the supine position, partially relieved by leaning forward or fetal position; Nausea and vomiting — present in >80% of cases; vomiting does NOT relieve the pain

treatments.intervention: Lactated Ringer's (LR) preferred over normal saline — LR has anti-inflammatory properties (reduces SIRS, CRP) compared t
Rate: 250-500 mL/hr initially (or 5-10 mL/kg/hr) for the first 12-24 hours
Goal: urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, decreasing BUN and hematocrit, heart rate normalization

diagnostics.test: 0-4: Appendicitis unlikely; consider discharge with return precautions or observ
7-8: Probable appendicitis; surgical consult, CT to confirm

differentials.distinguishing: Pain may localize to the right flank or back rather than the RLQ; Minimal or absent abdominal tenderness (the appendix lies behind the cecum, away from the anterior p; Psoas sign may be the only positive finding

treatments.intervention: NPO — essential for anticipated surgical intervention; begin NPO status upon clinical suspicion
IV access — 18-gauge or larger peripheral IV; two IVs if septic or perforated
IV fluid resuscitation — normal saline or lactated Ringer's bolus (20 mL/kg for pediatric; 1-2 L for adults) then mainte

diagnostics.test: Transition point: Abrupt change from dilated proximal to decompressed distal bow

differentials.distinguishing: Inspection: Distension (may be asymmetric), visible peristalsis, surgical scars (look for ALL scars ; Auscultation: High-pitched, tinkling, or hyperactive bowel sounds with rushes; late — hypoactive or ; Palpation: Diffuse tenderness; focal tenderness with guarding and rebound = strangulation until prov

treatments.intervention: Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr
Monitor for signs of adequate resuscitation: improving tachycardia, normalizing lactate, adequate urine output
Indicated for significant vomiting, marked distension, or complete obstruction

differentials.distinguishing: <10: Mild withdrawal — may not need medication; 10-18: Moderate withdrawal — typically requires pharmacotherapy; 19-30: Severe withdrawal — aggressive treatment needed

treatments.intervention: Most intoxicated patients need observation, not treatment — time is the treatment
ABCs — protect airway (aspiration risk), monitor breathing
Fingerstick glucose — hypoglycemia from alcohol-induced hepatic gluconeogenesis impairment

diagnostics.test: CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) — most widely validated bedside tool:
CAM-ICU: Modified for intubated/nonverbal ICU patients; uses RASS (Richmond Agit

differentials.distinguishing: Hyperactive (25%): Agitation, restlessness, hallucinations, pulling at lines/tubes, combativeness — ; Hypoactive (50%): Lethargy, reduced responsiveness, withdrawal, quiet confusion — most common AND mo; Mixed (25%): Fluctuates between hyperactive and hypoactive

treatments.intervention: Treat infection with appropriate antibiotics
Correct metabolic derangements (Na, Ca, glucose, hydration)
Relieve urinary retention (catheterize if needed)

diagnostics.test: Non-contrast CT head: First-line emergency imaging; rules out hemorrhage; early
CT Angiography (CTA): Identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) for thrombectomy c
CT Perfusion (CTP): Identifies ischemic core vs salvageable penumbra; guides ext

differentials.distinguishing: Contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg in MCA); Aphasia (dominant hemisphere -- typically left): Broca's (expressive), Wernicke's (receptive), globa; Hemineglect/inattention (non-dominant hemisphere -- typically right)

treatments.intervention: IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg; 10% bolus, remainder over 60 min) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
Tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg) increasingly used; single bolus, non-inferior for LVO
Extended window (4.5-9 hours or wake-up stroke): guided by perfusion imaging mismatch or DWI-FLAIR mismatch

diagnostics.test: Substance used, route, timing, amount (usually from EMS, bystanders, or scene ev
Opioid use history — tolerance level, prior overdoses, recent abstinence

differentials.distinguishing: Somnolent but arousable to voice/stimulation; RR 10-12, SpO2 >92% on room air; Miosis present

treatments.intervention: Fentanyl's high receptor affinity may require higher total naloxone doses (4-10 mg or more)
Fentanyl's high lipophilicity creates a tissue reservoir → recurrent sedation likely as naloxone wears off
Carfentanil and nitazenes may require massive doses (>10 mg) or continuous infusion

diagnostics.test: Minimum 3-5 deep tissue/bone samples from different locations during surgery
Do NOT rely on wound swabs — they reflect surface flora, not deep infection

differentials.distinguishing: Fistula, sinus tract, or wound breakdown communicating with bone/hardware; Purulent drainage from the fracture site; Pathogens identified on ≥2 separate deep tissue/bone samples (same organism)

treatments.intervention: Always pathogen-directed — culture-guided, not empiric (except for acute sepsis)
Typical duration: 6-12 weeks (longer for retained hardware)
IV initially → may transition to PO (if oral agents with good bioavailability available)

diagnostics.test: High-sensitivity troponin (hs-cTnI or hs-cTnT) is the gold standard biomarker
Serial measurements are essential: 0-hour and 1-hour (rapid rule-out/rule-in) or
A rise and/or fall pattern with at least one value above the 99th percentile con

differentials.distinguishing: Chest pain or pressure — substernal, may radiate to left arm, jaw, neck, back, or epigastrium; Pain often described as squeezing, heaviness, or tightness; May be exertional or occur at rest

treatments.intervention: First-line antiplatelet; reduces mortality by ~25% in ACS
Chewed, not swallowed whole — chewing accelerates absorption (peak in 15 min vs 60 min)
Contraindications: true aspirin allergy (use clopidogrel as alternative), active GI hemorrhage

diagnostics.test: Low probability (≤ 4): D-dimer → if negative, PE excluded; if positive → CT angi
High probability (> 4): Proceed directly to CT angiography (skip D-dimer)

differentials.distinguishing: Dyspnea (most common symptom; sudden onset); Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse with inspiration; from pulmonary infarction); Tachypnea (most common sign)

treatments.intervention: Anticoagulation (same as below)
Close monitoring in ICU/step-down
Consider catheter-directed therapy or half-dose thrombolysis (evolving evidence)

differentials.distinguishing: Chest pain: Substernal, pressure/squeezing/heaviness ("elephant on my chest"); may radiate to left a; Diaphoresis (sweating); Nausea/vomiting (especially inferior MI → vagal stimulation)

treatments.intervention: Primary PCI is ALWAYS preferred when achievable within time window
Fibrinolysis contraindications: Active bleeding, prior ICH, ischemic stroke within 3 months, suspected aortic dissection
If fibrinolysis fails (ST elevation < 50% resolved after 90 minutes) → rescue PCI

diagnostics.test: Low probability (≤ 4): D-dimer → if negative, PE excluded; if positive → CT angi
High probability (> 4): Proceed directly to CT angiography (skip D-dimer)

differentials.distinguishing: Dyspnea (most common symptom; sudden onset); Pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse with inspiration; from pulmonary infarction); Tachypnea (most common sign)

treatments.intervention: Anticoagulation (same as below)
Close monitoring in ICU/step-down
Consider catheter-directed therapy or half-dose thrombolysis (evolving evidence)

presentations.chiefComplaint: Chief Complaint — Eye Complaints (Vision Change, Eye Pain, Red Eye)

diagnostics.test: Visual acuity — document carefully; decreased acuity suggests macular involvemen
Visual field testing (confrontation) — may reveal field cut corresponding to det
Pupillary exam — check for relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) in extensiv

differentials.distinguishing: Flashes (photopsia) — brief flashes of light, especially in peripheral vision, more noticeable in da; Mechanism: vitreous traction on retina stimulates photoreceptors mechanically; Floaters — sudden onset of new floaters, often described as "cobwebs," "gnats," "shower of spots"

treatments.intervention: Pneumatic retinopexy and gas tamponade: Patient must maintain specific head position (often face-down) for days to weeks
No flying until gas absorbs (2-8 weeks depending on gas type) — gas expands at altitude → catastrophic intraocular press
No nitrous oxide anesthesia — same mechanism; N₂O diffuses into gas bubble → pressure increase

presentations.chiefComplaint: Chief Complaint — Fever / Fever Without Source

diagnostics.test: Antigen detection: Results in 15-30 minutes. Widely available
Sensitivity: 50-70% — a negative RIDT does NOT rule out influenza. If clinical s
Specificity: >95% — a positive test is reliable

differentials.distinguishing: High fever (38.5-40°C / 101-104°F) — often the first symptom; Myalgias — diffuse, severe ("feels like being hit by a truck"); Headache (often frontal)

treatments.intervention: Age ≥65 or <2 years
Pregnant or ≤2 weeks postpartum
Immunocompromised

differentials.distinguishing: Lacrimation (tearing) — most common; Conjunctival injection (red eye); Nasal congestion or rhinorrhea

treatments.intervention: 100% O₂ at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask × 15-20 minutes
Effective in ~70-80% of attacks
Onset: 5-10 minutes

diagnostics.test: Non-contrast CT head: First-line emergency imaging; rules out hemorrhage; early
CT Angiography (CTA): Identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) for thrombectomy c
CT Perfusion (CTP): Identifies ischemic core vs salvageable penumbra; guides ext

differentials.distinguishing: Contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg in MCA); Aphasia (dominant hemisphere -- typically left): Broca's (expressive), Wernicke's (receptive), globa; Hemineglect/inattention (non-dominant hemisphere -- typically right)

treatments.intervention: IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg; 10% bolus, remainder over 60 min) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
Tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg) increasingly used; single bolus, non-inferior for LVO
Extended window (4.5-9 hours or wake-up stroke): guided by perfusion imaging mismatch or DWI-FLAIR mismatch

differentials.distinguishing: See full condition note for details

treatments.intervention: See full note

diagnostics.test: Respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheeze, stridor, hypoxia, reduced PEF)
Hypotension or associated symptoms (syncope, incontinence, collapse)

differentials.distinguishing: Urticaria (hives) — raised, pruritic, erythematous wheals; Flushing — diffuse erythema; Angioedema — deep tissue swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, face

treatments.intervention: Dose: 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1,000 (1 mg/mL) concentration IM
Site: Anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) — best absorption
Repeat: Every 5-15 minutes as needed — there is NO contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis

diagnostics.test: History is everything — what touched the skin, where, when, and what's the patte
Distribution pattern is the strongest diagnostic clue
Ask about: occupation, hobbies, new products, jewelry, topical medications

differentials.distinguishing: Erythema, edema, vesicles/bullae (especially ACD); Weeping, crusting; Intense pruritus

treatments.intervention: Identify and eliminate the contactant — the most important intervention
For nickel: clear nail polish on jewelry as barrier; nickel-free products
For glove allergy: switch to nitrile (avoid rubber accelerators); vinyl for low-risk tasks

differentials.distinguishing: Acute onset (within 1 week of known insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms); Severe dyspnea, tachypnea (RR often > 30); Tachycardia

treatments.intervention: Tidal volume 6 mL/kg IBW in volume-cycled modes (ACV, IMV) — this is non-negotiable
IBW determined by measured height (see IBW formula in Nursing Considerations)
Plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH₂O — unknown threshold for obese patients where low chest wall compliance raises plateau withou

differentials.distinguishing: See full condition note for details

treatments.intervention: Continuous pulse oximetry, cardiac monitoring
Obtain IV access (may not need immediately in mild-moderate)
Check PEF if patient can perform it

presentations.chiefComplaint: Chief Complaint — Vaginal Bleeding / OB-GYN Complaints

differentials.distinguishing: See full condition note for details

treatments.intervention: Magnesium sulfate — THE drug for eclamptic seizures AND seizure prophylaxis in severe preeclampsia
Loading: 4-6 g IV over 15-20 minutes
Maintenance: 1-2 g/hr continuous infusion

presentations.chiefComplaint: Chief Complaint — Weakness / Focal Neurological Deficit

diagnostics.test: Non-contrast CT head: First-line study; acutely hyperdense (bright) lesion in br
CT Angiography (CTA): Spot sign (active contrast extravasation) predicts hematom
Hematoma volume: ABC/2 method (A = greatest hemorrhage diameter on CT, B = perpe

differentials.distinguishing: Sudden onset of focal neurological deficit (similar to Ischemic Stroke but not reliably distinguisha; Headache (40-50%; more common than in ischemic stroke); Nausea and vomiting (common with elevated ICP)

treatments.intervention: Acute BP lowering to systolic <140 mmHg within 1 hour if presenting SBP 150-220 (INTERACT2 trial: safe, trend toward imp
IV agents: nicardipine, labetalol, clevidipine (continuous infusion preferred)
Avoid rapid drops >60 mmHg and hypotension (SBP <120 may cause renal injury)

diagnostics.test: Non-contrast CT head: First-line emergency imaging; rules out hemorrhage; early
CT Angiography (CTA): Identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) for thrombectomy c
CT Perfusion (CTP): Identifies ischemic core vs salvageable penumbra; guides ext

differentials.distinguishing: Contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg in MCA); Aphasia (dominant hemisphere -- typically left): Broca's (expressive), Wernicke's (receptive), globa; Hemineglect/inattention (non-dominant hemisphere -- typically right)

treatments.intervention: IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg; 10% bolus, remainder over 60 min) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
Tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg) increasingly used; single bolus, non-inferior for LVO

CT spine (initial): Fast, available; identifies fractures, dislocations, bony ca

differentials.distinguishing: ASIA A (Complete): No motor or sensory function in S4-S5 (sacral segments); ASIA B (Sensory incomplete): Sensory but no motor function below level, including S4-S5; ASIA C (Motor incomplete): Motor function below level with >50% of key muscles below grade 3

Spinal immobilization: Cervical collar, logroll, backboard (minimize time on board — pressure injury risk)

conditions.title: Sepsis
conditions.overview: Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock = sepsis + vasopressors needed to maintain MAP ≥65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation + lactate >2. Early recognition and treatment is the single biggest factor in survival.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Cascade
- Infection triggers **massive inflammatory response** (cytokines, complement, coagulation)
- Vasodilation → **distributive shock** (warm shock initially, cold shock late)
- Capillary leak → third-spacing → **relative hypovolemia**
- Microvascular thrombosis → **tissue hypoxia** → organ dysfunction
- Myocardial depression → decreased cardiac output

### Through the DO2/VO2 Lens
- DO2 drops: CO decreases (myocardial depression) + maldistribution of blood flow
- VO2 increases: metabolic demand surges from infection/inflammation
- Supply-demand mismatch → **lactate production** → lactic acidosis
conditions.keyFeatures:
### SIRS Criteria (MultiCare screening)
- Temp >38.0°C or <36.0°C
- HR >90
- RR >20 or PaCO2 <32
- WBC >12,000 or <4,000 or >10% bands

### Sepsis Red Flags
- **Altered mental status** (confusion, agitation)
- **Hypotension** (SBP <90 or MAP <65)
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L**
- **Mottled skin**, delayed cap refill
- **Decreased urine output** (<0.5 mL/kg/hr)
conditions.management:
### Hour-1 Bundle (SEP-1)
- **Lactate** — draw immediately, repeat if >2
- **Blood cultures** x2 before antibiotics (don't delay abx for cultures)
- **Broad-spectrum antibiotics** within 1 hour
- **30 mL/kg crystalloid** for hypotension or lactate ≥4 (start immediately)
- **Vasopressors** (norepinephrine first-line) if MAP <65 after fluids

### Reassess
- Repeat lactate if initial >2 — trending down = good
- Reassess volume status — passive leg raise, pulse pressure variation
- Target: MAP ≥65, UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate normalizing
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Time zero = recognition** — start the clock when SIRS + suspected infection identified
- 2 large-bore IVs — run fluids wide open
- Blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics but do NOT delay antibiotics to draw cultures
- Strict I&O — Foley catheter for UOP monitoring
- Serial lactate trending
- Continuous hemodynamic monitoring — watch for fluid responsiveness vs fluid overload
- Central line for vasopressors (peripheral norepi is acceptable temporarily per current evidence)
- **Communicate with provider** if MAP stays <65 despite 30 mL/kg — needs pressors
pearls: **Lactate is a perfusion marker, not an infection marker** — it reflects tissue hypoxia from any cause. A rising lactate in treated sepsis means the patient is losing ground.
**Don't withhold fluids because 'the lungs sound wet'** — sepsis is distributive shock. The fluid is third-spacing, not staying intravascular. Give the 30 mL/kg and reassess.
**Altered mental status may be the only sign** — elderly and immunocompromised patients may not mount fever or tachycardia. Confusion + infection = sepsis until proven otherwise.

conditions.title: STEMI
conditions.overview: Complete thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery causing transmural (full-thickness) myocardial necrosis. Time-critical emergency — every minute of occlusion kills more myocardium. "Time is muscle."
conditions.pathophysiology: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture or erosion exposes subendothelial collagen → platelet adhesion (via vWF) → platelet activation and aggregation → fibrin mesh formation → complete coronary occlusion → downstream myocardium loses oxygen supply → ischemia progresses to necrosis from endocardium outward (wavefront phenomenon). Necrosis is irreversible within 20-40 minutes without reperfusion. Infarcted tissue releases troponin, CK-MB, and inflammatory mediators.
conditions.keyFeatures: Crushing substernal chest pressure radiating to left arm/jaw/back. Diaphoresis, nausea, dyspnea. Women and diabetics may present atypically (fatigue, dyspnea, back pain, indigestion). ECG shows ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal ST depression. Troponin rises within 3-6 hours.
conditions.management: Activate cath lab immediately (Code STEMI). Door-to-balloon goal <90 minutes. ASA 325mg chewed + P2Y12 inhibitor + heparin drip. Nitroglycerin SL if not hypotensive and not inferior MI. Morphine only if pain uncontrolled (controversial — may worsen outcomes). Post-PCI: dual antiplatelet therapy, statin, beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor. Risk stratify with TIMI score.
conditions.nursingPriorities: 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival. Two large-bore IVs. Continuous cardiac monitoring. Serial troponins. Right-sided ECG if inferior STEMI (leads II, III, aVF) — determines if nitro/diuretics are safe. Monitor for reperfusion arrhythmias post-PCI. Strict I&O. Emotional support — this is terrifying for patients.
pearls: Inferior STEMI (II, III, aVF) — get a right-sided ECG. Right ventricular involvement means the patient is preload-dependent: NO nitro, NO diuretics, give fluids.
"Time is muscle" — every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases mortality. Treat ECG acquisition like a vital sign.
Wellens syndrome (deep symmetric T-wave inversions in V2-V3) = critical LAD stenosis about to occlude. Patient may feel fine between episodes. This is a pre-STEMI warning.
Post-discharge 5-drug bundle: ASA + P2Y12 + statin + beta-blocker + ACE inhibitor. Know why each one is there.

conditions.title: Pulmonary Embolism
conditions.overview: Thrombus (usually from DVT) lodges in the pulmonary vasculature, obstructing blood flow through the lungs. Ranges from incidental subsegmental PE to massive PE with hemodynamic collapse (obstructive shock). Time-critical — massive PE kills fast.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Obstruction
- Clot lodges in pulmonary artery → **dead space ventilation** (ventilated but not perfused lung)
- Increased pulmonary vascular resistance → **RV strain** → RV dilation → RV failure
- RV dilation pushes septum into LV → decreased LV filling → decreased CO → shock
- V/Q mismatch → **hypoxemia**

### Risk Factors (Virchow's Triad)
- **Stasis**: immobility, surgery, long travel, paralysis
- **Endothelial injury**: trauma, surgery, central lines
- **Hypercoagulability**: cancer, OCP, pregnancy, genetic (Factor V Leiden, etc.)
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Classic Presentation
- **Acute dyspnea** (most common symptom)
- **Pleuritic chest pain** (worse with breathing)
- **Tachycardia** — often out of proportion to other findings
- **Hypoxemia** — but SpO2 can be normal in smaller PE
- **Clear lungs** — this is key: tachycardic + dyspneic + clear lungs = think PE

### Massive PE Signs
- Hypotension, syncope, cardiac arrest (PEA)
- JVD, RV heave
- ECG: S1Q3T3 (classic but uncommon), right heart strain, sinus tach
conditions.management:
### Risk Stratification
- **Massive PE** (hypotension/shock): systemic tPA + heparin, consider catheter-directed therapy
- **Submassive PE** (RV strain, normal BP): heparin + close monitoring, consider tPA if deteriorating
- **Low-risk PE**: heparin, transition to DOAC, may be appropriate for outpatient treatment

### Anticoagulation
- **Heparin drip** (weight-based) — start immediately if high clinical suspicion, don't wait for CT
- Transition to DOAC (rivaroxaban, apixaban) for ongoing treatment
- Duration: 3-6 months minimum, longer if unprovoked or recurrent
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Oxygen** — high-flow if hypoxemic
- **Continuous monitoring** — watch for RV failure, hypotension
- **Heparin drip** — weight-based dosing, monitor aPTT q6h
- **Prepare for rapid deterioration** — massive PE can arrest without warning
- **If tPA given**: no invasive procedures, watch for bleeding, neuro checks
- **Elevate head of bed**, avoid Valsalva maneuvers
- **Emotional support** — patients with PE are terrified, often feel like they're dying
pearls: **Tachycardia + dyspnea + clear lungs = PE until proven otherwise.** Most other causes of dyspnea have abnormal lung findings.
**Don't wait for CT to start heparin** — if clinical suspicion is high (Wells score), anticoagulate immediately. The CT confirms, the heparin treats.
**PEA arrest in a young person = think massive PE.** Bedside echo showing RV dilation is your clue. tPA can be given during arrest for suspected massive PE.

conditions.title: Acute Ischemic Stroke
conditions.overview: Sudden loss of brain function due to interrupted blood supply to a region of the brain. Either thrombotic (clot forms in situ) or embolic (clot travels from elsewhere, usually the heart in afib). Time-critical — "time is brain." Every minute of occlusion = ~1.9 million neurons lost.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Ischemic Cascade
- Arterial occlusion → tissue distal to clot loses oxygen
- **Core infarct**: irreversibly damaged tissue (minutes)
- **Penumbra**: ischemic but salvageable tissue surrounding the core — THIS is what we're trying to save
- Without reperfusion, penumbra converts to core over hours

### Why Timing Matters
- tPA window: **within 4.5 hours** of last known normal (LKN)
- Thrombectomy window: **up to 24 hours** for large vessel occlusion with salvageable tissue on imaging
conditions.keyFeatures:
### BE-FAST Screening
- **B**alance — sudden loss of balance/coordination
- **E**yes — sudden vision loss or double vision
- **F**ace — facial droop (ask to smile)
- **A**rm — arm drift (hold both arms out, eyes closed)
- **S**peech — slurred or garbled speech
- **T**ime — note the LKN time

### Important Distinctions
- **LKN (last known normal)** — NOT when symptoms were found. If patient woke up with symptoms, LKN = when they were last seen normal (bedtime)
- Posterior circulation strokes (brainstem/cerebellum): vertigo, ataxia, diplopia — easy to miss
conditions.management:
### Code Stroke Timeline (MultiCare)
- **5 min**: MD at bedside
- **10 min**: CT head started
- **20 min**: CT interpreted
- **35 min**: tPA decision
- **45 min**: tPA administered if indicated

### Thrombolytics
- **Alteplase (tPA)**: 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg), 10% as bolus, rest over 60 min
- Within 4.5 hours of LKN
- **Contraindications**: active bleeding, recent surgery, INR >1.7, platelets <100K, BP >185/110

### Thrombectomy
- Large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, basilar)
- Up to 24 hours with favorable imaging
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **LKN time is CRITICAL** — ask family, witnesses, check cameras, phone records
- **NIHSS** — perform and document baseline and serial assessments
- **BP management**: keep <185/110 pre-tPA, <180/105 post-tPA x 24 hrs
- **1:1 nursing for 6 hours post-tPA** — neuro checks q15 min x 2 hr, q30 min x 6 hr
- **Watch for hemorrhagic conversion** — sudden headache, worsening neuro exam, vomiting = STOP tPA, stat CT
- **NPO until swallow screen** — aspiration risk is high
- **Blood glucose** — keep 140-180, hypoglycemia worsens outcomes
pearls: **The LKN time drives EVERYTHING** — not when symptoms were noticed, not when EMS was called. If the patient woke up with symptoms, LKN = last time seen normal before sleep.
**Don't drop the BP too fast** — the hypertension is compensatory, maintaining perfusion to the penumbra. Only treat to get below thrombolytic thresholds.
**Posterior circulation strokes are the ones we miss** — dizziness, nausea, ataxia, diplopia don't scream 'stroke' the way face/arm/speech do. Always consider it.

conditions.title: DKA
conditions.overview: Metabolic emergency from absolute or relative insulin deficiency. Hyperglycemia + ketoacidosis + dehydration. Most common in Type 1 DM but occurs in Type 2 with illness/stress. Mortality <1% with proper treatment, but mistreatment (especially K+ mismanagement) kills.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### The Cascade
- **No insulin** = cells can't use glucose = glucose rises in blood
- Body switches to **fat metabolism** = free fatty acids = hepatic ketogenesis = **ketone bodies**
- Ketones are acids = **anion gap metabolic acidosis**
- Hyperglycemia = **osmotic diuresis** = massive fluid loss (3-6L deficit typical)
- Fluid loss = electrolyte depletion (K+, Na+, Phos, Mg2+)

### The Potassium Trap
- Total body K+ is DEPLETED from osmotic diuresis
- But serum K+ may appear **normal or high** because acidosis shifts K+ out of cells
- When you give insulin and correct acidosis, K+ shifts BACK into cells = **life-threatening hypokalemia**
- **DO NOT give insulin if K+ <3.3** = replace K+ first
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Classic Triad
- **Hyperglycemia** (usually >250, often >500)
- **Ketosis** (positive ketones, elevated beta-hydroxybutyrate)
- **Acidosis** (pH <7.3, bicarb <18, anion gap >12)

### Presentation
- Polyuria, polydipsia, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain
- **Kussmaul respirations** = deep, rapid breathing (compensatory)
- **Fruity breath** (acetone)
- Dehydration, tachycardia, altered mental status
- **Find the trigger**: infection, missed insulin, new-onset DM, MI, steroids
conditions.management:
### MultiCare DKA Protocol
- **Fluids first**: NS 1-2L bolus, then 250-500 mL/hr. Switch to D5 1/2 NS when glucose <200
- **Potassium**: Check BEFORE insulin. K+ <3.3 = replace first, hold insulin. K+ 3.3-5.3 = add 20-40 mEq to each liter. K+ >5.3 = hold K+, recheck q2h
- **Insulin drip**: 0.1-0.14 units/kg/hr. Goal: glucose drop 50-75/hr. Add dextrose when glucose <200. Do NOT stop insulin until anion gap closes
- **Bicarb**: only if pH <6.9
- **Monitor**: BMP q2h, glucose q1h, strict I&O
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Check K+ BEFORE starting insulin** = life-saving step
- **Two IVs** = one for fluids, one for insulin drip
- **Hourly glucose checks** = titrate insulin to drop 50-75/hr
- **BMP every 2 hours** = watching K+, bicarb, anion gap
- **Continuous cardiac monitoring** = K+ shifts cause arrhythmias
- **Keep insulin drip until gap closes** = overlap SubQ insulin 2hr before stopping drip
- **Find the trigger** = why did they go into DKA?
pearls: **Check potassium before insulin, every single time.** Giving insulin to a hypokalemic patient can kill them.
**Don't stop the insulin drip when glucose normalizes** = the goal is to close the anion gap. Add dextrose and keep the drip running.
**Abdominal pain in DKA is often the DKA itself**, not a surgical abdomen. It resolves with treatment.

conditions.title: Anaphylaxis
conditions.overview: Severe, life-threatening systemic allergic reaction. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation causes vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, mucosal edema. Can kill in minutes. EPINEPHRINE IS THE TREATMENT.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Type I Hypersensitivity
- Prior sensitization = IgE antibodies on mast cells
- Re-exposure = antigen cross-links IgE = **mast cell degranulation**
- **Histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins** released:
  - Vasodilation = distributive shock
  - Bronchoconstriction = wheezing, respiratory distress
  - Mucosal edema = airway swelling
  - Capillary leak = urticaria, third-spacing
- Biphasic reactions in 5-20% = can recur 4-12 hours later
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Diagnostic Criteria
- Skin/mucosal involvement (hives, angioedema) + respiratory compromise OR hypotension
- Two or more rapidly after allergen: skin, respiratory, GI, hypotension
- Hypotension after KNOWN allergen

### Common Triggers
- Foods (peanuts, shellfish, tree nuts)
- Medications (antibiotics, NSAIDs, contrast)
- Insect stings (bees, wasps)
- Latex
conditions.management:
### EPINEPHRINE FIRST
- **Epi 0.3-0.5 mg IM** anterolateral thigh. Repeat q5-15 min if no improvement
- **Epi drip** 1-10 mcg/min for refractory cases

### Adjuncts (AFTER epi)
- IV fluids = aggressive for distributive shock
- Albuterol neb = for bronchospasm
- Diphenhydramine 50mg IV = treats urticaria, NOT first-line
- Methylprednisolone 125mg IV = may reduce biphasic reaction
- Prepare for intubation if airway edema progressing
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **EPI FIRST** = IM anterolateral thigh. Nothing else matters until epi is given
- **Two large-bore IVs** = fluids wide open for shock
- **Continuous monitoring** = watch for airway compromise
- **Observe 4-6 hours** = biphasic reactions
- **Before discharge**: epi auto-injector Rx, allergy referral, action plan
pearls: **Epi is first, epi is second, epi is third.** Benadryl is for hives, not anaphylaxis.
**IM in the thigh, not subQ in the arm.** Anterolateral thigh has fastest absorption.
**Observe 4-6 hours** = biphasic reactions in 5-20% of patients.

conditions.title: Atrial Fibrillation
conditions.overview: Most common sustained arrhythmia. Disorganized atrial electrical activity causes irregular, often rapid ventricular response. Major stroke risk from atrial thrombus formation.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Electrical Chaos
- Multiple re-entrant wavelets in atria = chaotic depolarization (350-600/min)
- AV node filters irregularly = **irregularly irregular** ventricular rate
- Loss of organized atrial contraction = blood **stasis** = thrombus = stroke

### Hemodynamic Impact
- Loss of atrial kick (15-25% of CO)
- Rapid rate = decreased diastolic filling = decreased CO
- Chronic uncontrolled = tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy
conditions.keyFeatures:
### ECG
- **No P waves** = fibrillatory baseline
- **Irregularly irregular** R-R intervals
- Narrow QRS (unless aberrancy or BBB)

### Presentation
- Palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue
- If RVR: chest pain, dizziness, near-syncope
- May be asymptomatic (incidental finding)
- Stroke may be the presenting event
conditions.management:
### Stable or Unstable?
- Unstable (hypotension, AMS, chest pain) = synchronized cardioversion per ACLS

### Rate Control
- **Diltiazem** 0.25 mg/kg IV over 2 min, drip 5-15 mg/hr (first-line)
- **Metoprolol** 5mg IV q5min x3 (alternative, preferred in HFrEF)
- Target HR <110

### Rhythm Control
- Afib <48 hours = may cardiovert
- >48 hours or unknown = need 3+ weeks anticoagulation OR TEE first

### Anticoagulation
- CHA2DS2-VASc score determines need
- Most afib patients need long-term anticoagulation (DOAC preferred)
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Rate control is the ED priority** = get HR <110
- **Diltiazem drip**: monitor BP, hold if SBP <90
- **Do NOT cardiovert unknown-duration afib** without anticoag or TEE = stroke risk
- **Look for the trigger**: thyroid, PE, sepsis, alcohol, missed meds
- **Anticoagulation discussion before discharge**
pearls: **New afib = always ask WHY.** Afib is often a symptom of PE, sepsis, thyroid storm, acute HF, or alcohol.
**Never give diltiazem to afib with WPW** (delta waves on ECG). Forces conduction down accessory pathway = can degenerate to VFib.
**Afib with RVR is rate control, not cardioversion** (unless unstable).

conditions.title: Hyperkalemia
conditions.overview: Serum K+ >5.0. Life-threatening when >6.0 or with ECG changes. The heart is exquisitely sensitive to potassium. Treat the ECG, not just the number.
conditions.pathophysiology:
### Why It's Dangerous
- Elevated K+ = resting membrane potential less negative = cells easier to depolarize initially
- Higher levels = Na+ channels inactivate = conduction slows = cardiac arrest
- ECG progression: **peaked T waves = PR prolongation = widened QRS = sine wave = VFib/asystole**

### Common Causes
- **Renal failure** (most common)
- **Medications**: ACE-I, ARBs, K+-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs, TMP-SMX
- **Cell lysis**: rhabdo, tumor lysis, hemolysis, burns
- **Acidosis**: H+ in, K+ out
conditions.keyFeatures:
### ECG Changes (progressive)
- 5.5-6.0: Peaked T waves
- 6.0-7.0: PR prolongation, flattened P waves
- 7.0-8.0: Widened QRS
- >8.0: Sine wave = VFib = asystole

### Symptoms
- Often **asymptomatic** until cardiac arrest
- Weakness, paresthesias, nausea
conditions.management:
### Three Categories
**1. STABILIZE** (minutes)
- **Calcium gluconate 10mL of 10% IV over 2-3 min** = does NOT lower K+, stabilizes heart

**2. SHIFT** (15-30 min, temporary)
- **Insulin 10 units IV + D50 25g** = most effective. ALWAYS give dextrose
- **Albuterol 10-20mg neb** = adjunct
- **Bicarb** = only if acidotic

**3. REMOVE** (hours, definitive)
- **Furosemide** = if kidneys work
- **Kayexalate** = slow, controversial
- **Dialysis** = definitive for renal failure
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **12-lead ECG immediately** = peaked T waves = treat NOW
- **Calcium gluconate at bedside** if K+ >6.0
- **Give insulin AND dextrose together** = check glucose q30 min
- **Continuous cardiac monitoring**
- **Recheck K+ in 1-2 hours** = shift treatments are temporary
- **Avoid K+-containing fluids (LR)** until resolved
pearls: **Calcium gluconate is a membrane stabilizer, not a K+ treatment.** It buys time while insulin/glucose shifts K+ into cells.
**Always give dextrose with insulin.** Insulin without dextrose = hypoglycemia.
**Peaked T waves = calcium gluconate immediately.** Don't wait for the lab.

conditions.title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
conditions.overview: A permanent, irreversible localized dilatation of the infrarenal aorta involving all three vessel wall layers (intima, media, adventitia), defined as an aortic diameter >=30 mm or infrarenal-to-suprarenal diameter ratio >=1.5. Classified as small (<55 mm, surveillance) or large (>=55 mm, consider repair).
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — AAA is driven by chronic transmural inflammation: macrophage infiltration → MMP secretion → degradation of elastin and collagen → progressive wall weakening. Hemodynamics — as aortic diameter increases, wall tension rises (Laplace: T = P × r); larger aneurysms generate progressively greater wall stress with each heartbeat, accelerating expansion.*

AAA results from progressive degenerative changes in the aortic wall involving multiple interconnected mechanisms:

1. **ECM Degradation:** Proteolytic fragmentation of elastin (promotes dilatation) and collagen (promotes rupture) by matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9) and serine proteases (plasmin, leukocyte elastase)
2. **Intraluminal Thrombus (ILT):** Present in most AAAs; highly proteolytic and oxidative environment that recruits neutrophils, platelets, and RBCs; enzymes are outwardly convected through the wall, degrading ECM
3. **VSMC Loss:** Smooth muscle cell death from proteolytic detachment (plasmin, elastase) and oxidative stress (ceroids, ROS); thinning of the media
4. **Inflammation:** Innate immunity (neutrophil infiltration of ILT, mast cells in adventitia) and adaptive immunity (tertiary lymphoid organs in adventitia producing antibodies against neo-antigens)
5. **Oxidative Stress:** Sources include PMN leukocyte oxidases (NADPH, myeloperoxidase) and redox-active ferrous iron from trapped RBCs; smoking increases oxidative stress
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Intact AAA (most common presentation):**
- Usually **asymptomatic** -- most diagnosed incidentally on imaging or via screening
- Occasionally: pulsatile abdominal mass on palpation (large AAAs)
- Rarely: compression of adjacent organs

**Ruptured AAA (surgical emergency):**
- **Classic triad** (present in only 25-50% of cases):
  1. Sudden-onset abdominal or back pain
  2. Hypotension/hemorrhagic shock
  3. Pulsatile abdominal mass
- Severity of shock depends on location/extent of rupture (intraperitoneal vs retroperitoneal)
- Contained rupture: pain without hemodynamic instability (rare)

### Complications
- **Rupture** -- near 100% mortality if untreated
- Distal embolization from ILT
- Consumptive coagulopathy (decreased platelets, fibrinogen; increased D-dimers)
- Aortoenteric fistula (rare, associated with mycotic AAA)
- Consumptive anaemia from RBC trapping in ILT

**Annual rupture risk by diameter:**
| Diameter | Risk per 100 person-years |
|----------|--------------------------|
| <40 mm | Negligible |
| 40-54 mm | 0.7-1.0 |
| 55-70 mm | ~5.3 |
| >70 mm | ~6.3 |
conditions.management:
### Screening
- **Recommended:** One-time abdominal ultrasound for all men at age 65
- Participation rate 75-85% in UK/Sweden programs
- Associated with reduction in AAA-specific mortality (4.0%/year decline)
- No current evidence for population-based screening in women

### Surveillance Protocol
| Diameter | Men | Women |
|----------|-----|-------|
| 2.5-3.0 cm | Consider follow-up | Consider follow-up |
| 3.0-3.9 cm | Every 2-3 years | Every 2-3 years |
| 4.0-4.4 cm | Every 6-12 months | Every 6-12 months |
| 4.5-4.9 cm | Every 6 months | Every 3 months |
| 5.0-5.4 cm | Every 3-6 months | **Consider surgery** |
| >=5.5 cm | **Consider surgery** | **Consider surgery** |

*Consider earlier intervention for: rapid growth (>10 mm/year), saccular AAA, mycotic AAA, inflammatory AAA, family history of rupture*

### Acute (Ruptured AAA)
- **Surgical emergency** -- immediate repair required
- Options: EVAR or open repair surgery
- Hemodynamic stabilization, permissive hypotension
- EVAR shows lower 3-year mortality vs open repair for ruptured AAA (42% vs 54%)

### Elective Repair
**Open Repair Surgery:**
- Replacement of aneurysmal segment with synthetic vascular graft via laparotomy
- Infrarenal aortic cross-clamping (suprarenal if AAA extends above renals -- higher renal risk)
- Better long-term durability; reduced need for surveillance/reintervention
- 30-day mortality: ~2.8% men, ~5.4% women
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Pre-operative assessment:**
  - Baseline vital signs, peripheral pulses (bilateral pedal, femoral)
  - Cardiac and pulmonary risk assessment (high prevalence of comorbid CAD, COPD)
  - Renal function baseline (Cr, BUN) -- especially for contrast exposure and cross-clamping
  - Coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer)
- **Post-operative monitoring (EVAR and Open):**
  - Frequent neurovascular checks of lower extremities (pulses, sensation, color, temperature)
  - Hemodynamic monitoring -- watch for hypotension, tachycardia (hemorrhage)
  - Urine output (risk of renal ischemia, especially with suprarenal clamping)
  - Abdominal girth measurements
  - Monitor for signs of bowel ischemia (abdominal pain, bloody stool, lactic acidosis)
  - Incision site/groin access site assessment (hematoma, pseudoaneurysm)
  - Spinal cord ischemia assessment (0.21-0.9% incidence -- check lower extremity motor/sensory function)
- **Patient education:**
  - Smoking cessation (most critical modifiable risk factor)
  - Surveillance imaging schedule adherence (especially post-EVAR)
  - Signs of rupture: sudden severe abdominal/back pain, dizziness, syncope -- call 911 immediately

conditions.title: Acute Heart Failure
conditions.overview: Acute heart failure is the rapid onset or worsening of HF signs and symptoms requiring urgent evaluation and treatment. May be new-onset (de novo) or, more commonly, acute decompensation of chronic HF (ADHF). Presentations range from gradual worsening congestion to cardiogenic shock to flash pulmonary edema. It is the most common cause of hospitalization in adults > 65 and carries 30-day readmission rates of ~25% and 1-year mortality of ~20-30%.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — in acute decompensated HF, the Starling curve is flattened and shifted right; the failing ventricle requires excessive filling pressures (preload) to generate even reduced stroke volume, while further preload increase floods the pulmonary capillaries → edema*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — acute decompensation occurs when compensatory loops (baroreceptor reflex → tachycardia + RAAS → fluid retention → hypertrophy) are overwhelmed by a precipitant; the very compensatory responses (vasoconstriction, fluid retention) become maladaptive and worsen filling pressures*

Two primary hemodynamic profiles drive acute HF:

### Classification: "Warm/Cold" and "Wet/Dry" (Nohria-Stevenson)
| Profile | Congestion (Wet/Dry) | Perfusion (Warm/Cold) | Presentation | Treatment |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Warm and Dry** (A) | No | Yes | Compensated; not acute HF | Optimize oral GDMT |
| **Warm and Wet** (B) | Yes | Yes | MOST COMMON (~70%); congested but adequate perfusion | Diuresis + vasodilators |
| **Cold and Wet** (C) | Yes | No | Congested AND low output; pre-shock | Diuresis + inotropes ± vasopressors |
| **Cold and Dry** (L) | No | No | Low output without congestion; least common | Volume challenge (cautious) + inotropes |

### Flash Pulmonary Edema
- Sudden, severe pulmonary congestion, often triggered by acute HTN crisis, acute MR, or acute MI
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Congestion (most common presentation):**
- Dyspnea (rest or exertional), orthopnea, PND
- Crackles/rales (bilateral = cardiogenic; unilateral = consider pneumonia, PE)
- JVD (most specific bedside sign for elevated filling pressures)
- Peripheral edema (pitting), weight gain
- Hepatomegaly, ascites (right-sided/biventricular failure)
- S3 gallop

**Low output/Hypoperfusion:**
- Fatigue, altered mental status, confusion
- Cool extremities, mottled skin
- Weak pulses, narrow pulse pressure
- Hypotension, tachycardia
- Oliguria (< 0.5 mL/kg/hr)

### Complications
- Respiratory failure (requiring intubation)
- Cardiogenic Shock (see Vasopressors and Inotropes)
- Acute kidney injury (cardiorenal syndrome)
- Arrhythmias (VT/VF, AFib with RVR)
- Organ hypoperfusion → multi-organ dysfunction
- In-hospital mortality 4-10%; higher in cardiogenic shock (~40-50%)
conditions.management:
### Immediate (First 1-2 Hours)
1. **Assess hemodynamic profile:** Warm/wet vs. cold/wet vs. cold/dry
2. **Oxygen/Ventilation:** Supplemental O2 if SpO2 < 90%; BiPAP/CPAP for acute pulmonary edema (reduces preload, work of breathing, intubation rates); intubate if refractory
3. **Positioning:** Sit upright (reduces preload, improves lung expansion)
4. **IV access:** Large-bore; labs drawn simultaneously

### Pharmacologic Management

**Congested ("Wet") — Decongest:**
| Drug | Dose | Notes |
|------|------|-------|
| **IV furosemide** | If on home dose: give 1-2.5x daily dose as IV bolus; if diuretic-naive: 20-40 mg IV | First-line; titrate to urine output > 100-150 mL/hr initially; can give bolus or continuous infusion |
| **IV nitroglycerin** | 5-200 mcg/min infusion | Predominant venodilator (reduces preload); excellent for acute pulmonary edema with elevated BP; contraindicated if SBP < 90 |
| **IV nitroprusside** | 0.3-5 mcg/kg/min | Arterial + venous vasodilation; for severe HTN-driven acute HF; monitor for cyanide toxicity |
| **Nesiritide (BNP analog)** | 2 mcg/kg bolus → 0.01 mcg/kg/min | Vasodilation + mild diuresis; largely fallen out of favor (no outcome benefit in ASCEND-HF) |

**Hypoperfused ("Cold") — Improve Output:**
| Drug | Dose | Notes |
|------|------|-------|
| **Dobutamine** | 2-20 mcg/kg/min | Beta-1 agonist inotrope; increases contractility and CO; may cause tachycardia and arrhythmias |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - **Continuous telemetry and SpO2**
  - Hourly I&O during active diuresis; daily weights
  - Assess response to diuretics: Urine output target > 100-150 mL/hr initially (spot check at 2 hr post-dose; if < 100-150 mL → notify provider for dose escalation)
  - Lung sounds q2-4h; JVD; edema assessment
  - Hemodynamics: BP, HR, MAP; if PA catheter: PAWP, CI, SVR
  - Mental status (perfusion indicator)
  - Electrolytes q12-24h during active diuresis (K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cr)
  - Signs of over-diuresis: Hypotension, tachycardia, rising creatinine, thirst
- **Medication administration:**
  - IV nitroglycerin: Dedicated line; titrate q5-15 min to BP and symptoms; monitor for headache, hypotension; use non-PVC tubing (drug adsorbs to PVC)
  - IV furosemide: Give slowly (rapid push can cause ototoxicity at high doses); document time and urine output response
  - Inotropes/vasopressors: Central line preferred; continuous monitoring; assess for arrhythmias
  - BiPAP: Ensure proper mask fit; monitor respiratory status closely; have intubation supplies ready
- **Patient/Discharge teaching:**
pearls: **The #1 precipitant of acute HF decompensation is medication and dietary non-adherence.** Ask every patient: "Have you been taking all your medications? How much salt and fluid have you been consuming?" Address this before anything else.

**JVD is the most specific bedside sign for elevated filling pressures.** Crackles can be from pneumonia; edema can be from venous insufficiency. But JVD at 45 degrees in a dyspneic patient = elevated right-sided pressures = think HF.

**BiPAP before intubation in acute pulmonary edema.** Non-invasive ventilation reduces preload (positive pressure), improves oxygenation, reduces work of breathing, and decreases intubation rates. Start early while diuretics and vasodilators are working.
**"Warm and wet" is the most common profile (~70%).** These patients need diuresis and vasodilation, NOT inotropes. Giving dobutamine to a warm/wet patient adds risk without benefit.

**Urine output is your real-time diuretic effectiveness monitor.** Check at 2 hours post-IV furosemide. If < 100-150 mL → the dose wasn't enough → double it. Don't wait 6 hours to discover the patient didn't respond.

conditions.title: Acute Kidney Injury
conditions.overview:
Acute kidney injury is defined by a sudden loss of excretory kidney function, resulting in accumulation of nitrogenous waste products (urea, creatinine), dysregulation of extracellular volume, and disruption of electrolyte and acid-base homeostasis. AKI represents a spectrum from subclinical tubular injury detectable only by biomarkers through overt organ failure requiring kidney replacement therapy (KRT).

The KDIGO consensus definition unifies prior RIFLE and AKIN criteria into a single diagnostic framework, emphasizing that even small changes in serum creatinine are clinically significant and associated with increased morbidity and mortality.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — prerenal AKI = reduced renal perfusion pressure (MAP↓ or renal artery resistance↑); GFR falls when autoregulation limits are exceeded. Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — intrinsic AKI disrupts tubular function → electrolyte chaos (↑K+, ↑phosphate, ↓bicarb, volume overload). Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — once tubular epithelium is destroyed (ATN), all fluid/electrolyte loops lose their effector organ.*

AKI pathophysiology varies by etiology but can be organized into three categories:

### Pre-renal (functional, ~60% of cases)
1. Decreased renal perfusion from hypovolemia, decreased cardiac output, or systemic vasodilation
2. Reduced glomerular filtration pressure triggers compensatory afferent arteriole dilation and efferent constriction via RAAS activation
3. Prolonged hypoperfusion overwhelms autoregulation, leading to ischemic tubular injury
4. NSAIDs block prostaglandin-mediated afferent dilation; ACE Inhibitors/ARBs block efferent constriction — both can precipitate AKI in volume-depleted patients

### Intrinsic renal (~35%)
1. Ischemic or nephrotoxic injury causes tubular epithelial cell death (acute tubular necrosis)
2. Cellular debris obstructs tubular lumens, increasing intratubular pressure
3. Back-leak of filtrate through damaged epithelium reduces net filtration
4. Inflammatory cascade with neutrophil infiltration, cytokine release, and endothelial injury perpetuates damage
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Early/Subtle:**
- Decreased urine output (oliguria <400 mL/day or <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6 hours)
- Rising serum creatinine (may lag behind actual injury by 24-48 hours)
- Nausea, malaise, fatigue

**Volume Overload:**
- Peripheral edema, pulmonary edema, dyspnea
- Hypertension, jugular venous distension
- Weight gain

**Uremic Symptoms (advanced):**
- Anorexia, nausea, vomiting
- Altered mental status, asterixis, seizures
- Pericardial friction rub (uremic pericarditis)

**Electrolyte Disturbances:**
- Hyperkalemia: muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, peaked T-waves
- Metabolic acidosis: Kussmaul respirations
- Hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia

### Complications
- Hyperkalemia with life-threatening arrhythmias
- Pulmonary edema requiring emergent dialysis
- Uremic encephalopathy and seizures
- Uremic pericarditis and cardiac tamponade
- Bleeding diathesis from platelet dysfunction
- Infection (AKI is immunosuppressive)
- Progression to Chronic Kidney Disease
- Increased long-term cardiovascular morbidity and mortality
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Volume resuscitation:** Balanced crystalloids (Lactated Ringer's preferred over normal saline) for pre-renal AKI; avoid fluid overload in oliguric/anuric patients
- **Hemodynamic optimization:** Target MAP ≥65 mmHg; vasopressors if needed for septic Shock
- **Nephrotoxin avoidance:** Discontinue NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, IV contrast when possible; hold ACE Inhibitors/ARBs during acute illness
- **Hyperkalemia management:** IV calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization), insulin + dextrose, sodium bicarbonate, kayexalate, emergent dialysis if refractory
- **Relieve obstruction:** Foley catheter for bladder outlet obstruction; ureteral stents or nephrostomy for upper tract obstruction
- **Treat underlying cause:** Antibiotics for sepsis, immunosuppression for glomerulonephritis

### Kidney Replacement Therapy (KRT)
- **Absolute indications:** Refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, refractory volume overload, uremic symptoms (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding)
- **Modalities:** Intermittent hemodialysis (hemodynamically stable), continuous renal replacement therapy/CRRT (hemodynamically unstable ICU patients)
- **Timing:** No benefit to early initiation based on biomarkers alone; initiate for conventional clinical indications

### Chronic/Maintenance
- Monitor renal function for 3-12 months post-AKI (risk of incomplete recovery)
- Avoid nephrotoxic agents long-term
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Strict I&O monitoring** is the single most important nursing intervention. Measure urine output hourly via Foley in critically ill patients. Report output <0.5 mL/kg/h immediately — do not wait for the next assessment cycle.
- **Daily weights** at the same time, same scale, same clothing. A 1 kg weight gain equals approximately 1 liter of fluid retention.
- **Medication reconciliation** every shift: scan the MAR for nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, IV contrast). Be the safety net that catches the order for ketorolac in the AKI patient.
- **Potassium vigilance:** Check potassium results as soon as they post. K+ >5.5 mEq/L — get an ECG and notify provider. K+ >6.5 mEq/L — this is an emergency. Have IV calcium gluconate drawn up.
- **Fluid balance assessment:** Lung sounds (crackles = overload), JVD, peripheral edema, daily weights. Patients can swing from volume depletion to overload within hours.
- **CRRT management:** Monitor circuit pressures, access patency, replacement fluid rates. Ensure anticoagulation (citrate or heparin) is running correctly. Understand that drug dosing changes dramatically on CRRT — communicate with pharmacy.
pearls: **A "normal" creatinine does not rule out AKI** — a young muscular patient with baseline Cr 0.6 who rises to 0.9 has had a 50% increase in creatinine and meets KDIGO Stage 1 criteria. Always compare to baseline.

**Urine output is your earliest bedside biomarker** — creatinine lags 24-48 hours behind actual kidney injury. The nurse who catches oliguria early and intervenes with fluids or alerts the team can prevent progression to Stage 3.

**The combination of NSAIDs + ACE inhibitors + diuretics is the "triple whammy"** — this triad devastates renal autoregulation. When you see all three on the MAR of a dehydrated patient, that is your moment to intervene.
**Furosemide does not treat AKI** — it manages volume overload, but giving lasix to "kick-start the kidneys" has no evidence of benefit and may worsen injury by causing further volume depletion.
**AKI is not just an acute problem** — even a single episode increases lifetime risk of CKD by 8-fold and cardiovascular events by 40%. Ensure discharge planning includes nephrology follow-up.

conditions.title: Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
conditions.overview: A malignant clonal proliferation of lymphoid precursor cells (lymphoblasts) in the bone marrow, blood, and extramedullary sites. ALL is the most common childhood malignancy and represents a biologically heterogeneous group of diseases defined by recurrent genetic alterations. The WHO-HAEM5 (2022) classification is molecular-based, recognizing that specific genetic subtypes drive prognosis and treatment. B-cell precursor ALL (BCP-ALL) accounts for ~80% and T-cell ALL (T-ALL) for ~20% of cases.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — ALL is arrested lymphoblast maturation + uncontrolled proliferation; blast cells fill marrow, crowding out normal hematopoiesis. Genetics and Protein Expression — chromosomal translocations (Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22), ETV6-RUNX1) create fusion oncoproteins that drive proliferation and block differentiation.*

**Leukemogenesis:**
- Arises from malignant transformation of a lymphoid progenitor cell blocked at an early stage of differentiation
- Accumulation of genetic hits: initiating event (often prenatal — chromosomal translocation) + secondary cooperating mutations → uncontrolled proliferation of lymphoblasts
- Lymphoblasts accumulate in bone marrow → crowd out normal hematopoiesis → pancytopenia

**WHO-HAEM5 2022 molecular classification (key subtypes):**

**B-cell precursor ALL (BCP-ALL):**
- **High hyperdiploidy (>50 chromosomes):** ~25% of pediatric BCP-ALL; favorable prognosis; most common subtype in children
- **ETV6::RUNX1 [t(12;21)]:** ~25% of pediatric BCP-ALL; excellent prognosis; often cryptic (not seen on karyotype, needs FISH)
- **BCR::ABL1 [t(9;22)] — Philadelphia chromosome (Ph+):** ~3% pediatric, ~23% adult ALL; historically poor prognosis; now improved significantly with TKI addition
- **Ph-like ALL (BCR::ABL1-like):** Gene expression profile mimicking Ph+ ALL without BCR::ABL1; ~15% childhood, ~25% young adult; actionable kinase fusions (ABL-class, JAK-STAT) — may respond to TKIs or JAK inhibitors
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Bone marrow failure (most common presentation):**
- Anemia: fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, tachycardia
- Thrombocytopenia: petechiae, purpura, bruising, mucosal bleeding, epistaxis
- Neutropenia: fever, infections (may be first presentation — sepsis is an emergency)

**Leukemic infiltration:**
- Bone pain (25-40%, especially in children) — may present as limping child refusing to walk; misdiagnosed as growing pains or juvenile arthritis
- Hepatosplenomegaly (50-60%)
- Lymphadenopathy (50%)
- CNS involvement (5-8% at diagnosis): headache, cranial nerve palsies, meningismus, vomiting
- Testicular infiltration (2-5% in boys; painless testicular enlargement)
- Mediastinal mass (T-ALL — can cause SVC syndrome, respiratory compromise; oncologic emergency)

**Constitutional symptoms:**
- Fever (~60%), night sweats, weight loss, anorexia
- Fatigue out of proportion to anemia

### Complications
- **Tumor lysis syndrome** — especially with high WBC count at diagnosis; see Tumor Lysis Syndrome
- DIC (more common in T-ALL with high WBC)
- Leukostasis (WBC >100,000): respiratory distress, altered mental status, stroke — emergency leukapheresis
- CNS relapse (without adequate prophylaxis)
- Treatment toxicity: infection (leading cause of treatment mortality), mucositis, hepatotoxicity (methotrexate, asparaginase), pancreatitis (asparaginase), osteonecrosis (steroids, especially adolescents), thrombosis (asparaginase), cardiac toxicity (anthracyclines)
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Tumor lysis prevention/management:** Hydration (2-3x maintenance IV fluids), rasburicase (high risk: elevated uric acid, high tumor burden, high WBC) or allopurinol (intermediate risk); monitor K+, phosphate, calcium, uric acid, creatinine Q6-8h — see Tumor Lysis Syndrome and Fluid and Electrolyte Balance
- **Hyperleukocytosis/leukostasis (WBC >100,000):** Leukapheresis, urgent hydroxyurea + dexamethasone; avoid RBC transfusion before cytoreduction (increases viscosity)
- **Infection:** Blood cultures x2, broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately for febrile neutropenia; antifungal coverage if persistent fever
- **DIC management:** Blood Products and Transfusion — platelets, cryoprecipitate, FFP as needed; treat underlying leukemia
- **Mediastinal mass (T-ALL):** Emergent steroids; avoid sedation/general anesthesia (airway collapse risk); may need urgent radiation

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Pediatric ALL treatment (~2-3 years total):**

1. **Induction (4-6 weeks):** Goal is complete remission (CR) + MRD negativity
   - 4-drug induction: Vincristine + dexamethasone (or prednisone) + pegylated asparaginase + anthracycline (daunorubicin)
   - >95% of children achieve CR; ~85% of adults achieve CR
   - MRD assessment at end-induction (day 29): most important prognostic timepoint

2. **Consolidation/Intensification (several months):**
   - High-dose methotrexate, cytarabine, 6-mercaptopurine, cyclophosphamide — varies by protocol and risk group
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Tumor lysis syndrome is your Day 1 priority with newly diagnosed ALL:** Hydration should be running before chemotherapy starts; monitor potassium, phosphate, uric acid, calcium, and creatinine Q6-8h during induction; have rasburicase available; cardiac monitor for hyperkalemia — see Tumor Lysis Syndrome and Fluid and Electrolyte Balance
- **Febrile neutropenia kills more ALL patients than leukemia does during treatment:** Temperature ≥38.3C (101F) once or ≥38.0C (100.4F) sustained for 1 hour in a neutropenic patient = blood cultures x2 + broad-spectrum antibiotics WITHIN 60 MINUTES; do not wait for culture results; teach families to take temperatures and come to ED immediately
- **Vincristine is a vesicant — extravasation is a tissue emergency:** Always verify IV patency before and during infusion; never give through a peripheral line that has any question of infiltration; central line preferred for repeated dosing; if extravasation occurs, stop infusion immediately, aspirate residual, apply warm compresses, consider hyaluronidase injection; there is no effective antidote once tissue necrosis begins
pearls: **A child with unexplained bone pain, bruising, and fatigue does NOT have growing pains — get a CBC** — ALL is the most common childhood cancer and frequently masquerades as musculoskeletal complaints; a child who stops walking, has nighttime bone pain, or develops unexplained bruising needs a complete blood count before any other workup; the peripheral smear may show blasts

**Never transfuse a patient with hyperleukocytosis (WBC >100,000) with packed RBCs before cytoreduction** — transfusing RBCs increases blood viscosity and can precipitate fatal leukostasis with stroke, pulmonary failure, or tumor lysis; cytoreduction with hydroxyurea and steroids comes first; platelet transfusion is safe and often needed
