1. **Papillary/NMIBC pathway:** FGFR3 mutations (~60% of NMIBC), HRAS mutations, PI3K pathway activation → low-grade papillary tumors; lower risk of progression; FGFR3 is a therapeutic target (erdafitinib)
2. **CIS/MIBC pathway:** TP53 and RB1 inactivation, ERBB2 amplification, chromatin remodeling gene mutations (ARID1A, KDM6A) → flat CIS and high-grade invasive disease; higher mutation burden → more immunogenic → better response to immunotherapy

**Key genomic features:**
- **TERT promoter mutations:** Present in 70-80% of ALL bladder cancers (most frequent single mutation); enables telomere maintenance; potential biomarker for detection/surveillance
- **FGFR3 mutations/fusions:** ~60% NMIBC, ~15% MIBC; targetable with erdafitinib
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Painless gross hematuria (80-90%):** Most common presenting symptom; intermittent; any adult with painless gross hematuria needs cystoscopy — bladder cancer until proven otherwise
- **Microscopic hematuria:** Detected incidentally on urinalysis; requires workup per AUA guidelines (cystoscopy + upper tract imaging)
- **Irritative voiding symptoms:** Frequency, urgency, dysuria — particularly associated with CIS (mimics UTI but cultures are negative or symptoms persist despite treatment)
- **Recurrent UTIs:** Especially in older men, should prompt consideration of underlying malignancy
- **Advanced/metastatic symptoms:** Flank pain (ureteral obstruction), lower extremity edema (pelvic lymphadenopathy), bone pain, weight loss, anorexia

### Complications
- Ureteral obstruction → hydronephrosis → renal insufficiency
- Urinary retention (large tumors)
- Recurrence (NMIBC recurs in 50-70% within 5 years — lifelong surveillance needed)
- Progression from NMIBC to MIBC (10-30% depending on risk group)
- Metastatic disease (lung, liver, bone most common)
- Treatment complications: BCG sepsis, cystectomy morbidity (urinary diversion, sexual dysfunction, metabolic complications)
- Anemia from chronic hematuria
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Gross hematuria management:** Continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) with normal saline via 3-way catheter if active clotting; manual irrigation to evacuate clots; monitor hemoglobin; transfuse PRBCs if symptomatic anemia; urgent cystoscopy with fulguration if bleeding uncontrolled
- **Urinary obstruction:** Percutaneous nephrostomy or ureteral stent placement for obstructive uropathy
- **Surgical emergency:** Bladder perforation during TURBT (rare, <5%) — intra- vs extraperitoneal; intraperitoneal requires surgical repair; extraperitoneal managed with prolonged catheter drainage

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC):**

1. **TURBT (transurethral resection):** Complete resection of all visible tumor; essential first step for both diagnosis and treatment
2. **Intravesical therapy:**
   - **Single post-operative instillation:** Mitomycin C or gemcitabine within 6 hours of TURBT (reduces recurrence by 35%); for low/intermediate risk
   - **Intravesical BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guerin):** Standard of care for high-risk NMIBC; induction: 6 weekly instillations; maintenance: 3 weekly instillations at 3, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36 months (SWOG protocol); reduces both recurrence AND progression; BCG is a live attenuated tuberculosis vaccine — induces local immune response
   - **BCG-unresponsive disease:** Pembrolizumab (Keytruda), nadofaragene firadenovec (Adstiladrin — gene therapy), intravesical gemcitabine + docetaxel, or radical cystectomy
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Painless hematuria in any adult is bladder cancer until proven otherwise — never dismiss it:** Especially in smokers or anyone >50; a single episode of gross painless hematuria warrants full workup (cystoscopy + CT urogram); do not attribute to UTI without cultures; if cultures are negative and symptoms persist, push for urology referral
- **Intravesical BCG administration requires strict safety protocols:** BCG is a LIVE organism; use PPE (gloves, mask); patient voids BCG into toilet with household bleach (undiluted, sit for 15 min before flushing) for 6 hours after instillation; monitor for systemic BCG infection (fever >101.3F lasting >48h, persistent malaise — notify urology immediately); hold BCG for active UTI or traumatic catheterization (risk of systemic absorption)
- **Post-TURBT nursing care centers on hemorrhage monitoring and catheter management:**
  - Monitor CBI flow: maintain pink-to-clear output; increase rate if dark red or clots present; decrease when clearing
  - Watch for catheter obstruction from clots — bladder distension, patient complaining of suprapubic pain/urgency; may need manual irrigation with 60mL syringe
pearls: **A patient with recurrent "UTIs" that don't fully respond to antibiotics, especially an older man, needs a cystoscopy** — CIS (carcinoma in situ) causes irritative voiding symptoms that perfectly mimic a urinary tract infection; negative cultures with persistent dysuria and frequency should raise the alarm; CIS is flat and invisible on imaging — only cystoscopy finds it

**If the TURBT pathology report does not mention muscularis propria (detrusor muscle) in the specimen, the staging is incomplete** — you cannot distinguish T1 (lamina propria invasion, manageable with BCG) from T2 (muscle invasion, needs cystectomy) without muscle in the specimen; re-TURBT is mandatory; this is a common and consequential oversight

**BCG is the only cancer immunotherapy that's been around for almost 50 years and still works** — intravesical BCG for bladder cancer predates modern checkpoint inhibitors by decades; it works by recruiting T-cells to the bladder wall; but it's a live organism — if a patient spikes a fever >101.3F lasting more than 48 hours after instillation, think BCG sepsis and call urology immediately; this is rare but can be fatal without anti-tuberculosis treatment

**Radical cystectomy patients with ileal conduits can develop a metabolic acidosis that looks like nothing else** — the intestinal segment reabsorbs chloride and secretes bicarbonate into urine, creating a hyperchloremic non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis; this is expected and usually mild but can worsen with dehydration or renal impairment; know why the bicarb is low before reaching for interventions

**The male-to-female incidence ratio of 4:1 masks a diagnostic disparity** — women with bladder cancer are more likely to have their hematuria attributed to menstrual or gynecological causes and face diagnostic delays of months to years; women consequently present at later stages with worse outcomes; advocate for any woman with unexplained hematuria to get the same workup as a man

conditions.title: Brain Metastases
conditions.overview: Malignant tumors that have metastasized to the brain parenchyma from a primary cancer elsewhere in the body via hematogenous spread. Brain metastases are the most common intracranial tumors in adults, outnumbering primary brain tumors by approximately 10:1. They represent advanced (stage IV) disease but increasingly have treatment options that can provide meaningful survival and quality-of-life benefit.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — metastasis requires epithelial-mesenchymal transition, intravasation, hematogenous/lymphatic transit, extravasation, and colonization of a new microenvironment; brain has unique immune privilege facilitating metastatic survival.*

1. Cancer cells detach from primary tumor and enter systemic circulation
2. Survive in bloodstream and arrest in cerebral microcirculation
3. Extravasate through blood-brain barrier at the **gray-white matter junction** (where vessel caliber changes sharply — mechanical trapping)
4. Establish microenvironment: angiogenesis, immune evasion, interaction with brain-specific stroma (astrocytes, microglia)
5. Grow as well-circumscribed masses with surrounding vasogenic edema (peritumoral edema often more symptomatic than the tumor itself)

**Distribution reflects blood flow:** 80% cerebral hemispheres, 15% cerebellum, 5% brainstem

**The "seed and soil" hypothesis:** Certain tumors have tropism for brain tissue (melanoma, lung — molecular interactions favor brain colonization). Some primary cancers rarely metastasize to the brain (prostate, sarcoma).

**Blood-brain barrier (BBB) considerations:** Brain mets disrupt the BBB locally but the intact BBB limits penetration of many systemic therapies → historically a "sanctuary site" for cancer; newer targeted therapies and immunotherapies are improving CNS penetration.
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Headache (40-50%):** Progressive, often worse in morning, may be positional; can be focal or generalized; may be sudden if hemorrhagic metastasis (melanoma, renal, choriocarcinoma — highly vascular tumors bleed)
- **Focal neurological deficits (40%):** Hemiparesis, sensory loss, visual field deficits, aphasia — depends on metastasis location
- **Seizures (15-25%):** More common with cortical metastases; may be presenting symptom
- **Cognitive/behavioral changes:** Memory impairment, personality changes, confusion; especially with multiple or frontal metastases
- **Signs of raised ICP:** Headache, nausea/vomiting, papilledema, altered consciousness
- **Cerebellar metastases:** Ataxia, vertigo, nystagmus, hydrocephalus (posterior fossa mass obstructing 4th ventricle — surgical emergency)
- **Hemorrhagic presentation:** Acute severe headache mimicking stroke; classic for melanoma, RCC, choriocarcinoma, thyroid cancer

### Complications
- Brain herniation from mass effect or edema
- Hemorrhage (especially melanoma, RCC metastases)
- Leptomeningeal carcinomatosis (diffuse meningeal spread — very poor prognosis)
- Hydrocephalus (posterior fossa metastases)
- Radiation necrosis (late complication of treatment)
- Cognitive decline from disease and/or treatment (especially WBRT)
- Status epilepticus
- Venous thromboembolism
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Vasogenic edema:** Dexamethasone 4-16 mg/day (10 mg IV load for severe symptoms); dramatic symptom improvement within 24-48 hours
- **Seizures:** Antiseizure medication if seizures occur; levetiracetam first-line; prophylactic AEDs NOT recommended without prior seizure
- **Raised ICP/herniation:** Mannitol or hypertonic saline, HOB elevation, dexamethasone; emergency surgical decompression for posterior fossa mass with hydrocephalus
- **Hemorrhagic metastasis:** Manage as hemorrhagic stroke + treat underlying lesion

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Treatment selection depends on:** Number of metastases, size, location, primary tumor type, molecular markers, extracranial disease status, performance status, and patient goals.

**Surgery:**
- Indicated for: Solitary accessible metastasis, tissue diagnosis needed, large symptomatic lesion causing mass effect, radiation-resistant histology
- Followed by cavity SRS or focal radiation to reduce local recurrence

**Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS):**
- High-dose, focused radiation in 1-5 fractions (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife, linear accelerator-based)
- Indicated for: 1-4 metastases (increasingly used for more); small-moderate size (<3-4 cm); combined with surgery for larger lesions
- **Preferred over WBRT for limited metastases** — better neurocognitive preservation
- ~80-90% local control rates

**Whole brain radiation therapy (WBRT):**
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Neurological assessment frequency:** Q2-4h in acute setting; baseline and serial assessments; document GCS, pupil reactivity, motor/sensory function, speech, orientation; report ANY new deficit immediately
- **Steroid management:**
  - Monitor blood glucose (check Q6h initially — dexamethasone causes significant hyperglycemia; sliding scale insulin often needed)
  - GI prophylaxis with PPI
  - Monitor for steroid psychosis (insomnia, agitation, mood swings — common with high doses)
  - Document taper schedule clearly; educate patient NEVER to stop abruptly
  - Long-term: monitor for proximal myopathy (difficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs), cushingoid features, infection risk
- **Seizure precautions:** Pad side rails, suction at bedside, O2 ready; medication adherence teaching; educate family on seizure first aid
- **Fall prevention:** Focal deficits, steroid myopathy, and seizure risk all increase fall risk; assess each shift; appropriate assistive devices
- **Post-SRS/surgery care:** Neuro checks, wound assessment, monitor for radiation necrosis symptoms (may mimic tumor progression weeks-months later)
- **Emotional support and goals of care:**
pearls: **Any cancer patient with a new headache or neurological symptom needs an MRI brain** — don't wait for "classic" signs of raised ICP; even a single new seizure or subtle personality change warrants imaging

**Hemorrhagic brain metastases mimic stroke** — melanoma, renal cell, and choriocarcinoma mets bleed; when an older patient presents with intracerebral hemorrhage and no clear hypertension or vascular malformation, think metastasis; CT the chest

**Steroid taper is an art, not a science** — patients feel great on dexamethasone, then awful during taper; go slowly (reduce by 2-4 mg every 3-5 days), watch for adrenal insufficiency with prolonged courses, and resist the urge to keep patients on high doses long-term

**One brain met in a patient with otherwise controlled cancer changes everything** — a single, resectable brain metastasis may be treated aggressively (surgery + SRS) with curative intent in selected patients; multidisciplinary tumor board input is essential

**The question is no longer IF systemic therapy works in the brain, but WHICH one** — osimertinib, lorlatinib, tucatinib, ipilimumab/nivolumab all have proven intracranial activity; always check molecular markers and consider systemic therapy alongside or instead of radiation

conditions.title: Bronchiectasis
conditions.overview: Chronic, irreversible dilation of the bronchi due to repeated cycles of airway infection, inflammation, and structural damage. Characterized by impaired mucociliary clearance leading to mucus retention, chronic productive cough, and recurrent respiratory infections.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — recurrent/chronic airway infections trigger persistent neutrophilic inflammation → proteases (elastase, MMP) destroy bronchial wall → irreversible dilation; a vicious cycle of infection → inflammation → structural damage → more infection. Oxygenation and Ventilation — dilated mucus-filled airways create V/Q mismatch and shunt; hypoxemia is proportional to extent of disease.*

The "vicious cycle" hypothesis drives disease progression:
1. Initial insult damages airway epithelium (infection, aspiration, immune defect)
2. Impaired mucociliary clearance → mucus retention
3. Chronic bacterial colonization (especially *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, *Haemophilus influenzae*)
4. Persistent neutrophilic inflammation → protease release → further airway wall destruction
5. Progressive bronchial dilation and architectural distortion → more mucus trapping

Airway remodeling leads to permanent structural changes including smooth muscle hypertrophy, cartilage destruction, and neovascularization (which causes hemoptysis).
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Cardinal symptom:** Chronic productive cough with large volumes of purulent sputum (often "cupfuls")
- Recurrent respiratory infections (≥3/year suggests bronchiectasis)
- Hemoptysis (mild to massive — from hypertrophied bronchial arteries)
- Dyspnea on exertion (progressive)
- Pleuritic chest pain
- Coarse crackles and wheezing on auscultation
- Digital clubbing (in advanced disease)
- Rhinosinusitis (common comorbidity)

### Complications
- Massive hemoptysis (life-threatening — may need bronchial artery embolization)
- Recurrent pneumonia and progressive lung damage
- Chronic *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* colonization (associated with worse outcomes)
- Respiratory failure
- Pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale (late)
- Nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) infection
conditions.management:
### Acute (Exacerbation)
- Sputum culture BEFORE starting antibiotics
- Empiric antibiotics based on prior culture data (14-day course typical)
  - Non-Pseudomonal: amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline
  - Pseudomonal: oral ciprofloxacin or IV piperacillin-tazobactam / cefepime / meropenem
- Increase airway clearance frequency
- Bronchodilators if bronchospasm present
- Consider short course of Systemic Corticosteroids if significant wheezing/airflow obstruction

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Airway clearance is the cornerstone:** chest physiotherapy, oscillating PEP devices (Acapella, Flutter), postural drainage — at least twice daily
- Pulmonary rehabilitation
- Mucolytics: hypertonic saline (7%) nebulization to thin secretions
- Macrolide therapy (azithromycin 250mg 3x/week) for patients with ≥3 exacerbations/year — reduces exacerbation frequency by ~50%
- Inhaled antibiotics (tobramycin, colistin) for chronic *Pseudomonas* colonization
- Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination
- Surgical resection for localized disease refractory to medical therapy

### Pharmacology
- Antibiotics Overview — culture-directed for exacerbations; macrolides for prevention
- Bronchodilators — albuterol PRN for bronchospasm
- Inhaled Corticosteroids — only if concurrent asthma/COPD overlap
- Hypertonic saline nebulization — mucolytic effect
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Airway clearance education is essential** — teach proper use of PEP devices and postural drainage positions; adherence determines outcomes
- Monitor sputum volume, color, and consistency — changes signal exacerbation
- Collect quality sputum specimens (deep cough, early morning, before antibiotics)
- Assess for hemoptysis — quantify amount; massive hemoptysis (>300 mL/24h) is an emergency
- Monitor for medication side effects: macrolide GI effects, hearing changes, QTc prolongation; aminoglycoside nephro/ototoxicity
- Nutritional support — chronic infection increases caloric demands; monitor weight trends
- Psychosocial support — chronic productive cough causes social embarrassment and isolation
- Smoking cessation if applicable
- Teach recognition of exacerbation signs: increased sputum volume/purulence, worsening dyspnea, fever
pearls: **"Signet ring sign" on CT is your diagnostic anchor** — when the bronchus is larger than its accompanying pulmonary artery, bronchiectasis is confirmed
**Never skip the sputum culture** — Pseudomonas changes everything about the treatment plan; always culture before starting empiric antibiotics
**Airway clearance is the treatment, not a nice-to-have** — patients who commit to twice-daily chest physiotherapy have measurably fewer exacerbations than those who rely on medications alone
**Think bronchiectasis when the "frequent flyer" keeps coming back with pneumonia** — recurrent lower respiratory infections in the same lobe warrant an HRCT
**Hemoptysis can be sudden and massive** — hypertrophied bronchial arteries can bleed catastrophically; have a plan for bronchial artery embolization access

conditions.title: Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolism
conditions.overview: Cancer-associated venous thromboembolism (VTE) encompasses deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE) occurring in the setting of active malignancy. Cancer creates a hypercoagulable state through multiple mechanisms, making VTE one of the most common and serious complications of malignancy. VTE is the second leading cause of death in cancer patients (after the cancer itself) and affects ~4-20% of cancer patients during their disease course. The relationship is bidirectional: cancer causes VTE, and unprovoked VTE may be the first sign of occult malignancy.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — cancer cells express tissue factor, cancer procoagulants, and PAI-1; tumor-derived microparticles activate the coagulation cascade, creating a hypercoagulable state (Virchow's triad: hypercoagulability + stasis + endothelial injury).*

Cancer promotes thrombosis through ALL elements of Virchow's Triad:

1. **Hypercoagulability (most important):**
   - Tumor cells produce **tissue factor (TF)** → directly activates extrinsic coagulation cascade → thrombin generation
   - Cancer procoagulant (CP) → directly activates Factor X
   - Tumor-derived extracellular vesicles with TF circulate systemically → widespread thrombotic potential; polyphosphate-positive vesicles activate Factor XII and platelets
   - **Podoplanin (PDPN):** Tumor-expressed protein released on extracellular vesicles → directly activates platelets (particularly relevant in brain tumors)
   - Mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, GI) produce mucins that activate platelets and selectins → particularly high VTE risk
   - Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) formation promoted by tumor → additional prothrombotic mechanism
   - **PAI-1** (plasminogen activator inhibitor 1) released by tumors → inhibits fibrinolysis
   - Inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) from tumor and host → activate endothelium → pro-thrombotic surface
   - Decreased natural anticoagulants (protein C, protein S, antithrombin) in cancer

2. **Stasis:**
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Same as non-cancer VTE:**
  - DVT: Unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, erythema, Homan's sign (unreliable)
  - PE: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, tachypnea, hemoptysis, syncope, hypotension (massive PE)
  - See Pulmonary Embolism for detailed PE presentation
- **Cancer-specific presentations:**
  - **Unusual site thrombosis:** Upper extremity DVT (from central lines), portal/hepatic vein thrombosis, mesenteric vein thrombosis, cerebral sinus thrombosis — should prompt cancer workup if unprovoked
  - **Recurrent VTE despite anticoagulation** — hallmark of cancer-associated hypercoagulability
  - **Migratory thrombophlebitis** (Trousseau) — superficial veins; shifts locations
  - **Incidental PE** — found on staging CT scans in ~3-5% of cancer patients; clinically significant and requires treatment
  - **Catheter-related thrombosis** — upper extremity DVT at PICC or port site

### Complications
- Death (VTE is #2 cause of cancer death)
- Recurrent VTE (higher recurrence rate than non-cancer VTE even on anticoagulation)
- Post-thrombotic syndrome (chronic leg swelling, skin changes, ulceration)
- Anticoagulation-related bleeding (cancer patients bleed MORE AND clot MORE simultaneously — major management challenge)
- Treatment delays (VTE may delay/interrupt chemotherapy)
- Reduced quality of life
- Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
conditions.management:
### Anticoagulation — Cancer-Specific Considerations

**Initial Treatment (First 5-10 days):**
| Agent | Details |
|-------|---------|
| LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID) | Traditional first-line for cancer VTE; reliable, injectable, no food interactions |
| Apixaban 10 mg BID × 7 days | Can be used as initial monotherapy (no heparin bridge needed); ADAM-VTE trial |
| Rivaroxaban 15 mg BID × 21 days | Can be used as initial monotherapy; SELECT-D, CALLISTO trials |
| UFH (if CrCl < 30 or need for procedural reversal) | aPTT-guided; more predictable offset for peri-procedural management |

**Long-term Treatment (3-6+ months):**
| Agent | Evidence | Key Considerations |
|-------|---------|-------------------|
| **LMWH (enoxaparin, dalteparin)** | CLOT trial (dalteparin); traditional standard of care; 50% VTE reduction vs. warfarin | Daily injections (adherence challenge); dose-adjustable; safe with most chemo |
| **Apixaban 5 mg BID** | Caravaggio trial: Non-inferior to dalteparin; NO increase in GI bleeding | Preferred DOAC for cancer VTE; avoids GI bleeding concern |
| **Edoxaban 60 mg daily** | Hokusai-VTE Cancer: Non-inferior to dalteparin; INCREASED GI bleeding in GI/GU cancers | Effective but AVOID in upper GI malignancies |
| **Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily** | SELECT-D: Non-inferior; increased GI bleeding in GI cancers | Same GI bleeding concern as edoxaban |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - VTE screening at every visit/admission: Leg circumference, swelling, pain, Homan's sign (low sensitivity but still assessed)
  - Respiratory: New dyspnea, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain → think PE
  - Catheter sites: Swelling, pain, difficulty flushing PICC/port (catheter-related thrombosis)
  - Bleeding assessment (concurrent risk): Stool guaiac, urine dipstick, gum bleeding, bruising, hemoptysis
  - Platelet count trending: Chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia affects anticoagulation dosing
  - Renal function: Affects LMWH clearance and DOAC dosing
- **Medication administration:**
  - LMWH: SubQ injection technique; rotate injection sites (abdomen preferred); do NOT expel air bubble from prefilled syringe; do NOT rub site after injection
  - DOACs: Rivaroxaban MUST be taken with food (bioavailability drops ~40% without); apixaban can be taken with or without food; educate on missed dose protocols
  - Monitor for drug interactions with chemotherapy (CYP3A4/P-gp interactions with DOACs)
- **Patient teaching:**
  - Signs of DVT/PE: When to go to ED immediately (sudden dyspnea, chest pain, new severe leg swelling)
pearls: **Cancer patients simultaneously have the highest clotting AND highest bleeding risk of any patient population.** This makes every anticoagulation decision a careful balance. There is no risk-free option — both treating and not treating VTE carry significant danger.

**Apixaban is emerging as the preferred DOAC for cancer-associated VTE.** The Caravaggio trial showed it was non-inferior to dalteparin WITHOUT the increased GI bleeding seen with rivaroxaban and edoxaban. For patients without GI/GU malignancy, apixaban offers the convenience of oral dosing with excellent safety.

**AVOID DOACs in active upper GI or GU cancers.** Rivaroxaban and edoxaban showed significantly higher GI bleeding rates in patients with luminal GI cancers (SELECT-D, Hokusai-VTE Cancer trials). Use LMWH for these patients. Apixaban appears safer but caution is still warranted.

**Warfarin is the WORST choice for cancer VTE.** The CLOT trial definitively showed LMWH was superior. Warfarin INR is nearly impossible to manage with chemotherapy (N/V, drug interactions, liver dysfunction, variable vitamin K intake). It should only be used when LMWH and DOACs are truly not options.

**An unprovoked VTE may be the first sign of cancer.** About 5-10% of patients with unprovoked VTE will be diagnosed with cancer within 1 year. Age-appropriate cancer screening is recommended. The cancers most commonly found: lung, GI, pancreatic, GU, hematologic.

conditions.title: Cardiac Arrest
conditions.overview:
Cardiac arrest is the abrupt cessation of effective cardiac mechanical activity, resulting in the absence of a palpable pulse and loss of consciousness. Without immediate intervention — high-quality CPR and defibrillation when indicated — death follows within minutes.

Cardiac arrest is NOT a diagnosis. It is the **final common pathway** of many diseases. The arrest rhythm tells you WHAT the heart is doing; the H's and T's help you figure out WHY.

**Related notes:** Code Blue Management · Electricity and Excitability · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Antiarrhythmics · Vasopressors and Inotropes

> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

---
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### During the Code
- See Code Blue Management for detailed team roles and workflow
- **Focus on compression quality** — this is the ONE thing that saves lives
- **Minimize interruptions** — pre-charge the defibrillator, have pads in place, keep pauses <10 sec
- **Track time** — announce 2-minute cycles, drug timing, total code duration
- **Push epi early for PEA/asystole** — don't wait for a rhythm check cycle
- **Think H's and T's** — if the rhythm isn't converting, the team needs to broaden the differential
- **Communicate clearly** — closed-loop communication for every order and intervention

### Post-ROSC
- **Continuous monitoring** — arterial line if available (most accurate BP), waveform capnography, continuous SpO2
- **Re-arrest readiness** — keep pads on, defibrillator at bedside, drugs drawn up. 30-50% of ROSC patients re-arrest
- **Temperature monitoring** — esophageal or bladder probe. Avoid fever aggressively even if not doing active cooling
- **Neuroprognostication** — DO NOT prognosticate within 72 hours post-arrest (minimum). Brain recovery takes time. Premature withdrawal of care based on early neuro exam is a leading cause of preventable death post-arrest
pearls: **Push hard, push fast, don't stop.** Compression quality is the single biggest determinant of survival. Everything else — drugs, intubation, IV access — is secondary. If you're not compressing, the patient is dying.

**ETCO2 is your CPR report card.** Waveform capnography during CPR tells you if compressions are generating cardiac output (ETCO2 >10). A sudden spike to >40 mmHg = check for ROSC. This is more reliable than pulse checks.

**PEA is not a rhythm — it's a symptom.** PEA means "something is preventing the heart from generating output despite electrical activity." Your job is to figure out WHAT. Run through H's and T's every cycle.
**VF doesn't stay VF forever.** Coarse VF degenerates to fine VF, then to asystole over minutes. Early defibrillation catches VF when it's most treatable. This is why public-access AEDs save lives.

**Epi is for coronary perfusion, not for "restarting the heart."** During CPR, epinephrine's alpha-1 effect constricts peripheral vessels → redirects blood to the coronary arteries → improves the chance that defibrillation will restore a perfusing rhythm. It doesn't restart anything.

conditions.title: Cardiac Tamponade
conditions.overview: Cardiac tamponade is a life-threatening condition in which fluid accumulation in the pericardial space compresses the heart, impairing diastolic filling and reducing cardiac output. The pericardium normally contains 15-50 mL of fluid. Tamponade physiology depends not just on volume but on the RATE of accumulation — rapid accumulation of even 100-200 mL can cause tamponade, while slow accumulation (malignancy) can tolerate 1-2 L before becoming hemodynamically significant.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — pericardial pressure exceeds ventricular filling pressure → preload falls → SV falls → CO falls; this is obstructive shock from mechanical restriction. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) reflects compensatory mechanisms: tachycardia and venoconstriction maintain BP temporarily until decompensation. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — pulsus paradoxus is the hemodynamic signature of tamponade: exaggerated drop in SBP with inspiration as RV fills at LV's expense in the constrained pericardial space.*

1. **Pericardial fluid accumulation** → increased intrapericardial pressure
2. **When intrapericardial pressure exceeds intracardiac filling pressures** → compression of cardiac chambers (right-sided chambers compress first due to lower pressures)
3. **Impaired diastolic filling** → decreased ventricular preload → decreased stroke volume → decreased cardiac output
4. **Compensatory mechanisms:** Tachycardia (to maintain CO), peripheral vasoconstriction (to maintain BP), increased venous pressure (to overcome pericardial pressure)
5. **Decompensation:** When compensatory mechanisms are exhausted → profound hypotension → cardiogenic shock → PEA arrest → death
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms — Beck's Triad (Classic but NOT Always Present)
1. **Hypotension** (low cardiac output)
2. **Jugular venous distension (JVD)** (impaired venous return to compressed heart)
3. **Muffled/distant heart sounds** (fluid insulates sounds)

> Beck's triad is present in < 50% of cases. Don't wait for all three.

**Other findings:**
- Tachycardia (compensatory — most reliable early sign)
- **Pulsus paradoxus** > 10 mmHg (SBP drops > 10 mmHg during inspiration); measure with manual BP cuff or arterial line
- Dyspnea, tachypnea
- Anxiety, restlessness
- Cool, clammy extremities
- Narrowed pulse pressure
- Kussmaul sign (paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration) — more specific for constrictive pericarditis but can occur
- **Electrical alternans on ECG** (alternating QRS amplitude — swinging heart in fluid)
- PEA arrest (tamponade is a reversible cause of PEA — always consider in cardiac arrest)

### Complications
- Cardiogenic Shock
- PEA cardiac arrest
- Death (rapidly fatal if untreated)
- Recurrence (especially malignant effusions)
conditions.management:
### Acute — This Is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY

**Temporizing Measures (while preparing for drainage):**
- IV fluid bolus (500-1000 mL NS) — increases preload to overcome pericardial pressure; buys time
- Avoid vasodilators (nitroglycerin), diuretics, and positive pressure ventilation (all reduce preload → worsens tamponade)
- Keep patient upright (reduces pericardial pressure on heart)
- Prepare for emergent pericardiocentesis

**Definitive Treatment:**
| Procedure | Indication | Details |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Pericardiocentesis** (needle drainage) | First-line in medical tamponade | Subxiphoid approach under echo guidance (or fluoroscopy); aspirate fluid; send for analysis; can leave pigtail catheter for continued drainage |
| **Surgical pericardial window** | Recurrent effusions (malignancy), traumatic tamponade, loculated effusions | Creates permanent drainage pathway into pleural space or peritoneum |
| **Emergent thoracotomy** | Traumatic tamponade (penetrating injury) with cardiac arrest or hemodynamic collapse | ED thoracotomy if penetrating trauma + loss of vital signs within 10-15 min |

### Post-Drainage
- Monitor for reaccumulation (serial echo)
- Treat underlying cause (malignancy → oncology, chemo, radiation; infection → antibiotics; uremia → dialysis; autoimmune → immunosuppression)
- If malignant: Consider pericardial window, intrapericardial chemotherapy, or sclerotherapy for recurrent effusions
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - Continuous telemetry and hemodynamic monitoring
  - Recognize Beck's triad: Hypotension + JVD + muffled heart sounds
  - Check pulsus paradoxus: Inflate BP cuff above systolic → slowly deflate → note pressure where sounds first heard only during expiration → continue deflating until heard throughout → difference > 10 mmHg = positive pulsus paradoxus
  - Monitor for PEA arrest — tamponade is on the "H's and T's" reversible causes list
  - Trending vitals: Worsening tachycardia + narrowing pulse pressure + rising JVP = deteriorating
- **Pericardiocentesis assistance:**
  - Emergency equipment at bedside (crash cart, intubation supplies)
  - Position patient at 30-45 degrees (pools fluid inferiorly near needle insertion)
  - Continuous ECG monitoring during procedure (ST elevation on ECG = needle touching myocardium → withdraw slightly)
  - Monitor for complications: dysrhythmias, pneumothorax, myocardial laceration, coronary artery injury
  - Post-procedure: Monitor output from pigtail catheter; report sudden cessation (clot) or bloody drainage; serial vitals and echo
- **Critical teaching points:**
pearls: **Tamponade is a CLINICAL diagnosis, not an echocardiographic one.** A large pericardial effusion without hemodynamic compromise is not tamponade. A small effusion in a hypotensive tachycardic patient IS tamponade. Treat the patient, not the ultrasound.
**Pulsus paradoxus is the most sensitive bedside finding** (~80% sensitivity). If you suspect tamponade, check pulsus paradoxus before anything else. > 10 mmHg drop = significant; > 20 mmHg = severe.

**"Give fluids, NOT diuretics" is counterintuitive but critical.** The JVD and edema make it look like volume overload. But the problem is external compression. IV fluids increase intracardiac pressures enough to overcome pericardial pressure and maintain filling. Diuretics in tamponade can be fatal.

**Positive pressure ventilation (including BiPAP) worsens tamponade** by reducing venous return. Avoid intubation if possible. If the patient must be intubated, be prepared for hemodynamic collapse at induction — have pericardiocentesis ready.

**Tamponade is a reversible cause of PEA arrest.** During cardiac arrest, if the rhythm is PEA → perform bedside echo → if pericardial effusion present → emergent pericardiocentesis may restore circulation. This is lifesaving.

conditions.title: Cardiogenic Shock
conditions.overview:
Cardiogenic shock is a state of critical end-organ hypoperfusion caused by **primary cardiac pump failure** — the heart cannot generate sufficient cardiac output to meet tissue metabolic demands. It is the most lethal form of shock, with mortality of 40-50% even with modern treatment.

The DO2 problem: CO is critically low because the heart can't squeeze (systolic failure), can't fill (diastolic failure/mechanical obstruction), or has a catastrophic structural problem (acute valvular rupture, VSD).

**Related notes:** Shock · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops · Vasopressors and Inotropes · Cardiac Arrest

---
conditions.pathophysiology:
Through the Oxygen Delivery and Consumption lens:

1. **Primary insult** → myocardial damage → ↓ contractility → ↓ stroke volume → ↓ cardiac output
2. **↓ CO → ↓ DO2** → tissue hypoperfusion → rising lactate → organ dysfunction
> *First principles: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — cardiogenic shock breaks Lever 1 (cardiac output); when CO collapses, DO2 drops regardless of Hgb or SaO2. Treatment restores CO via inotropes and MAP via vasopressors — always rebuilding DO2 to meet VO2.*

3. **Compensatory response** (see Homeostasis and Feedback Loops):
   - ↑ HR (sympathetic activation) — but ↑ HR → ↑ myocardial O2 demand on an already failing heart
   - ↑ SVR (vasoconstriction) — but ↑ afterload → harder for the failing ventricle to eject
   - ↑ Preload (RAAS, ADH) — but ↑ preload → pulmonary congestion → worsened oxygenation
4. **Vicious cycle:** Every compensatory mechanism worsens the underlying problem → **downward spiral** → death without intervention

> **This is the central tragedy of cardiogenic shock:** the body's normal compensatory responses to low CO (tachycardia, vasoconstriction, fluid retention) are EXACTLY the wrong things for a failing heart. Treatment is aimed at breaking these maladaptive compensations.

### Hemodynamic Profile

| Parameter | Expected Finding | Why |
|-----------|-----------------|-----|
| **CO/CI** | ↓↓ (CI <2.2 L/min/m²) | Pump failure — the defining feature |
| **SVR** | ↑↑ | Compensatory vasoconstriction (sympathetic) |
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Presentation
- **Hypotension:** SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg (or >30 mmHg drop from baseline for >30 min)
- **Tachycardia:** Compensatory (but may be bradycardic if AV nodal disease or inferior MI)
- **Cool, clammy, mottled skin** — vasoconstriction redirecting blood to vital organs
- **Altered mental status** — confusion, agitation, obtundation (cerebral hypoperfusion)
- **Oliguria** — urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr (renal hypoperfusion)
- **Pulmonary edema** — dyspnea, crackles, pink frothy sputum (LV failure → pulmonary congestion)
- **JVD** — elevated right-sided pressures
- **Diaphoresis** — sympathetic activation
- **Narrow pulse pressure** — ↓ SV → ↓ systolic BP with maintained diastolic (vasoconstriction)

### Key Distinction from Other Shock Types

| Finding | Cardiogenic | Hypovolemic | Distributive |
|---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| **JVD** | Present | Absent | Variable |
| **Lungs** | Wet (crackles, edema) | Clear | Usually clear |
| **Skin** | Cold, clammy | Cold, clammy | Warm, flushed (early) |
| **ScvO2** | ↓↓ (<65%) | ↓ | ↑ or normal (>70%) |
| **PCWP** | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ or normal |

---
conditions.management:
### Initial Stabilization
1. **Airway/breathing** — high-flow O2; BiPAP for pulmonary edema if patient can tolerate (reduces preload AND afterload); intubate if respiratory failure
2. **IV access** — two large-bore IVs; avoid excessive fluid boluses (this is NOT hypovolemic shock — fluid worsens pulmonary edema)
3. **Monitoring** — continuous telemetry, arterial line for beat-to-beat BP, Foley for urine output, central line for CVP/ScvO2 if available

### Vasopressors and Inotropes

| Agent | Receptor | Effect | When to Use |
|-------|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Norepinephrine** | Alpha-1 >> Beta-1 | ↑ SVR (vasoconstriction) + mild ↑ contractility | **First-line vasopressor** (SOAP II trial). Use when MAP <65 despite inotropes |
| **Dobutamine** | Beta-1 > Beta-2 | ↑ Contractility, ↑ HR, mild ↓ SVR (vasodilation) | **First-line inotrope** — directly addresses the pump failure. Watch for hypotension from vasodilation |
| **Milrinone** | PDE-3 inhibitor | ↑ Contractility + ↓ SVR (vasodilation) | Alternative/adjunct to dobutamine. Longer half-life; greater hypotension risk. Useful if on chronic beta-blockers (works independently of beta receptors) |
| **Dopamine** | Dose-dependent | Low: D1 (renal vasodilation). Moderate: Beta-1 (inotropy). High: Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) | **Second-line** — higher arrhythmia risk than norepinephrine (SOAP II) |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Volume is the enemy in cardiogenic shock.** Unlike hypovolemic shock, fluid boluses WORSEN pulmonary edema and increase myocardial workload. If unsure of shock type → bedside echo before volume loading
- **Titrate vasopressors to MAP ≥65** (or higher per provider) — use arterial line for accuracy
- **Monitor ScvO2 if central line available** — goal >65%. Rising ScvO2 = improving CO. Falling ScvO2 = deteriorating
- **Strict I&O** — urine output is your real-time renal perfusion indicator. Target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
- **Watch for arrhythmias** — ischemic myocardium is electrically unstable. Have defibrillator at bedside
- **Trending lactate** — q2-4h. Clearance >10%/2hr = responding to treatment. Rising lactate = failing
- **BiPAP can be a game-changer** — reduces preload (↓ venous return) AND afterload (↓ intrathoracic pressure improves LV ejection) while improving oxygenation. But patient must be alert enough to protect airway
- **Prepare for rapid escalation** — cardiogenic shock can deteriorate to cardiac arrest quickly. Keep crash cart at bedside, ACLS drugs drawn

---
pearls: **Cardiogenic shock is a spiral, not a state.** Without intervention, every compensatory response (tachycardia, vasoconstriction, fluid retention) worsens the failing heart. Treatment breaks the cycle: inotropes restore CO, vasopressors maintain MAP, diuretics offload congestion.

**Cold and wet = cardiogenic shock.** Cool extremities (↑ SVR) + pulmonary crackles/edema (↑ PCWP). Warm and wet = sepsis with pulmonary edema. Cold and dry = hypovolemia. This bedside assessment takes 30 seconds and guides initial management.

**ScvO2 <65% with elevated lactate = inadequate DO2.** In cardiogenic shock, this means the pump isn't delivering enough. Add inotrope support. In contrast, septic patients often have ScvO2 >70% because the problem is extraction, not delivery.

**Avoid fluids unless CLEARLY hypovolemic.** If JVD is present and lungs are wet, more fluid will push the patient toward intubation or arrest. A "small fluid challenge" in cardiogenic shock is NOT benign.
**Door-to-balloon time saves myocardium in MI-driven cardiogenic shock.** The more myocardium you reperfuse, the more pump function you salvage. Time is muscle.

conditions.title: Catatonia
conditions.overview:
Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of motor dysregulation — the patient's ability to move, speak, and interact with the environment is profoundly disrupted despite an alert consciousness. It is NOT simply "a patient who won't move or talk." It is a distinct syndrome with specific motor signs, a diagnostic test (the lorazepam challenge), and a specific treatment (benzodiazepines and ECT).

The biggest clinical danger of catatonia is **not recognizing it.** It is underdiagnosed because it looks like many other things — depression, psychosis, delirium, stroke, NMS, or "just being difficult." Missing it means missing the treatment, and untreated catatonia can progress to the malignant form (fever, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis, death).
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — GABA-B hypofunction and NMDA receptor dysfunction are leading mechanistic hypotheses; benzodiazepines (GABA-A enhancers) are first-line treatment*
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — dopamine D2 receptor dysregulation in basal ganglia motor circuits contributes to the motor manifestations*
conditions.keyFeatures:
### The Three Subtypes

| Subtype | Features | Frequency |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Retarded/Stuporous** (most common) | Immobility, mutism, staring, withdrawal, refusal to eat/drink, posturing | ~60% |
| **Excited** | Purposeless agitation, stereotypies, verbigeration, combativeness, autonomic instability | ~15% |
| **Malignant** | Fever, autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), altered consciousness, rigidity, rhabdomyolysis | ~10% — LIFE-THREATENING |

### Key Motor Signs — Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale (BFCRS)

The BFCRS is the standard diagnostic instrument. The first 14 items are the screening tool:

| Sign | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **Immobility/Stupor** | Minimal or no response to external stimuli despite apparent wakefulness |
| **Mutism** | No or minimal verbal response (not explained by aphasia) |
| **Staring** | Fixed gaze, reduced blinking, prolonged eye contact without purpose |
| **Posturing** | Spontaneously maintains unusual postures (e.g., arm extended in air) for prolonged periods |
| **Waxy flexibility** (catalepsy) | Examiner moves the patient's limb into a position → patient maintains that exact position for minutes (like bending a wax figure) |
| **Negativism** | Active resistance to instructions or attempts to be moved — opposite of what is asked |
| **Rigidity** | Maintained muscle stiffness not influenced by examiner's attempts to move joints |
conditions.management:
### Recognition Is the First Step

1. **Consider catatonia** in any patient who is:
   - Mute and immobile without clear neurologic explanation
   - Staring and unresponsive but apparently awake
   - Maintaining bizarre postures
   - Showing waxy flexibility on exam
   - Agitated with purposeless repetitive movements
   - Not responding to usual psychiatric interventions

2. **Screen with BFCRS** — if ≥2 items positive, proceed

3. **Lorazepam challenge** — 1-2 mg IV

### Medical Workup

Catatonia has medical causes that must be investigated:

| Test | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| CBC, CMP, TSH | Metabolic/endocrine causes |
| Urinalysis, UDS | Infection, substance-related |
| CPK | Rhabdomyolysis (from immobility or malignant catatonia) |
| Ammonia | Hepatic encephalopathy |
| Blood cultures | If febrile |
| CT head | Stroke, mass lesion, structural pathology |
| LP | If encephalitis suspected (fever + altered mental status) |
| Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies | Young woman with psychosis → catatonia (test even if not classic presentation) |
| ECG | Baseline (especially if antipsychotics are being considered — they're generally contraindicated) |

### Treatment

**First-line: Benzodiazepines**
- Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV/IM q4-8h, titrate up to 8-24 mg/day
- Response rate: **60-80%** respond to benzodiazepines alone
- Continue for several days even after symptoms resolve to prevent relapse
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **The patient who "won't move" or "won't talk" may have catatonia, not a behavioral problem.** Before labeling a patient as uncooperative, assess for the motor signs of catatonia. Check for waxy flexibility — gently raise the patient's arm to an unusual position and release. If they hold it there for 30+ seconds, that is catatonia, not choice
- **Immobility kills.** A catatonic patient who has been immobile for hours to days is at high risk for DVT/PE, pressure injury, rhabdomyolysis, aspiration (if refusing to swallow), and dehydration. Nursing interventions: DVT prophylaxis, skin assessment, repositioning, oral care, IV hydration, foley if not voiding
- **Monitor for malignant progression.** Serial vital signs — any development of fever, tachycardia, or rigidity in a catatonic patient may signal malignant catatonia. Report immediately
- **Do not interpret mutism as refusal.** The patient may hear and understand everything but be physically unable to respond. Speak to them respectfully, explain what you're doing, and assume they are aware. Many catatonic patients later describe being fully conscious but trapped
pearls: **Catatonia is dramatically underdiagnosed.** It affects 10-15% of psychiatric inpatients but is recognized in far fewer. The reason: it mimics other conditions (depression, psychosis, stroke, NMS) and many clinicians have never seen a lorazepam challenge. When in doubt, try the challenge — the diagnostic yield is high and the risk is low.

**The lorazepam challenge response can be breathtaking.** A patient who has been mute and immobile for days may suddenly start talking, eating, and engaging within 15-30 minutes of IV lorazepam. If you've never seen it, it looks like a miracle. It's not — it's pharmacology correcting a specific circuit dysfunction.

**Catatonia is caused by mood disorders more often than schizophrenia.** The historical association with schizophrenia is misleading. In modern series, bipolar disorder and major depression account for the majority of catatonia cases. Always assess the underlying psychiatric condition.

**Antipsychotics + catatonia = potential disaster.** This is counterintuitive because catatonia often presents with psychotic features, and the reflex is to reach for haloperidol. But antipsychotics block dopamine, which further disrupts the already-impaired basal ganglia circuits → worsening catatonia or triggering malignant catatonia. Benzodiazepines first, always.

conditions.title: Chronic Kidney Disease
conditions.overview: Chronic kidney disease is defined by persistent abnormalities of kidney structure or function (present for >3 months) that have implications for health. These abnormalities include impaired excretory renal function (eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m2), persistent albuminuria (ACR ≥30 mg/g), urine sediment abnormalities, electrolyte disturbances due to tubular disorders, structural abnormalities detected by imaging, or history of kidney transplantation. CKD represents a progressive and generally irreversible loss of functional nephrons.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the kidney IS the master homeostatic organ for fluid, electrolytes, and acid-base; CKD progressively dismantles every feedback loop it governs. Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — nephron loss leads to inability to regulate volume, osmolality, and electrolyte balance across all compartments.*

CKD progression follows a common pathway regardless of initial etiology:

1. **Initiating injury** reduces functional nephron mass (diabetic glomerulosclerosis, hypertensive nephrosclerosis, glomerulonephritis, polycystic kidney disease, etc.)
2. **Compensatory hyperfiltration** occurs in remaining nephrons — single-nephron GFR increases via afferent arteriolar dilation and glomerular hypertrophy
3. **Maladaptive response:** Increased intraglomerular pressure and flow cause podocyte injury, proteinuria, and progressive glomerulosclerosis
4. **RAAS activation** sustains efferent arteriolar constriction, maintaining GFR temporarily but perpetuating glomerular hypertension and fibrosis
5. **Tubulointerstitial fibrosis** — filtered proteins are toxic to tubular epithelial cells, triggering inflammatory cytokine release, myofibroblast activation, and progressive scarring
6. **Further nephron loss** accelerates the cycle — a vicious positive feedback loop
7. **Systemic consequences emerge** as nephron mass falls below critical thresholds:
   - eGFR <45: phosphorus retention begins, secondary hyperparathyroidism develops
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Early (Stages 1-3a) — often asymptomatic:**
- Detected incidentally on routine labs (elevated creatinine, abnormal urinalysis)
- Nocturia (early loss of concentrating ability)
- Mild fatigue
- Foamy urine (proteinuria)

**Moderate (Stages 3b-4):**
- Progressive fatigue and exercise intolerance (anemia)
- Edema (sodium and water retention)
- Anorexia, nausea
- Pruritus (phosphorus retention, uremic toxins)
- Restless legs syndrome
- Cognitive impairment

**Advanced/Uremia (Stage 5):**
- Severe nausea, vomiting, anorexia, weight loss
- Uremic frost (rare), sallow skin color
- Pericardial friction rub
- Asterixis, myoclonus, seizures
- Peripheral neuropathy (stocking-glove distribution)
- Bleeding tendency (platelet dysfunction)
- Ammonia breath (uremic fetor)

### Complications
- Heart Failure and accelerated atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease
- Secondary hyperparathyroidism and renal osteodystrophy (CKD-MBD)
- Anemia of chronic kidney disease (erythropoietin deficiency)
- Metabolic acidosis with muscle wasting and bone demineralization
- Hyperkalemia and life-threatening arrhythmias
- Volume overload and resistant Hypertension
- Malnutrition (protein-energy wasting)
- Increased infection risk (uremic immunodeficiency)
- Progression to end-stage kidney disease requiring KRT
conditions.management:
### Acute
- Manage complications of advanced CKD presenting acutely: hyperkalemia, pulmonary edema, severe acidosis, uremic pericarditis
- Evaluate for reversible causes of acute-on-chronic decline: Acute Kidney Injury superimposed on CKD (volume depletion, nephrotoxins, obstruction, infection)
- Urgent dialysis initiation if life-threatening indications present

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Blood pressure control:** Target <120/80 mmHg in most CKD patients with albuminuria; ACE Inhibitors or ARBs are first-line for proteinuric CKD
- **RAAS inhibition:** ACE Inhibitors or ARBs reduce intraglomerular pressure and slow progression; monitor K+ and creatinine (accept up to 30% rise in Cr)
- **SGLT2 inhibitors:** Now a cornerstone of CKD therapy regardless of diabetes status — dapagliflozin and empagliflozin reduce progression to ESKD, cardiovascular death, and hospitalization for Heart Failure
- **Glycemic control:** HbA1c target ~7% for diabetic CKD (individualize for elderly/frail)
- **Metabolic acidosis:** Oral sodium bicarbonate to maintain serum HCO3 ≥22 mEq/L; slows progression
- **Anemia management:** Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs) targeting Hgb 10-11.5 g/dL; IV iron supplementation to maintain TSAT >20% and ferritin >100 ng/mL
- **CKD-MBD:** Phosphate binders (sevelamer, lanthanum), calcitriol or vitamin D analogs, dietary phosphorus restriction
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Medication safety is paramount.** CKD patients require dose adjustments for nearly every renally cleared drug. Check eGFR before administering and question any dose that seems standard — metformin, gabapentin, enoxaparin, and morphine are common culprits for toxicity in CKD.
- **Blood pressure monitoring:** Orthostatic measurements are essential. These patients are often on multiple antihypertensives and Diuretics. Assess lying, sitting, and standing BP before administering medications that lower blood pressure.
- **Potassium awareness:** Know the patient's most recent K+ before giving any potassium-containing fluids (including LR). Monitor for ECG changes. Educate patients on high-potassium foods (bananas, oranges, tomatoes, potatoes, salt substitutes).
- **Vascular access protection:** If the patient has an AV fistula or graft — NO blood pressures, NO blood draws, NO IVs in that arm. Assess for thrill (palpable) and bruit (auscultate) every shift. Report absence immediately.
- **Fluid balance:** Daily weights, strict I&O, assess for edema and lung crackles. Patients on fluid restrictions need creative nursing (ice chips, small cups, mouth care to relieve thirst).
pearls: **A 30% rise in creatinine after starting an ACE inhibitor is expected and acceptable** — this reflects reduced intraglomerular pressure, which is the therapeutic goal. Do not reflexively stop the drug. Only discontinue if creatinine rises >30% or hyperkalemia develops.

**CKD patients die with their kidneys, not of their kidneys** — cardiovascular disease kills more CKD patients than kidney failure ever will. Aggressive cardiovascular risk management (statins, BP control, smoking cessation) saves more lives than focusing solely on GFR.

**The AV fistula arm is sacred** — a single traumatic blood draw or tight BP cuff can destroy a fistula that took months to mature and is the patient's lifeline. Protect it like the critical access it is. No sticks, no pressures, no tourniquets.

**"Renal diet" contradicts almost every healthy eating message patients have heard** — whole wheat bread (phosphorus), bananas and oranges (potassium), dairy (phosphorus and potassium) are all restricted. Patients need intensive, repeated dietary education because it is genuinely counterintuitive.

**SGLT2 inhibitors will initially drop eGFR by 3-5 mL/min — this is hemodynamic and protective** — like ACE inhibitors, the initial dip reflects reduced hyperfiltration. Explain to patients that a small "worsening" of their kidney number is actually a sign the drug is working.

conditions.title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
conditions.overview: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is a progressive, largely irreversible airflow limitation caused by chronic inflammation of the airways and lung parenchyma, primarily from tobacco smoke exposure. The two overlapping phenotypes are **chronic bronchitis** (productive cough ≥3 months/year for ≥2 consecutive years) and **emphysema** (destruction of alveolar walls with air trapping). COPD is defined by post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70.
conditions.pathophysiology:
COPD involves two interconnected processes:

### Chronic Bronchitis
- Chronic inflammation → goblet cell hyperplasia → excessive mucus production
- Mucosal edema and bronchospasm narrow airways
- Impaired mucociliary clearance → mucus plugging → bacterial colonization
- Results in productive cough, airway obstruction, V/Q mismatch

### Emphysema
- Protease-antiprotease imbalance (neutrophil elastase destroys alveolar walls)
- Loss of elastic recoil → air trapping → hyperinflation
- Destruction of alveolar-capillary membrane → reduced gas exchange surface area
- Results in dyspnea, decreased diffusion capacity, increased dead space

### Acute Exacerbation Pathophysiology
> *First principles: Oxygenation and Ventilation COPD exacerbations worsen V/Q mismatch: bronchospasm creates low-V/Q shunt-like units; emphysema adds high-V/Q dead space; together driving hypoxemia with hypercapnia.*

- Trigger (infection, pollution, cold air) → increased airway inflammation
- Worsened bronchospasm + mucus production + edema
- V/Q mismatch worsens → hypoxemia → potential hypercapnia
- Increased work of breathing → respiratory muscle fatigue → potential respiratory failure
- Can trigger right heart strain (cor pulmonale) in severe cases
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs and Symptoms
**Chronic/Stable COPD:**
- Progressive dyspnea (initially exertional, then at rest)
- Chronic productive cough (especially morning)
- Wheezing, chest tightness
- Exercise intolerance, fatigue
- **Pink puffer** phenotype (emphysema-dominant): thin, pursed-lip breathing, barrel chest, accessory muscle use, minimal cyanosis
- **Blue bloater** phenotype (bronchitis-dominant): overweight, chronic cough with copious sputum, peripheral edema, cyanosis

**Acute Exacerbation (AECOPD):**
- Worsened dyspnea beyond normal day-to-day variation
- Increased sputum volume AND/OR purulence
- Increased cough
- Tachypnea (RR >24), tachycardia
- Accessory muscle use, tripod positioning
- Inability to speak in full sentences
- Decreased breath sounds, diffuse wheezing, prolonged expiratory phase
- Altered mental status (CO2 narcosis = late/ominous sign)
- Paradoxical chest wall movement (sign of respiratory failure)

### Complications
- Acute respiratory failure (hypoxemic and/or hypercapnic)
- Cor pulmonale (right heart failure from chronic pulmonary hypertension)
- Pneumothorax (rupture of blebs/bullae)
- Polycythemia (chronic hypoxemia → increased RBC production)
- Depression and anxiety (up to 40% of COPD patients)
- Pulmonary hypertension
- Cachexia/muscle wasting in advanced disease
- Frequent pneumonia
conditions.management:
### Acute Management (AECOPD in ED)
1. **Oxygen:** Target SpO2 88-92% (NOT 94-100% — risk of CO2 retention)
   - Nasal cannula 1-2 L/min first
   - Venturi mask for precise FiO2 delivery (24-28%)
   - **Do NOT withhold oxygen** if patient truly hypoxemic — just titrate carefully
2. **Short-acting bronchodilators:**
   - Albuterol 2.5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulizer q20min × 3, then q1-4h
   - Or albuterol MDI 4-8 puffs q20min × 3 via spacer
3. **Systemic corticosteroids:**
   - Prednisone 40 mg PO daily × 5 days (REDUCE trial — 5 days = 14 days)
   - Or methylprednisolone 125 mg IV if unable to take PO
4. **Antibiotics (if indicated):**
   - Indicated for: increased sputum purulence + increased dyspnea or sputum volume
   - Azithromycin 500 mg × 1 then 250 mg × 4 days OR doxycycline 100 mg BID × 5-7 days
   - Amoxicillin-clavulanate for moderate-severe or recent antibiotic use
   - Levofloxacin 750 mg daily × 5 days for severe or treatment failure
5. **Non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP):**
   - **First-line** for acute respiratory failure with COPD exacerbation
   - Indications: respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35, PaCO2 >45), severe dyspnea with accessory muscle use, persistent hypoxemia despite supplemental O2
   - Start: IPAP 10-12, EPAP 4-5; titrate to comfort, RR, tidal volume
   - Reduces intubation rate by ~65% and mortality by ~50%
6. **Intubation:** Last resort if NIV fails (worsening pH, mental status, hemodynamic instability)
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### ED Assessment Priorities
- **ABCs first** — assess airway patency, work of breathing, oxygen saturation
- **Obtain ABG early** in moderate-severe exacerbation (establishes baseline, guides ventilation decisions)
- **Ask about home oxygen use** and baseline SpO2 — a patient on 2L at home with SpO2 of 88% may be at THEIR baseline
- **Medication history:** Current inhalers, recent steroid courses, recent antibiotic use, home BiPAP or CPAP
- **Assess for triggers:** URI symptoms, increased sputum, medication non-adherence, environmental exposures
- **Look for complications:** Pneumothorax (absent breath sounds unilaterally), PE, pneumonia, CHF

### Monitoring Parameters
- Continuous pulse oximetry (target 88-92%)
- Respiratory rate, work of breathing, accessory muscle use
- Mental status (CO2 narcosis: confusion → somnolence → coma)
- ABG trending if initially abnormal (repeat in 30-60 min after intervention)
- I&O (dehydration worsens mucus viscosity)
- Cardiac monitor (arrhythmias common — beta-agonists + hypoxemia + acidosis)

### Medication Administration
- **Nebulizers:** Continuous nebs for severe exacerbation; ensure adequate flow (6-8 L/min)
pearls: **"88 to 92 — that's the COPD SpO2 plan"** — Overtreating with high-flow O2 can worsen CO2 retention through the Haldane effect and suppression of hypoxic drive. Titrate to this range and check an ABG if the patient looks worse despite "good" SpO2

**"BiPAP before the tube"** — NIV is so effective in COPD exacerbations that it should be attempted in nearly every patient before intubation. The exception is the obtunded patient who cannot protect their airway or the patient in cardiac arrest

**"The quiet COPD patient is the scary one"** — Severe air trapping can reduce air movement to the point where wheezing disappears. No wheezing + increased distress = silent chest = impending respiratory arrest. This patient needs immediate escalation

**"Check the bicarb"** — A chronically elevated bicarb (28-34) on BMP tells you this patient is a CO2 retainer at baseline. Their "normal" PaCO2 may be 50-55, not 40. Treat the patient, not the number

**"Know their baseline"** — Ask every COPD patient: "What's your normal oxygen?" and "Do you use oxygen at home?" A SpO2 of 90% may be perfectly normal for them, or it may represent a crisis — context is everything

conditions.title: Chronic Pancreatitis
conditions.overview: Chronic pancreatitis is a progressive inflammatory disease of the pancreas characterized by irreversible morphological changes (fibrosis, calcifications, ductal dilation and irregularity) leading to permanent impairment of exocrine function (maldigestion, steatorrhea) and endocrine function (diabetes mellitus type 3c). Chronic pain is the dominant clinical feature.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Enzymes and Biological Catalysis — each acute episode of enzyme activation — however mild — triggers pancreatic stellate cell activation; these cells deposit collagen in a "necrosis-fibrosis sequence"; repeated cycles progressively replace secretory acinar tissue with fibrous scar, eventually causing >90% exocrine loss (steatorrhea threshold) and islet destruction (type 3c diabetes)*
> *First principles: Nutrition and Metabolism — exocrine insufficiency eliminates pancreatic lipase, amylase, and proteases; fats cannot be hydrolyzed to absorbable fatty acids without lipase, producing steatorrhea; fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) require micellar formation from digested fats and are severely depleted, driving specific deficiency syndromes including coagulopathy (K) and metabolic bone disease (D)*
> *First principles: Enzymes and Biological Catalysis — repeated trypsinogen activation (from PRSS1 gain-of-function or loss of SPINK1 inhibitor) destroys acinar cells from within; the protease/anti-protease imbalance is the molecular engine of acinar cell destruction and fibrosis*
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — chronic sentinel acute pancreatitis events drive fibroinflammatory progression; each episode recruits neutrophils, activates Kupffer-like stellate cells, and deposits collagen; what starts as acute inflammation becomes irreversible structural remodeling*
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Chronic abdominal pain (85-90% of patients):**
  - Epigastric, radiating to back
  - Constant or intermittent
  - Worse after eating (postprandial)
  - May decrease over years as gland "burns out" (fibrosis replaces functioning tissue)
- **Exocrine insufficiency (when >90% function lost):**
  - Steatorrhea (pale, bulky, foul-smelling, floating stools)
  - Weight loss and malnutrition
  - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K)
  - Protein malnutrition
- **Endocrine insufficiency:**
  - Diabetes mellitus type 3c (pancreatogenic diabetes)
  - Both insulin and glucagon deficiency
  - Brittle diabetes (hypoglycemia unawareness)
- **Nausea, vomiting, anorexia**
- **Jaundice (if bile duct stricture from fibrosis in pancreatic head)**

### Complications
- **Pancreatic pseudocyst:** Fluid collection surrounded by non-epithelialized wall; may become infected or rupture
- **Bile duct stricture:** Compression by inflamed pancreatic head; obstructive jaundice, cholangitis
- **Duodenal obstruction:** Rare; from pancreatic head inflammation/fibrosis
- **Splenic vein thrombosis:** Left-sided portal hypertension; gastric varices
- **Pancreatic ascites/pleural effusion:** From duct disruption
- **Pancreatic cancer:** Increased risk (2-3% cumulative risk); difficult to distinguish from chronic inflammation
- **Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies (A, D, E, K, B12), osteoporosis
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Pain management:**
  - **Non-opioid analgesics:** Acetaminophen, NSAIDs (if tolerated)
  - **Opioid analgesics:** Often required; risk of dependence; use multimodal approach
  - **Adjuvant agents:** Pregabalin, gabapentin, tricyclic antidepressants for neuropathic pain
  - **Celiac plexus block:** EUS-guided or CT-guided; temporary pain relief (3-6 months); repeat if effective
- **Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT):**
  - Indications: Steatorrhea, weight loss, fecal elastase <200 μg/g
  - High-dose enzymes (40,000-80,000 units lipase per meal; 20,000-40,000 units with snacks)
  - Enteric-coated formulations release enzymes in duodenum
  - Take with first bite of meal (distribute throughout meal if large)
  - Add proton pump inhibitor if inadequate response (optimize duodenal pH for enzyme activity)
- **Nutritional support:**
  - Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K)
  - Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oil (absorbed without lipase)
  - High-calorie, high-protein diet
  - Small, frequent meals
  - Dietitian referral
- **Diabetes management:**
  - Insulin therapy (type 3c diabetes)
  - Frequent glucose monitoring (risk of hypoglycemia due to glucagon deficiency)
  - Avoid sulfonylureas and other secretagogues
- **Alcohol cessation:** Absolute requirement; slows progression
- **Smoking cessation:** Slows progression; reduces pain

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Pain management:**
  - Continue multimodal analgesia
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Pain assessment:**
  - Comprehensive pain evaluation (intensity, location, quality, triggers, relieving factors)
  - Distinguish pancreatic pain from other causes (peptic ulcer, biliary colic, bowel obstruction)
  - Monitor for signs of opioid dependence/misuse
  - Non-pharmacologic pain management (relaxation techniques, positioning)
- **Nutritional assessment and support:**
  - Monitor weight, BMI, albumin, prealbumin
  - Assess for signs of malnutrition (muscle wasting, edema, poor wound healing)
  - Fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies: vitamin A (night blindness), vitamin D (bone pain, fractures), vitamin K (easy bruising/bleeding), vitamin E (neuropathy)
  - PERT teaching:
    - Take enzymes with first bite of food
    - Adequate dosing (40,000-80,000 units lipase per meal)
    - Swallow capsules whole (do not crush); if difficulty swallowing, open capsule and mix microspheres with acidic food (applesauce), do not chew
    - Assess efficacy: improved stool consistency, weight gain, reduced bloating
  - Dietary modifications: small frequent meals, avoid high-fat foods if not on PERT or suboptimal response
  - MCT oil supplementation
- **Diabetes management:**
pearls: **Alcohol cessation is non-negotiable** — continued drinking accelerates disease progression, worsens pain, reduces treatment efficacy, and increases mortality; every encounter should reinforce absolute abstinence.

**PERT dosing matters: start high and go higher if needed** — many patients are underdosed; start with 40,000-80,000 units lipase per meal (not per day); if steatorrhea persists, double or triple the dose; enzymes are safe at high doses and inadequate replacement perpetuates malnutrition.

**Take enzymes with the first bite, not before or after** — timing is critical for optimal enzyme-food mixing in the duodenum; distribute enzymes throughout large meals; emphasize this in patient teaching.

**Type 3c diabetes is brittle because of glucagon deficiency** — unlike type 1 or type 2 diabetes, patients with pancreatogenic diabetes lack both insulin AND glucagon; this creates high risk of hypoglycemia and makes glucose control challenging; warn patients and adjust insulin cautiously.

**Screen for osteoporosis, not just vitamin deficiencies** — fat-soluble vitamin D malabsorption plus chronic inflammation significantly increase osteoporosis risk; DEXA scan should be part of routine care, especially in patients with long-standing disease or corticosteroid use.

conditions.title: Cluster Headache
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — trigeminovascular activation releases CGRP (calcitonin gene-related peptide), a neuropeptide that acts via GPCRs to drive vasodilation and neurogenic inflammation; galcanezumab and sumatriptan both target this CGRP signaling pathway*
> *First principles: Pain Physiology and Management — trigeminal V1 branch transmits nociceptive signals via A-delta and C fibers; the clockwork circadian periodicity reflects hypothalamic circadian circuit involvement, not a peripheral pain generator*

### The Trigeminal-Hypothalamic-Autonomic Circuit
Cluster headache involves three interconnected neural systems:

1. **Hypothalamic activation** — the pacemaker
   - PET imaging shows **posterior hypothalamic grey matter activation** during attacks
   - Explains the **clockwork periodicity** — attacks at the same time daily (often 1-2 AM), same season yearly
   - The hypothalamus is the body's circadian clock → explains why clusters correlate with circadian/circannual rhythms
   - Supports role of altered circadian regulation in pathogenesis

2. **Trigeminal-vascular system** — the pain pathway
   - Activation of the **ophthalmic division (V1)** of the trigeminal nerve
   - Release of **CGRP** (calcitonin gene-related peptide) from trigeminal sensory neurons → vasodilation, neurogenic inflammation → intense periorbital pain
conditions.keyFeatures:
### The Attack
| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pain quality** | Excruciating, boring, stabbing — described as "ice pick through the eye," "red-hot poker" |
| **Pain location** | Strictly **unilateral** — periorbital, retro-orbital, temporal; same side every cluster |
| **Intensity** | **10/10** — the worst pain imaginable; worse than kidney stones, fractures, childbirth in patient reports |
| **Duration** | 15-180 minutes per attack (untreated) |
| **Frequency** | 1-8 attacks per day during cluster period |
| **Timing** | Clockwork regularity — often 1-2 AM (REM sleep-related), same time daily |

### Ipsilateral Autonomic Features (Must Be Present)
At least one of:
- **Lacrimation** (tearing) — most common
- **Conjunctival injection** (red eye)
- **Nasal congestion or rhinorrhea**
- **Forehead/facial sweating**
- **Eyelid edema**
- **Ptosis and/or miosis** (partial Horner's syndrome)
- **Sensation of aural fullness**

### Behavioral Features — The Diagnostic Clue
**Cluster headache patients are AGITATED during attacks:**
- Pacing, rocking, hitting their head against the wall
- Cannot lie still — they want to move
- May be found in unusual positions (kneeling, squatting)

> [!important]
conditions.management:
### Acute Attack Treatment (ED)

#### High-Flow Oxygen — First-Line
- **100% O₂ at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask** × 15-20 minutes
- Effective in ~70-80% of attacks
- Onset: 5-10 minutes
- **No side effects, no contraindications** — the ideal first-line
- Mechanism: possibly vasoconstriction, hypothalamic modulation, CGRP inhibition
- Patient should sit upright and breathe deeply

#### Sumatriptan — First-Line (Fastest Pharmacologic)
- **Sumatriptan 6 mg SC** — the gold standard; onset 5-10 minutes; effective in ~75%
  - SC > nasal > oral for cluster (oral is too slow for a 15-180 minute attack)
- **Sumatriptan 20 mg intranasal** — alternative if SC not available
- Max: 2 SC injections per 24 hours, separated by ≥1 hour
- Contraindicated in uncontrolled HTN, recent MI/stroke, coronary artery disease

#### Other Acute Options
| Agent | Route/Dose | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Zolmitriptan** 5 mg nasal | Intranasal | Alternative triptan |
| **Lidocaine** 4% nasal | Ipsilateral nostril, head tilted back 45° | Sphenopalatine ganglion block; adjunctive, ~30% effective |
| **Octreotide** 100 mcg SC | Subcutaneous | If triptans contraindicated |

> [!warning]
> **Opioids and butalbital are NOT effective for cluster headache** and should not be used. They don't work, they delay effective treatment, and they risk medication overuse headache. Oxygen and sumatriptan SC are the evidence-based treatments.

### Transitional Therapy (Bridge to Preventive)
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### In the ED
- **Treat FAST** — cluster attacks are among the most painful conditions in medicine; delays are cruel
- **Oxygen first** — no order needed for O₂ in many EDs; get the NRB at 15 L/min flowing immediately
- **Sumatriptan SC** — prepare for rapid administration; the autoinjector can be taught for self-use
- **Dark, quiet room** — while less critical than migraine (patient is agitated, not photophobic), a calm environment helps
- **Suicidal ideation screening** — "suicide headache" is not hyperbole; the severity of pain drives genuine suicidality; ASQ or direct questioning

### Patient Assessment
- **Characterize the headache** — use OPQRST; the pattern (unilateral, autonomic features, duration, agitation) is diagnostic
- **Document the autonomic features** — tearing, red eye, ptosis, nasal congestion (these distinguish cluster from migraine and other headaches)
- **Assess for medication overuse** — frequent ED visits for headache → ask about analgesic use pattern

### Patient Education
- **Oxygen at home** — patients can get home O₂ prescribed for abortive use; dramatically reduces ED visits
- **Sumatriptan autoinjectors** — can be prescribed for home use
pearls: **Agitated + unilateral + red eye + tearing = cluster headache** — the behavior during the attack is the single best diagnostic clue; migraine patients lie still, cluster patients pace and hit things
**Oxygen first, triptan second** — 15 L/min NRB mask is as effective as sumatriptan, has zero side effects, and can be started while preparing the injection
**Oral triptans are too slow** — cluster attacks can be 15 minutes; by the time an oral tablet absorbs, the attack may be over; always use SC or intranasal route
**Opioids don't work for cluster headache** — they are ineffective AND harmful (medication overuse, delay in proper treatment); push back against opioid requests and offer evidence-based alternatives
**Screen for suicidality** — the name "suicide headache" exists for a reason; these patients are in unbearable pain multiple times daily; ask directly about suicidal thoughts

conditions.title: Contact Dermatitis
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — allergic contact dermatitis = type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity: sensitization phase (antigen-presenting Langerhans cells prime T cells) then elicitation phase (memory T cells release cytokines → spongiotic dermatitis); irritant contact dermatitis = innate inflammatory response without prior sensitization.*

### Irritant Contact Dermatitis (ICD) — Direct Damage
- **No immune memory required** — happens on first exposure
- The substance directly damages keratinocytes through:
  - Chemical disruption of the lipid barrier (detergents, solvents)
  - pH extremes (acids, alkalis)
  - Friction/mechanical irritation
  - Dehydration of stratum corneum
- Damaged keratinocytes release **pro-inflammatory cytokines** (IL-1, TNF-α) → inflammation
- Severity depends on: **concentration, duration, frequency, and skin barrier integrity**
- **Healthcare worker hand dermatitis is almost always ICD** — from frequent handwashing, glove use, alcohol-based sanitizers

### Allergic Contact Dermatitis (ACD) — Type IV Hypersensitivity
A two-phase immune response (from Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology):

**Phase 1 — Sensitization (10-14 days, asymptomatic):**
1. Allergen (hapten — small molecule, <500 Da) penetrates skin
2. Hapten binds to carrier proteins → becomes a complete antigen
3. **Langerhans cells** (dendritic cells of the epidermis) capture the antigen
