4. Langerhans cells migrate to regional lymph nodes
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Acute Contact Dermatitis
- **Erythema, edema, vesicles/bullae** (especially ACD)
- Weeping, crusting
- Intense pruritus
- **Sharp demarcation** at the contact boundary — the distribution mirrors the contactant
  - Linear streaks = plant (poison ivy)
  - Periumbilical = belt buckle (nickel)
  - Dorsal hands with finger web sparing = glove dermatitis
  - Wrist circumferential = watch/bracelet
  - Earlobes = earrings (nickel)

### Chronic Contact Dermatitis
- Lichenification (thickened skin with exaggerated skin lines)
- Fissuring, scaling
- Hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation
- Less vesiculation than acute
- Distribution may be less clearly demarcated

### ICD vs ACD — Key Differences
| Feature | ICD | ACD |
|---|---|---|
| **Immune memory** | No | Yes (Type IV) |
| **First exposure** | Can cause reaction | No reaction (sensitization only) |
| **Onset** | Minutes to hours | 24-72 hours (delayed) |
| **Dose-response** | Strong (more irritant = more damage) | Weak (small amount can trigger) |
| **Borders** | Sharp, limited to contact | May spread beyond contact area |
| **Vesicles** | Less common | Common |
| **ID reaction** | No | Possible (autoeczematization — spread to distant sites) |

---
conditions.management:
### Acute Management
| Severity | Treatment |
|---|---|
| **Mild, localized** | Mid-potency topical corticosteroid (triamcinolone 0.1% BID × 2-3 weeks); cool compresses; avoidance |
| **Moderate** | High-potency topical steroid (clobetasol 0.05% BID for body; avoid face/folds); antihistamine for pruritus |
| **Severe/widespread** | **Oral prednisone taper** — 0.5-1 mg/kg/day, taper over **2-3 weeks minimum**; shorter courses → rebound |
| **Facial/intertriginous** | Low-potency steroid (hydrocortisone 1-2.5%) or calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) |

> [!warning]
> **Poison ivy prednisone tapers must be long enough** — a 5-day Medrol dose pack is too short; poison ivy ACD lasts 2-3 weeks; stopping steroids at day 5 almost always results in rebound dermatitis. Use a 14-21 day taper.

### Allergen Avoidance
- **Identify and eliminate the contactant** — the most important intervention
- For nickel: clear nail polish on jewelry as barrier; nickel-free products
- For glove allergy: switch to nitrile (avoid rubber accelerators); vinyl for low-risk tasks
- For occupational dermatitis: PPE modifications, workplace accommodation
- Substitute products (fragrance-free, preservative-free alternatives)

### Skin Barrier Repair
- **Emollients** — ceramide-containing moisturizers (CeraVe, Vanicream)
- Apply immediately after hand washing
- Cotton glove liners under protective gloves
- Minimize wet work when possible

---
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Assessment
- **Document distribution precisely** — shape, borders, bilateral vs unilateral, relationship to potential contactants
- **Occupational history** — what do they do for work? What do they touch?
- **Product history** — new soaps, detergents, cosmetics, topical medications
- **Timing** — when did it start? What happened 24-72 hours before onset?

### For Your Own Hands (Healthcare Worker Skin Protection)
- **Moisturize after every hand wash** — hospital-provided moisturizers or personal ceramide cream
- **Pat dry, don't rub** — friction worsens barrier damage
- **Nitrile gloves** if rubber accelerator allergy suspected
- Alcohol-based hand rub is actually **less drying** than soap and water — prefer it when hands aren't visibly soiled
- Report hand dermatitis to occupational health — don't ignore it; worsening barrier → increased infection risk for you AND patients

### Patient Education
- **Avoidance is the treatment** — identifying and eliminating the trigger is more important than any medication
- Complete the steroid course — don't stop when it "looks better"
- Apply topical steroids to affected skin only, not as moisturizer
pearls: **Distribution is diagnosis** — linear streaks = plant; periumbilical = nickel belt buckle; dorsal hands sparing webs = gloves; ear lobes = nickel earrings; the pattern tells you the allergen
**5-day Medrol dose pack for poison ivy = rebound** — urushiol dermatitis lasts 2-3 weeks; you need a 14-21 day prednisone taper; this is one of the most common ED prescribing errors
**"I've used this product for years" doesn't rule out ACD** — sensitization can develop after months or years of prior tolerance; it only takes one exposure to create memory T cells
**Healthcare worker hand dermatitis is an occupational hazard** — most is ICD from handwashing; some is ACD to rubber accelerators; neither should be ignored; compromised skin is an infection risk

**Neomycin allergy is common and underrecognized** — Neosporin is one of the most common causes of ACD; if a wound is getting worse with topical antibiotic, it may be the "treatment" causing dermatitis, not infection

conditions.title: Crohn's Disease
conditions.overview: Crohn's disease is a chronic, relapsing-remitting inflammatory bowel disease characterized by transmural inflammation that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract from mouth to anus. It is distinguished by skip lesions (discontinuous areas of inflammation), non-caseating granulomas, cobblestone appearance of mucosa, and complications including strictures, fistulae, and abscesses.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — Crohn's = dysregulated innate + adaptive immune response at gut mucosa; impaired bacterial clearance by innate immunity → excessive Th1/Th17 adaptive response → transmural inflammation with TNF-α, IL-12, IL-23; transmural depth distinguishes Crohn's from UC's mucosal-only pattern*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — loss of mucosal barrier tolerance is the initiating failure; once Th1/Th17 activation becomes self-sustaining (positive feedback through cytokine amplification), the system can no longer return to mucosal homeostasis → fistulae, strictures, and systemic extraintestinal disease are the manifestations of complete feedback loop failure*

Crohn's disease results from dysregulated immune response to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals:
- **Genetic factors:** NOD2/CARD15 mutations impair innate immune recognition of bacteria; autophagy genes (ATG16L1, IRGM) affect intracellular bacterial handling; IL-23 receptor pathway genes affect Th17 responses
- **Environmental triggers:** Smoking, diet, infections, antibiotics in early life alter microbiome composition
- **Altered microbiome:** Dysbiosis with decreased bacterial diversity, reduced Firmicutes (especially Faecalibacterium prausnitzii), increased adherent-invasive E. coli
- **Immune dysregulation:**
  - Impaired epithelial barrier function allows bacterial translocation
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Gastrointestinal symptoms:**
  - Chronic diarrhea (may be bloody if colonic involvement)
  - Abdominal pain (often right lower quadrant; cramping, postprandial)
  - Weight loss and malnutrition
  - Nausea and vomiting (if obstruction)
  - Rectal bleeding (less common than in Ulcerative Colitis)
  - Perianal disease (skin tags, fissures, fistulae, abscesses) in 30-40%
- **Extraintestinal manifestations (affecting 25-40% of patients):**
  - **Musculoskeletal:** Peripheral arthritis (knees, ankles, wrists), axial arthropathy (ankylosing spondylitis, sacroiliitis)
  - **Dermatologic:** Erythema nodosum (tender red nodules on shins), pyoderma gangrenosum (ulcerating skin lesions)
  - **Ophthalmologic:** Uveitis, episcleritis
  - **Hepatobiliary:** Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC, more common in Ulcerative Colitis), cholelithiasis
- **Constitutional symptoms:** Fever, fatigue, night sweats

### Complications
- **Strictures:** Fibrostenotic disease causing bowel obstruction (abdominal pain, distension, vomiting)
- **Fistulae:** Enteroenteric, enterovesical (pneumaturia, UTIs), enterocutaneous, perianal
- **Abscesses:** Intra-abdominal or perianal; present with fever, pain, leukocytosis
- **Perforation:** Free perforation (rare) or contained perforation with abscess
- **Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies (B12, folate, fat-soluble vitamins A/D/E/K), mineral deficiencies (iron, zinc, magnesium)
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Mild-moderate disease:**
  - **5-ASA compounds:** Limited efficacy in Crohn's; may use in mild colonic disease
  - **Budesonide:** Locally active corticosteroid; 9 mg daily for 8 weeks (ileal/right colon disease); fewer systemic side effects
  - **Antibiotics:** Metronidazole or ciprofloxacin for perianal disease
- **Moderate-severe disease:**
  - **Systemic corticosteroids:** Prednisone 40-60 mg daily, taper over 8-12 weeks; bridge to steroid-sparing therapy
  - **Nutritional therapy:** Exclusive enteral nutrition (EEN) effective in children; role in adults less clear
- **Severe/fulminant disease:**
  - Hospitalization
  - IV corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 40-60 mg daily or hydrocortisone 300-400 mg daily)
  - NPO, IV fluids, electrolyte repletion
  - Nutritional support (TPN if needed)
  - Rule out complications (abscess, perforation, toxic megacolon)
  - Biologic therapy if corticosteroid-refractory

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Treatment approach:** Step-up (start with less aggressive therapy, escalate if needed) vs. top-down (start with biologics early, especially in high-risk patients)

- **Immunomodulators (steroid-sparing agents):**
  - **Azathioprine** (2-2.5 mg/kg/day) or **6-mercaptopurine** (1-1.5 mg/kg/day): Slow onset (3-6 months); monitor CBC, LFTs; check TPMT activity before starting
  - **Methotrexate:** 15-25 mg weekly SC/IM; monitor CBC, LFTs; requires folate supplementation; teratogenic
- **Biologic therapy (disease-modifying agents):**
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Nutritional assessment:**
  - Monitor weight, BMI, albumin, prealbumin
  - Screen for micronutrient deficiencies (B12, iron, folate, vitamin D, zinc)
  - Dietitian referral for meal planning
  - Consider enteral supplementation (oral supplements, tube feeding if severe malnutrition)
  - TPN if bowel rest required
- **Medication education:**
  - **Biologics:** Teach self-injection technique for SC agents (adalimumab); coordinate IV infusion schedules (infliximab, vedolizumab)
  - Infection precautions: Avoid live vaccines, report fever/infection promptly
  - Importance of adherence to prevent loss of response and disease progression
  - Monitoring requirements (CBC, LFTs for immunomodulators)
- **Symptom management:**
  - Diarrhea management: Antidiarrheals (loperamide) with caution (avoid in severe flares)
  - Pain assessment: Distinguish inflammatory pain from stricture/obstruction
  - Perianal care: Sitz baths, barrier creams, wound care for fistulae
- **Stoma care:** If ileostomy or colostomy; teach stoma management, skin care, dietary modifications
- **Psychosocial support:**
  - Screen for anxiety, depression (higher rates in IBD)
pearls: **Smoking cessation is the single most important modifiable factor** — smoking doubles the risk of developing Crohn's, increases flare frequency, accelerates progression to stricturing/fistulizing disease, increases post-operative recurrence, and reduces medication efficacy. Every encounter should include smoking cessation counseling.

**Perianal disease is a red flag for Crohn's, not hemorrhoids** — 30-40% of Crohn's patients develop perianal manifestations (fistulae, abscesses, fissures, skin tags); persistent or recurrent "hemorrhoids" in a young patient should prompt evaluation for IBD.

**Fecal calprotectin is your objective marker of intestinal inflammation** — levels >250 μg/g are highly specific for active IBD; use to distinguish inflammatory flare from functional symptoms (IBS), monitor treatment response, and assess for subclinical inflammation.

**Right lower quadrant pain in a young adult? Think terminal ileum** — the most common Crohn's presentation is ileocecal disease mimicking appendicitis; don't miss it on the differential for RLQ pain with chronic diarrhea.

**B12 deficiency signals terminal ileum involvement** — B12 is absorbed exclusively in the terminal ileum; deficiency suggests disease at this location or prior ileal resection; screen annually and supplement if needed.

conditions.title: Cystic Fibrosis
conditions.overview: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder caused by mutations in the CFTR gene (cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator), resulting in dysfunctional chloride and bicarbonate transport across epithelial cell membranes. This leads to thick, viscous secretions in multiple organ systems — primarily lungs, pancreas, GI tract, and reproductive system. Progressive bronchiectasis and respiratory failure are the leading cause of morbidity and mortality. CF is the most common life-limiting autosomal recessive disease in Caucasian populations.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Genetics and Protein Expression — CF mutations (most commonly F508del) produce a misfolded CFTR protein that is ubiquitin-tagged and degraded by the proteasome before reaching the cell surface; no functional chloride channel = no water secretion = thick viscous mucus*
> *First principles: Oxygenation and Ventilation — mucus plugging creates obstructive V/Q mismatch (low V/Q) progressing to airway collapse (atelectasis = shunt); chronic infection and neutrophilic inflammation eventually destroy airway walls (bronchiectasis), producing permanent structural dead space and shunt units*

1. **CFTR dysfunction:** CFTR protein is a chloride/bicarbonate channel on apical surface of epithelial cells; mutations → absent, dysfunctional, or insufficient CFTR protein at cell surface
2. **Defective chloride and water secretion:**
   - Airway surface liquid is depleted (reduced chloride secretion + increased sodium absorption via ENaC) → dehydrated, thick mucus
   - Impaired mucociliary clearance → mucus plugging → bacterial colonization
3. **Pulmonary consequences:**
   - Thick mucus obstructs airways → chronic bacterial infection → intense neutrophilic inflammation → bronchiectasis (irreversible airway dilation and destruction)
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Pulmonary (dominant clinical feature):**
- Chronic productive cough (thick, purulent sputum)
- Recurrent respiratory infections (especially Pseudomonas)
- Progressive dyspnea, exercise intolerance
- Bronchiectasis (CT findings, hemoptysis)
- Hemoptysis (massive hemoptysis is life-threatening complication)
- Digital clubbing
- Nasal polyps (~25-50%), chronic sinusitis
- Pneumothorax (thin-walled blebs rupture)

**GI/Nutritional:**
- Steatorrhea (bulky, foul-smelling, oily stools) — pancreatic insufficiency
- Failure to thrive (children), poor weight gain, nutritional deficiency
- Meconium ileus at birth (~15-20% — may be presenting sign)
- DIOS (distal intestinal obstruction syndrome — acute in adults)
- Rectal prolapse (childhood)
- CF-related liver disease (hepatomegaly, cirrhosis)
- Fat-soluble vitamin deficiency: Vitamin A (night blindness), D (osteoporosis), E (neurologic), K (coagulopathy)

**Endocrine:**
- CF-related diabetes mellitus (CFRD) — unique type; features of both T1 and T2; no autoimmune component; insulin deficiency predominant; screen annually from age 10

**Reproductive:**
- Male infertility (absent vas deferens)
- Female subfertility (can conceive; pregnancy management needed)

### Complications
- Respiratory failure (leading cause of death)
- Massive hemoptysis (bronchial artery erosion) — annual incidence ~0.87% (1 in 115 patients/year)
- Pneumothorax — annual incidence ~0.64% (1 in 167 patients/year)
conditions.management:
### CFTR Modulator Therapy — GAME-CHANGING

| Drug | Mechanism | Eligible Mutations | Impact |
|------|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| **Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Trikafta)** | Triple combination: 2 correctors + 1 potentiator; elexacaftor acts as BOTH corrector and potentiator | F508del (at least one copy) — covers ~90% of CF patients | Revolutionary: FEV1 improvement ~14%; massive reduction in pulmonary exacerbations; improved BMI, sweat chloride, quality of life; transforming CF from fatal disease to manageable chronic condition; however, CFTR modulators only PARTIALLY restore function — chronic infections are NOT eliminated (bacterial burden decreases but persists); residual inflammation continues |
| **Ivacaftor (Kalydeco)** | CFTR potentiator (opens defective channel) | Gating mutations (G551D and others) | First CFTR modulator (2012); dramatic benefit in gating mutations |
| **Lumacaftor/ivacaftor (Orkambi)** | Corrector + potentiator | F508del homozygous | Modest benefit; largely superseded by Trikafta |
| **Tezacaftor/ivacaftor (Symdeko)** | Corrector + potentiator | F508del homozygous and select residual function | Better tolerated than Orkambi; also largely superseded by Trikafta |

### Airway Clearance (Still Essential Even on Modulators)
| Technique | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Chest physiotherapy (CPT)** | Manual percussion and postural drainage; traditional method |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - Pulmonary: Lung sounds, cough character and frequency, sputum (color, volume, consistency), SpO2, work of breathing, FEV1 trending
  - Nutritional: Weight at every visit; BMI trending; caloric intake; stool character (steatorrhea = inadequate enzyme replacement)
  - GI: Abdominal assessment (DIOS presents with RLQ pain, distension, constipation)
  - Blood glucose: Monitor for CFRD; OGTT annually from age 10
  - Psychosocial: Depression and anxiety screening (high prevalence); treatment burden assessment; body image
- **Medication administration:**
  - PERT: Must be taken with ALL meals and fat-containing snacks; swallow whole, do not crush; inadequate dosing → malabsorption
  - Nebulized medications: Order matters! Bronchodilator first → hypertonic saline → dornase alfa → airway clearance → inhaled antibiotic LAST
  - Trikafta: Take with fat-containing food (improves absorption); monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity risk); drug interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors
  - Aminoglycosides: Monitor peak/trough levels (tobramycin); assess hearing (ototoxicity) and renal function (nephrotoxicity)
- **Infection control (CRITICAL):**
pearls: **Trikafta (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor) is the most transformative therapy in CF history.** Since 2019, it has fundamentally changed the disease trajectory for ~90% of CF patients. FEV1 improves, exacerbations decrease, BMI increases, and sweat chloride normalizes. CF is transitioning from a childhood-lethal disease to a manageable chronic condition. But it is NOT a cure — patients still need airway clearance, PERT, and monitoring.

**CF patients need MORE calories and MORE fat — the opposite of standard dietary advice.** Pancreatic insufficiency causes malabsorption. CF patients should eat 120-150% of normal caloric needs with high fat content. A CF dietitian who tells a patient to "eat healthy and cut back on fat" misunderstands the disease. PERT enzymes are taken with every meal to digest the fat.

**Pseudomonas colonization is a major milestone in CF.** Initial Pseudomonas acquisition triggers aggressive eradication attempts (inhaled tobramycin). Once chronic colonization is established, eradication is nearly impossible, and chronic suppressive therapy begins. Early detection through regular sputum cultures is critical.

**CF patients must NEVER share airspace with other CF patients.** Different CF patients harbor different (and often resistant) organisms. Cross-infection with B. cepacia or resistant Pseudomonas strains can be catastrophic. This means no shared hospital rooms, no shared clinic waiting areas, and no CF support group meetings in person (online only).

**Nebulizer hygiene is a silent killer in CF.** Nebulizers that aren't properly cleaned between uses become colonized with bacteria (including Pseudomonas and NTM). Patients should clean, disinfect, and air-dry nebulizer components after each use. This is an essential — but often neglected — nursing teaching point.

conditions.title: Degenerative Mitral Regurgitation
conditions.overview: Degenerative mitral regurgitation is the backward flow of blood from the left ventricle into the left atrium during systole due to structural (primary) abnormalities of the mitral valve apparatus. The valve leaflets, chordae tendineae, or papillary muscles are intrinsically diseased — distinguishing it from functional (secondary) MR, where the valve itself is normal but the ventricle is dilated. Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) is the most common cause of primary MR in developed countries and the most common indication for mitral valve surgery.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — chronic MR creates volume overload: the regurgitant fraction recirculates, increasing total preload; LV compensates via eccentric hypertrophy and increased EDV to maintain forward SV (Frank-Starling). Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the LV accommodates MR for years via compensatory dilation; decompensation occurs when contractile reserve is exhausted and EF begins to fall.*

1. **Structural valve abnormality** (prolapse, leaflet thickening, chordal elongation/rupture) → inadequate leaflet coaptation during systole
2. **Regurgitant volume** flows backward into LA during systole → effective forward stroke volume decreases
3. **Volume overload of LA and LV:**
   - **LA dilation** → increased pulmonary venous pressure → pulmonary congestion → dyspnea
   - **LV eccentric hypertrophy** (volume overload) → LV dilation → initially maintains forward CO through Frank-Starling mechanism
4. **Chronic compensated phase:** LV dilates and increases total stroke volume to compensate; patient may be asymptomatic for years to decades despite severe MR; EF appears preserved or supranormal because blood is ejected into the low-resistance LA
   - **Annular disjunction:** Separation between the LA wall and LV free wall at the mitral valve junction; leads to abnormal annular displacement and flattening; increases stress on leaflets/chordae → progression of degeneration; associated with myocardial replacement fibrosis adjacent to papillary muscles
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Asymptomatic for years** (most common initial presentation of chronic MR)
- **Dyspnea on exertion** (earliest symptom) → orthopnea → PND
- Fatigue, exercise intolerance (decreased forward cardiac output)
- **Palpitations** (AFib, PVCs)
- **Mid-systolic click + late systolic murmur** (classic MVP finding)
  - Click moves EARLIER with standing/Valsalva (decreased preload → earlier prolapse)
  - Click moves LATER with squatting (increased preload → delayed prolapse)
- **Holosystolic murmur** at apex, radiating to axilla (severe MR)
  - Best heard with diaphragm, patient in left lateral decubitus position
  - Increases with handgrip (increased afterload → more regurgitation)
  - Decreases with Valsalva/standing (decreased preload)
- S3 (volume overload)
- Displaced PMI (LV dilation)
- **Acute MR:** Sudden severe dyspnea, pulmonary edema, hypotension; murmur may be SOFT or ABSENT (equalized pressures)

### Complications
- Heart failure (systolic dysfunction from chronic volume overload)
- Atrial fibrillation (~30-40% of severe MR patients)
- Pulmonary hypertension
- Infective endocarditis (higher risk with MVP, especially with MR)
- Stroke (AFib-related thromboembolism)
- Sudden cardiac death (rare; associated with severe bileaflet prolapse)
conditions.management:
### Surgical Indications — 2020 ACC/AHA Guidelines
| Class I (Recommended) | Details |
|----------------------|---------|
| Severe MR + symptoms (NYHA II-IV) | Surgery indicated regardless of EF |
| Severe MR + LV dysfunction (EF 30-60% or LVESD ≥ 40 mm) | Surgery indicated even if asymptomatic |
| Severe MR + undergoing other cardiac surgery | Concurrent MV repair |

| Class IIa (Reasonable) | Details |
|-----------------------|---------|
| Severe MR + asymptomatic + high likelihood of repair (>95%) + low surgical risk | Early repair at experienced center |
| Severe MR + new-onset AFib | Surgery reasonable |
| Severe MR + pulmonary HTN (PASP > 50 mmHg) | Surgery reasonable |

### Surgical Options
| Procedure | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Mitral valve REPAIR** | PREFERRED over replacement whenever feasible; preserves native valve; lower operative mortality; no lifelong anticoagulation; durability 90%+ at 10 years for degenerative MR; P2 prolapse is most repairable segment |
| **Mitral valve REPLACEMENT** | When repair not feasible (extensive calcification, severely destroyed leaflets); mechanical valve (younger patients — needs warfarin) vs. bioprosthetic (older patients — limited durability ~10-15 years) |
| **Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (MitraClip)** | For high surgical risk patients; clips anterior and posterior leaflets together; reduces MR 1-2 grades; COAPT trial showed benefit in secondary MR with HFrEF |

### Medical Management
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - Auscultation: Character and timing of murmur; new S3; crackles
  - Hemodynamics: BP, HR (tachycardia may indicate decompensation); orthostatic vitals
  - HF symptoms: Dyspnea, exercise intolerance, edema, weight gain
  - Rhythm monitoring: AFib onset is common and may trigger decompensation
  - Functional status: NYHA class; changes in exercise tolerance are clinically significant
- **Pre-operative (valve surgery):**
  - Standard cardiac surgical prep: CBC, BMP, coags, type and cross, ECG, chest X-ray
  - TEE scheduled (surgical planning)
  - Educate on ICU stay, chest tubes, sternal precautions, recovery timeline
  - Assess for modifiable risk factors (smoking cessation, dental clearance for endocarditis risk)
- **Post-operative:**
  - Hemodynamic monitoring (PA catheter, arterial line)
  - Chest tube management (output, patency)
  - Rhythm monitoring (AFib is most common post-op arrhythmia — ~30%)
  - Sternal precautions × 6-8 weeks (no pushing, pulling, lifting > 5-10 lbs)
  - Anticoagulation: Warfarin if mechanical valve (INR 2.5-3.5); aspirin if bioprosthetic or repair
  - Watch for complications: Bleeding, tamponade, stroke, infection, AKI
pearls: **"Repair, don't replace" is the mantra for degenerative MR.** Mitral valve repair has lower operative mortality, better long-term survival, no anticoagulation requirement, and better LV function preservation. A patient should be referred to a high-volume surgical center (>25 repairs/year) where repair rates exceed 95%.

**An EF of 60% in severe MR is NOT normal — it's already declining.** The volume-overloaded LV should be hyperdynamic (EF 65-70%). An EF of 60% means contractile function is already impaired. Surgical criteria use EF ≤ 60% as a trigger for this reason. Don't be falsely reassured by a "normal" EF.

**The click-murmur of MVP changes with maneuvers — this is a classic exam question.** Anything that DECREASES preload (standing, Valsalva) causes earlier prolapse → click moves earlier, murmur becomes longer. Anything that INCREASES preload (squatting) delays prolapse → click moves later, murmur shortens.

**Acute MR from chordal or papillary muscle rupture is a surgical emergency.** The non-compliant LA cannot accommodate the sudden regurgitant volume → massive pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock. The murmur may be SOFT because pressures equalize rapidly. Don't be fooled by a quiet precordium in an acutely decompensating patient.

**Watchful waiting in asymptomatic severe MR requires strict follow-up.** These patients need echo every 6-12 months and exercise testing. Any change — new symptoms, EF drop, LV dilation, AFib onset, pulmonary HTN — triggers surgical referral. Patients are notoriously poor at recognizing gradual symptom onset because they subconsciously limit activity.

conditions.title: Delirium
conditions.overview:
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and awareness with additional cognitive dysfunction (memory, orientation, language, perception) that develops over hours to days, represents a change from baseline, and is caused by an underlying medical condition, substance, or medication. It is a medical emergency signaling acute brain dysfunction and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality.

> **Delirium is NOT a psychiatric diagnosis — it is a medical emergency wearing psychiatric clothing.** The confused, agitated, hallucinating patient may look "psychiatric" but delirium has a medical cause that must be found and treated. See Acute Psychosis for the medical vs psychiatric framework.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — delirium reflects global neurotransmitter dysregulation: dopamine excess + acetylcholine deficiency + GABA imbalance, all mediated via GPCRs and ion-channel receptors. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — in critical illness, multiple homeostatic loops (circadian rhythm, metabolic, immune) fail simultaneously → the brain's integration function breaks down.*

The exact mechanism is incompletely understood but involves widespread cerebral dysfunction:
1. **Neurotransmitter imbalance:** Cholinergic deficiency (correlates with anticholinergic medication burden) + dopaminergic excess + altered serotonin, GABA, and glutamate signaling
2. **Neuroinflammation:** Systemic inflammation (sepsis, surgery) → microglial activation, blood-brain barrier disruption, cytokine-mediated neuronal injury
3. **Oxidative stress and metabolic derangement:** Impaired cerebral oxidative metabolism
4. **Altered neural network connectivity:** Disruption of attention networks (prefrontal cortex, thalamus, reticular activating system)

The "one-hit" vs. "two-hit" model: Patients with high baseline vulnerability (dementia, advanced age) need only a minor precipitant; robust patients require a major insult.

Delirium is a syndrome, not a diagnosis — the underlying CAUSE must always be identified and treated.
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Three motor subtypes:**
- **Hyperactive (25%):** Agitation, restlessness, hallucinations, pulling at lines/tubes, combativeness — the "classic" presentation that gets recognized
- **Hypoactive (50%):** Lethargy, reduced responsiveness, withdrawal, quiet confusion — **most common AND most frequently missed**; often mistaken for depression or "just tired"
- **Mixed (25%):** Fluctuates between hyperactive and hypoactive

**Core features:**
- **Acute onset** (hours to days — not gradual like dementia)
- **Fluctuating course** (lucid intervals interspersed with confusion; often worse at night — "sundowning")
- **Inattention** (cardinal feature — cannot maintain focus, easily distracted, cannot follow conversation or do serial 7s)
- **Altered level of consciousness** (from hypervigilant to obtunded)
- **Disorganized thinking** (tangential, incoherent speech, impaired reasoning)
- **Perceptual disturbances** (hallucinations — usually visual; illusions; paranoid delusions)
- **Sleep-wake cycle disruption** (daytime somnolence, nighttime agitation)
- **Emotional lability** (anxiety, fear, irritability, apathy)

**Delirium vs. Dementia vs. Depression:**
| Feature | Delirium | Dementia | Depression |
|---------|----------|----------|------------|
| Onset | Acute (hours-days) | Insidious (months-years) | Weeks-months |
| Course | Fluctuating | Progressive | Diurnal variation |
| Attention | Impaired (cardinal) | Usually preserved early | Variable |
conditions.management:
### Acute
**Identify and treat the underlying cause(s) — this is the ONLY definitive treatment:**
- Treat infection with appropriate antibiotics
- Correct metabolic derangements (Na, Ca, glucose, hydration)
- Relieve urinary retention (catheterize if needed)
- Treat fecal impaction
- Manage pain adequately (untreated pain causes delirium; but opioids also cause delirium — balance is key)
- Discontinue or reduce offending medications
- Treat hypoxia, correct oxygenation
- Address withdrawal syndromes (alcohol, benzodiazepine)

**Non-pharmacologic interventions (FIRST-LINE for ALL delirium):**
- Reorientation: clock, calendar, familiar objects, consistent caregivers; explain what's happening calmly and repeatedly
- Optimize sensory input: glasses, hearing aids at bedside
- Promote sleep-wake cycle: daytime bright light, nighttime minimal interruptions, cluster cares, avoid scheduled nighttime vitals if possible
- Early mobilization: get out of bed; remove tethers (catheters, restraints, IVs) as soon as medically appropriate
- Adequate hydration and nutrition
- Minimize restraints (restraints WORSEN delirium — use only as absolute last resort)
- Family presence at bedside when possible

**Pharmacologic management (ONLY for severe agitation posing danger to self or others):**
- **Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg PO/IV/IM** (low dose; avoid in Parkinson's, QTc prolongation, Lewy body dementia)
- **Atypical antipsychotics:** Quetiapine 12.5-50 mg, olanzapine 2.5-5 mg — may be better tolerated
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Screen every shift:** Use CAM or CAM-ICU; delirium fluctuates — a patient who is lucid at 8 AM may be delirious at 8 PM; brief cognitive screening should be part of routine assessment
- **Hypoactive delirium is the silent killer** — the quiet, withdrawn patient who "just sleeps a lot" is more likely delirious than the agitated one pulling at tubes; actively assess attention in ALL patients, not just the combative ones
- **THINK mnemonic for causes:** Toxic (medications, substances), Hypoxia (check SpO2, ABG), Infection (UTI most common in elderly, pneumonia), Nonpharmacologic interventions first, K+ and electrolytes (Na, Ca, Mg, glucose)
- **Medication review is nursing advocacy:** Review the MAR for anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, promethazine), benzodiazepines, and recent medication changes; question orders for Benadryl as sleep aid in elderly patients
- **De-escalation before medication:** Talk calmly, reorient, reduce stimulation, ensure comfort (pain, full bladder, thirst); most agitation can be managed without medications
pearls: **Delirium is NOT "normal" for hospitalized elderly patients** — "ICU psychosis" and "sundowning" are delirium with known causes that deserve investigation and treatment, not shrugging and acceptance

**The quiet patient in bed 4 who "just sleeps" needs a CAM score** — hypoactive delirium has WORSE outcomes than hyperactive because it goes unrecognized; the agitated patient gets attention, the quiet one gets ignored

**Benadryl is not a sleep aid for patients over 65** — diphenhydramine is one of the most potent anticholinergics available OTC; giving it to an elderly patient to "help them sleep" is giving them a delirium pill; question this order every time

**If a patient becomes confused after starting a new medication, the medication is guilty until proven innocent** — review the MAR, check timing of mental status changes vs. medication administration; this is the most fixable cause of delirium

**Restraints make delirium worse, not better** — a patient fighting restraints is more agitated, more confused, and at higher risk of injury than an unrestrained patient with a 1:1 sitter; advocate loudly for alternatives

conditions.title: Diabetes Insipidus
conditions.overview: > *First principles: Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — ADH (vasopressin) is the key regulator of plasma osmolality; its absence (central DI) or tubular resistance (nephrogenic DI) disrupts the osmolality set point → free water excretion without regulation → hypernatremia and hypertonicity. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the ADH feedback loop: ↑ osmolality → posterior pituitary → ADH release → aquaporin-2 insertion → water reabsorption → ↓ osmolality; DI breaks the effector arm of this loop.*

conditions.title: Diabetic Kidney Disease
conditions.overview: Diabetic kidney disease is chronic kidney disease attributable to diabetes mellitus, representing the single largest cause of end-stage kidney disease worldwide. It is defined by persistent albuminuria (ACR ≥30 mg/g) and/or sustained reduction in eGFR (<60 mL/min/1.73m2) in the setting of diabetes, after exclusion of other causes. CKD imposes the highest burden of long-term diabetes complications, and excess mortality in diabetes is largely confined to those who develop CKD.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — hyperglycemia → glomerular hyperfiltration (afferent arteriolar dilation + efferent vasoconstriction from Ang II) → elevated intraglomerular pressure → glomerulosclerosis over years. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — early diabetic nephropathy is a prolonged compensated phase (hyperfiltration maintains GFR); decompensation begins when glomerulosclerosis reduces filtration surface below the threshold for compensation.*

DKD pathophysiology involves hemodynamic, metabolic, and inflammatory pathways converging on the glomerulus and tubulointerstitium:

### Metabolic Injury
1. **Excessive glucose flux** into mesangial cells, podocytes, and tubular epithelial cells (glucose enters via insulin-independent GLUT transporters)
2. **Mitochondrial ROS overproduction** from excess glucose oxidation damages electron transport chain
3. **GAPDH inhibition** by ROS diverts glycolytic intermediates into four pathogenic signaling cascades:
   - **Polyol pathway:** Sorbitol accumulation causes osmotic stress and NADPH depletion
   - **Hexosamine pathway:** Altered protein glycosylation disrupts cellular signaling
   - **PKC pathway:** Diacylglycerol activates protein kinase C, increasing vascular permeability, inflammation, and fibrosis
   - **AGE pathway:** Advanced glycation end-products cross-link proteins, activate RAGE receptor, trigger NF-kB inflammation

### Hemodynamic Injury
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Early (hyperfiltration/microalbuminuria):**
- Often completely asymptomatic
- Elevated eGFR (>140 mL/min — hyperfiltration phase)
- Microalbuminuria detectable on screening (ACR 30-300 mg/g)
- Mild hypertension may develop

**Progressive (macroalbuminuria/declining GFR):**
- Foamy urine (heavy proteinuria)
- Worsening Hypertension and edema
- Nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia)
- Fatigue and exercise intolerance (anemia)
- Concurrent diabetic retinopathy common (~80% of T1DM with nephropathy have retinopathy)

**Advanced (ESKD):**
- All features of Chronic Kidney Disease stage 5 / uremia
- Often accompanied by advanced peripheral neuropathy, autonomic neuropathy, and severe retinopathy
- Gastroparesis complicating oral medication absorption and nutritional status

### Complications
- Accelerated cardiovascular disease (MI, stroke, peripheral arterial disease)
- Heart Failure (combined diabetic cardiomyopathy and volume overload)
- Hypoglycemia risk increases as GFR declines (reduced renal gluconeogenesis and insulin clearance)
- Diabetic foot ulcers with superimposed CKD impaired healing
- Severe anemia (EPO deficiency + functional iron deficiency)
- Hyperkalemia from RAAS inhibition plus diabetic hypoaldosteronism (type IV RTA)
- Progression to ESKD requiring dialysis or transplant
- Calciphylaxis (CKD-MBD with vascular calcification)
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Hypoglycemia management:** As GFR declines, insulin and sulfonylurea doses must be reduced. The kidney is responsible for ~30% of insulin clearance — patients on insulin who develop progressive CKD will have increasingly frequent hypoglycemia if doses are not proactively reduced.
- **Hyperkalemia:** Common from diabetic hypoaldosteronism compounded by RAAS inhibitors. Manage acutely; may require chronic patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate to maintain RAAS blockade.
- **AKI prevention:** DKD patients are extremely vulnerable to Acute Kidney Injury from volume depletion, contrast exposure, or nephrotoxins. Hold SGLT2 inhibitors and metformin during acute illness ("sick day rules").

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Glycemic control:** HbA1c target ~7% for most patients; individualize (7.5-8% for elderly, frailty, hypoglycemia unawareness). SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists preferred as they provide both glycemic control and renoprotection.
- **Blood pressure:** Target <130/80 mmHg (some guidelines <120 systolic for high-risk). ACE Inhibitors or ARBs are mandatory first-line in albuminuric DKD.
- **RAAS blockade:** ACE Inhibitors or ARBs — reduce proteinuria 30-40% and slow GFR decline independent of BP lowering. Titrate to maximum tolerated dose. Never combine ACE + ARB (ONTARGET: no benefit, increased harm).
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Glucose monitoring requires CKD-adjusted interpretation.** HbA1c becomes unreliable in advanced CKD (stages 4-5) because of shortened RBC lifespan from uremia and EPO therapy. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) or fructosamine levels provide more accurate glycemic assessment.
- **Hypoglycemia risk escalates as kidney function declines.** The kidney clears ~30% of circulating insulin. Patients on insulin with progressive CKD will have increasingly prolonged insulin action. Watch for hypoglycemia, especially overnight. Educate patients that "good" blood sugars on the same insulin dose may actually signal worsening kidney function.
- **"Sick day rules" are critical patient education.** Teach patients to hold SGLT2 inhibitors, metformin, ACE Inhibitors/ARBs, and Diuretics during acute illness with vomiting, diarrhea, or poor oral intake. Provide a written sick-day card.
- **Foot care is doubly important.** DKD patients have both diabetic neuropathy reducing sensation and CKD-related impaired wound healing. Perform foot assessments every shift for inpatients. A small ulcer in this population can become limb-threatening rapidly.
pearls: **Unexplained hypoglycemia in a diabetic patient may be the first sign of progressive DKD** — the kidney clears 30% of insulin and performs significant gluconeogenesis. When kidney function drops, insulin hangs around longer and glucose production falls. If a well-controlled diabetic starts having lows on a stable insulin dose, check that eGFR.

**The absence of diabetic retinopathy in a T1DM patient with kidney disease should make you question the diagnosis of DKD** — >80% of T1DM patients with true diabetic nephropathy have concurrent retinopathy. No retinopathy means consider kidney biopsy to rule out other diagnoses.

**SGLT2 inhibitors are now indicated for CKD even without diabetes** — but in DKD patients they provide triple benefit: renoprotection, cardioprotection, and glycemic control. They are arguably the most important drug class to emerge for DKD since ACE inhibitors in the 1990s.

**The "four pillars" of DKD therapy can stack** — ACE/ARB + SGLT2 inhibitor + GLP-1 RA + finerenone together provide additive renal and cardiovascular protection. The nursing challenge is managing the potassium and volume effects of this polypharmacy.

**Sick-day medication management prevents most DKD-related acute hospitalizations** — a laminated wallet card listing which medications to hold (metformin, SGLT2i, ACE/ARB, diuretics) during vomiting or diarrhea is one of the highest-yield patient education interventions you can provide.

conditions.title: Diabetic Neuropathy
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability — chronic hyperglycemia depletes myoinositol → reduced Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity → impaired ion gradient maintenance → disrupted axonal conduction velocity and eventually axonal degeneration; longer axons are disproportionately vulnerable*
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — advanced glycation end products (AGEs) bind RAGE receptors → NF-κB activation → pro-inflammatory cytokine release → neuroinflammation that compounds direct metabolic nerve injury*

Multiple interconnected pathways converge to cause nerve injury. This is a **metabolic neuropathy**, not primarily vascular (though vascular damage contributes):

### 1. Polyol Pathway (Sorbitol Accumulation)
- In hyperglycemia, excess glucose is shunted into the **polyol pathway** via **aldose reductase**
- Glucose → **sorbitol** (by aldose reductase) → fructose (by sorbitol dehydrogenase)
- **Sorbitol cannot cross cell membranes** → accumulates intracellularly
- Results:
  - **Osmotic stress** — sorbitol draws water into cells → Schwann cell swelling and injury
  - **NAD⁺/NADH imbalance** — depletes NADPH (needed for glutathione regeneration → oxidative stress)
  - **Myoinositol depletion** — sorbitol competes with myoinositol uptake → decreased Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase activity → impaired nerve conduction

### 2. Advanced Glycation End Products (AGEs)
- Non-enzymatic glycation of proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids by chronic glucose exposure
conditions.keyFeatures:
### DSPN — The Classic Presentation
**Positive symptoms** (from irritated/dying nerves):
- Burning, tingling, "pins and needles" — often worse at night
- Electric shock sensations
- Allodynia — pain from normally non-painful stimuli (sheet on feet)
- Hyperalgesia — increased pain from painful stimuli

**Negative symptoms** (from dead nerves):
- Numbness — "walking on cotton" or "feet feel dead"
- Loss of temperature sensation
- Loss of proprioception → unsteady gait (sensory ataxia)
- Loss of protective sensation → **unnoticed injuries, burns, ulcers**

**Motor symptoms** (late):
- Intrinsic foot muscle atrophy → toe deformities (claw toes, hammer toes)
- Altered foot biomechanics → abnormal pressure distribution → callus formation → ulceration
- Ankle dorsiflexor weakness → foot drop (late and uncommon)

### Diabetic Autonomic Neuropathy
| System | Manifestations |
|---|---|
| **Cardiovascular** | Resting tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, exercise intolerance, silent MI (no chest pain) |
| **GI** | Gastroparesis (nausea, early satiety, erratic glucose), constipation, diarrhea |
| **Genitourinary** | Neurogenic bladder (incomplete emptying, UTIs), erectile dysfunction |
| **Sudomotor** | Anhidrosis in feet (dry, cracked skin → infection risk), compensatory upper body sweating |
| **Hypoglycemia unawareness** | Loss of sympathetic warning signs (tremor, sweating, palpitations) → severe hypoglycemia without prodrome |

> [!warning]
conditions.management:
### Glucose Control — The Foundation
- **Tight glycemic control slows progression** — HbA1c <7% reduces neuropathy risk by 60% (DCCT/EDIC)
- In Type 2 DM, benefit is less dramatic but still significant
- **Cannot reverse established nerve damage** — prevention and slowing progression are the goals
- Rapid glycemic correction can paradoxically cause **treatment-induced neuropathy** (acute painful neuropathy from rapid HbA1c reduction) — avoid dropping HbA1c more than 2% in 3 months

### Neuropathic Pain Management
| Tier | Drug | Dose | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-line** | **Duloxetine** (Cymbalta) | 60 mg PO daily | SNRI; FDA-approved for DPN; also treats comorbid depression |
| **First-line** | **Pregabalin** (Lyrica) | 150-300 mg/day divided | Alpha-2-delta ligand; FDA-approved; sedation, weight gain, abuse potential (Schedule V) |
| **First-line** | **Gabapentin** | 300-1200 mg TID | Similar mechanism to pregabalin; less expensive; less evidence for DPN specifically |
| **Second-line** | **Amitriptyline/nortriptyline** | 25-75 mg nightly | TCA; effective but anticholinergic side effects; caution in elderly |
| **Second-line** | **Venlafaxine** | 150-225 mg daily | SNRI; less evidence than duloxetine |
| **Topical** | **Capsaicin 8% patch** (Qutenza) | Applied in clinic | Depletes substance P; initial burning then relief; repeated q3 months |
| **Topical** | **Lidocaine 5% patch** | Applied to painful area | Local anesthetic; minimal systemic absorption |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Assessment
- **Remove shoes and socks on EVERY diabetic patient** — regardless of chief complaint
- **Monofilament testing** — know how to perform it:
  1. Apply 10-g monofilament perpendicular to skin until it bends (~10 g of force)
  2. Hold for ~1 second, then release
  3. Test sites: great toe plantar, 1st/3rd/5th metatarsal heads plantar, medial and lateral midfoot
  4. Patient says "yes" when they feel it (eyes closed)
  5. ≥1 insensate site = loss of protective sensation
- **Skin assessment** — dryness, cracking, callus formation, deformity, color, temperature, hair distribution
- **Pulse assessment** — dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial bilaterally (PAD co-assessment)
- **Footwear assessment** — inspect the patient's shoes for wear patterns, fit, foreign objects

### Patient Education
- **This won't get better, but it can be managed** — set realistic expectations; pain medications control symptoms, they don't restore nerve function
- **Your feet are your most important daily assessment** — make it a habit; catch problems early
pearls: **Neuropathy doesn't wait for a diabetes diagnosis** — pre-diabetes (impaired glucose tolerance) causes small fiber neuropathy; your PAD patient with "pre-diabetes" and reduced foot sensation is a textbook example
**Remove shoes on EVERY diabetic in the ED** — you will find things the patient doesn't know about; this takes 10 seconds and can prevent a limb loss
**Pain and numbness coexist** — patients are confused when they have burning pain AND can't feel the monofilament; explain that different nerve fiber types are at different stages of damage
**Silent MI kills diabetics** — cardiac autonomic neuropathy eliminates chest pain as a warning; an MI may present as dyspnea, nausea, or unexplained hyperglycemia; keep your index of suspicion high

**Gastroparesis makes glucose management impossible** — erratic gastric emptying means food absorption is unpredictable; insulin timing becomes a nightmare; this is why some diabetics have "brittle" glucose control

conditions.title: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
conditions.overview: Dilated cardiomyopathy is a myocardial disease characterized by ventricular dilation and impaired systolic function (reduced LVEF) in the absence of abnormal loading conditions (hypertension, valvular disease) or coronary artery disease sufficient to cause global systolic dysfunction. The ventricle is enlarged, thin-walled, and contracts poorly. DCM is the most common cardiomyopathy and the leading indication for heart transplantation.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — DCM dilates the ventricle attempting to maintain SV by increasing preload (moving along the Starling curve); but the flattened, shifted curve means higher filling pressures produce little additional output. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — RAAS and SNS activation increase preload and HR to compensate for reduced contractility; these compensatory loops become maladaptive, driving further dilation and remodeling.*

1. **Myocardial injury** (toxic, infectious, genetic, autoimmune) → myocyte death and/or dysfunction
2. **Ventricular dilation:** Eccentric hypertrophy → increased chamber volume → wall thinning (Laplace's law: wall stress ↑ as radius ↑ and thickness ↓)
3. **Impaired contractility:** Global hypokinesis → reduced LVEF → decreased cardiac output
4. **Neurohormonal activation:** Same maladaptive cascade as Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (RAAS, SNS, ADH → fluid retention, vasoconstriction, further remodeling)
5. **Functional mitral regurgitation:** Annular dilation from LV enlargement → mitral valve leaflets don't coapt → MR → further volume overload (volume begets volume)
6. **Conduction abnormalities:** Fibrosis and dilation disrupt conduction → LBBB (common), AFib, VT/VF (sudden cardiac death risk)
7. **Intracardiac thrombus:** Stasis in dilated, poorly contracting ventricle → LV thrombus → systemic embolism (Ischemic Stroke)
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- Same as Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction:
  - Dyspnea on exertion → orthopnea → PND
  - Fatigue, exercise intolerance
  - Peripheral edema, JVD, weight gain
  - S3 gallop (volume overload)
  - Displaced PMI (left and inferior due to LV dilation)
  - Murmur of functional mitral regurgitation (holosystolic at apex)
- **Palpitations** (AFib, PVCs, VT)
- **Syncope** (arrhythmia or low CO)
- May be **asymptomatic** initially (discovered incidentally or on family screening)

### Complications
- Progressive heart failure → refractory (Stage D)
- Sudden cardiac death (VT/VF) — accounts for ~30% of DCM deaths
- Atrial fibrillation (~25-30%)
- Thromboembolism (stroke, systemic embolism)
- Functional mitral regurgitation (worsens prognosis)
conditions.management:
### Pharmacologic — Same as HFrEF GDMT
Full GDMT per Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction:
- **ARNI** (sacubitril/valsartan) or ACE-I/ARB
- **Evidence-based beta-blocker** (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol)
- **MRA** (spironolactone or eplerenone)
- **SGLT2 inhibitor** (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin)
- **Diuretics** for congestion
- **Hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate** if ACE/ARB/ARNI contraindicated

### Treat Reversible Causes
| Cause | Intervention |
|-------|-------------|
| Alcohol | Complete abstinence → EF may improve 10-15% over 6 months |
| Tachycardia-mediated | Rate or rhythm control; EF often normalizes within 3-6 months |
| Peripartum | GDMT + bromocriptine (may help in some protocols); ~50% recover EF |
| Thyroid disease | Correct thyroid function |
| Hemochromatosis | Phlebotomy, chelation |
| Chemotherapy-related | Cardioprotection (dexrazoxane); hold cardiotoxic agent if possible |

### Device Therapy
- **ICD:** EF ≤ 35% despite ≥ 3 months optimized GDMT; NYHA II-III; prevents sudden cardiac death
  - **Important:** Must wait ≥ 3-9 months on GDMT before ICD implant in new-onset DCM — many patients improve EF above 35% threshold with treatment
- **CRT/CRT-D:** EF ≤ 35% + LBBB + QRS ≥ 150 ms; NYHA II-IV

### Advanced Therapies
- LVAD (bridge to transplant or destination therapy)
- Heart transplant (DCM is #1 indication)
- Palliative care for Stage D refractory HF

### Family Screening
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - Same as HFrEF: Daily weights, I&O, lung sounds, JVD, edema, dyspnea, functional status
  - Monitor for arrhythmias (continuous telemetry if inpatient)
  - Assess for signs of thromboembolism (stroke symptoms, limb ischemia)
  - Medication adherence (most important modifiable factor for outcomes)
  - Screen for alcohol use (AUDIT-C); substance use history
- **ICD management:**
  - Educate patient on device function, what a shock feels like, when to call (multiple shocks = VT storm → 911)
  - MedicAlert bracelet; avoid strong magnets/magnetic fields
  - Regular device interrogation (in-person or remote monitoring)
- **Patient teaching:**
  - Same HF self-care as HFrEF (daily weights, sodium/fluid restriction, medications)
  - If alcohol-related: Complete abstinence — "your heart can partially recover if you stop"
  - Genetic implications: Family screening discussion; genetic counseling referral
  - Activity: Avoid extreme exertion and competitive sports (especially LMNA mutations — high arrhythmia risk); cardiac rehab for supervised exercise
pearls: **DCM is the #1 indication for heart transplant.** Unlike ischemic cardiomyopathy, DCM patients are often younger and may have fewer comorbidities, making them better transplant candidates.

**Always rule out CAD before diagnosing DCM.** Ischemic cardiomyopathy (from diffuse CAD) looks identical on echo. Coronary angiography or CCTA is essential. The distinction matters because ischemic CMP may benefit from revascularization.

**"Wait and optimize" before ICD in new-onset DCM.** Many patients with newly diagnosed DCM improve their EF significantly with GDMT (3-9 months). Implanting an ICD too early means some patients get an unnecessary device. The exception is LMNA mutations — high sudden death risk warrants earlier ICD.

**Alcoholic cardiomyopathy is one of the most reversible forms.** With complete abstinence + GDMT, EF can improve by 10-15% within 6 months. Some patients normalize completely. This is the most compelling argument for sobriety you can give a patient.

**LMNA mutations are the most dangerous genetic DCM.** They carry high risk for conduction disease (AV block → pacemaker) AND ventricular arrhythmias (sudden death → ICD at lower EF thresholds). Genetic testing changes management.

conditions.title: Distributive Shock
conditions.overview: > *First principles: Hemodynamics — distributive shock = massively reduced SVR (vasodilation/maldistribution); CO is preserved or elevated, but BP crashes because SVR collapses. Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — in septic distributive shock, DO2 may be normal or high yet tissues are starved; microvascular shunting and mitochondrial dysfunction prevent extraction — ScvO2 paradoxically rises while lactate climbs.*
conditions.management:
### Step 1: Volume Resuscitation
- **Crystalloid first** — 30 mL/kg in septic shock (per Surviving Sepsis Campaign), titrate in others
- **Balanced crystalloid** (LR/Plasmalyte) preferred over NS
- **Reassess after each bolus** — lung auscultation, JVD, ScvO2, lactate, urine output
- **NOT unlimited** — excessive fluid → pulmonary edema, abdominal compartment syndrome, tissue edema → worsened oxygen diffusion

### Step 2: Vasopressors (if MAP <65 despite volume)
- **Norepinephrine — first line** for all distributive shock subtypes
  - Alpha-1 dominant → restores SVR
  - Some beta-1 → augments CO
  - Dose: 0.1-2 mcg/kg/min (typical starting dose 5-10 mcg/min)
- **Vasopressin** — second-line adjunct (0.03-0.04 units/min). V1 receptor agonist → vasoconstriction through a NON-catecholamine pathway. Useful when norepinephrine requirements are high (catecholamine-resistant vasodilation)
- **Epinephrine** — third-line or for anaphylactic shock specifically
- **Phenylephrine** — pure alpha-1. Consider for neurogenic shock or when tachyarrhythmias limit norepinephrine use
- **Angiotensin II (Giapreza)** — newest agent for refractory vasodilatory shock. ATHOS-3 trial showed benefit when norepinephrine + vasopressin insufficient

### Step 3: Treat the Underlying Cause
- **Sepsis:** Antibiotics within 1 hour, source control (drainage, debridement, device removal). See Sepsis
- **Anaphylaxis:** Epinephrine, remove trigger
- **Neurogenic:** MAP ≥85, spinal cord management
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Warm skin + hypotension = think distributive.** This is the ONE shock type where the patient feels warm and flushed (vasodilation) rather than cold and clammy (vasoconstriction). As shock progresses, skin may become cool/mottled as vasodilatory capacity exhausts
- **Vasopressor titration is a nursing skill.** You'll titrate norepinephrine more than any other drug in the ICU. Know your facility's titration protocol, and titrate to MAP goal (typically ≥65)
- **Central line for vasopressors when possible** — but do NOT delay norepinephrine for central access. Peripheral norepinephrine via large-bore proximal IV (antecubital or above) is safe for initial resuscitation while central line is being placed
- **Arterial line for accuracy** — non-invasive BP cuffs are unreliable in shock. Push for an arterial line early
- **Trending lactate** — q2-4h during active resuscitation. Lactate clearance >10% per 2 hours = good response. Persistently elevated or rising lactate = failing resuscitation → escalate
pearls: **Distributive shock is the only shock type that presents WARM.** Cold extremities in shock = vasoconstriction (cardiogenic, hypovolemic, or late distributive). Warm, flushed skin with hypotension = vasodilation = distributive. This 5-second assessment narrows your differential immediately.

**Vasopressors don't fix distributive shock — they buy time.** Norepinephrine squeezes the dilated vessels to maintain MAP, but the underlying vasodilation continues until you treat the CAUSE (antibiotics for sepsis, epi for anaphylaxis, steroids for adrenal crisis). Vasopressors without source control = a losing battle.

**ScvO2 >70% + rising lactate = microvascular failure (sepsis).** The oxygen is being delivered globally but can't reach cells locally. This is the pathophysiological signature of septic shock and why it's so lethal — even adequate global DO2 doesn't help.

**Neurogenic shock = the one with bradycardia.** If a trauma patient is hypotensive AND bradycardic → spinal cord injury until proven otherwise. But ALWAYS rule out hemorrhage concurrently — hemorrhagic shock is more common and more immediately dangerous.

**Think adrenal crisis in the steroid-dependent patient who crashes.** Any patient on chronic steroids (>2 weeks of prednisone ≥5-10 mg/day) has a suppressed HPA axis. Surgery, infection, or illness without stress-dose steroids → vasopressor-resistant hypotension. Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV can be diagnostic and therapeutic.

conditions.title: Drug-Induced Liver Injury
conditions.overview: Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) is unexpected harm that drugs or other xenobiotics cause to the liver. It encompasses two major types: intrinsic DILI (dose-related, predictable, typically occurs in all exposed individuals above a threshold dose) and idiosyncratic DILI (unpredictable, host-dependent, occurring in susceptible individuals regardless of dose).
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — hepatic Phase I metabolism (CYP450 enzymes) produces reactive electrophilic intermediates; acetaminophen → NAPQI via CYP2E1 is the prototype; at therapeutic doses, Phase II conjugation (glutathione, glucuronidation, sulfation) detoxifies NAPQI safely; at toxic doses, Phase II enzymes saturate and glutathione is depleted → NAPQI accumulates → binds hepatocyte proteins → mitochondrial dysfunction → cell death*
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — immune-mediated (idiosyncratic) DILI: reactive drug metabolites covalently bind hepatic proteins, forming drug-protein adducts that act as neoantigens (hapten formation); dendritic cells present these to T cells → CD8+ cytotoxic T cell activation → hepatocyte destruction; the immune mechanism explains the delayed onset (weeks to months) and low incidence (requires immune sensitization)*

DILI results from multiple mechanisms operating individually or in combination:
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Presentation patterns** vary by injury type:
- **Hepatocellular pattern**: Jaundice, malaise, nausea, right upper quadrant pain, dark urine, elevated aminotransferases
- **Cholestatic pattern**: Pruritus, jaundice, pale stools, elevated Alkaline Phosphatase and Bilirubin
- **Mixed pattern**: Features of both hepatocellular and cholestatic injury

**Latency period**: Time from drug initiation to symptom onset varies from days to months depending on mechanism (immune-mediated typically 1-3 months)

**Systemic features**: Fever, rash, eosinophilia (suggest immune-mediated DILI)

### Complications

- **Acute liver failure** (ALF): Occurs in 10% of patients meeting Hy's Law criteria (ALT >3× ULN + bilirubin >2× ULN without other cause); manifests as hepatic encephalopathy, coagulopathy, multi-organ failure
- **Chronic DILI**: Persistent injury >6 months; may progress to cirrhosis
- **Vanishing bile duct syndrome**: Progressive cholestasis from immune-mediated bile duct destruction
- **Death**: Approximately 10% mortality in severe DILI; 80% mortality in untreated acetaminophen-induced ALF
conditions.management:
### Acute

**Immediate drug withdrawal**: Discontinue suspected causative agent(s) promptly

**Acetaminophen-specific treatment**:
- **N-Acetylcysteine (NAC)**: Most effective within 8 hours of ingestion but beneficial up to 72 hours; replenishes glutathione stores; dosing: 140 mg/kg loading dose, then 70 mg/kg every 4 hours × 17 doses (oral) or IV protocol
- Monitor acetaminophen level, INR, creatinine, liver enzymes
- Activated charcoal if within 1-2 hours of ingestion

**Supportive care for ALF**:
- ICU admission for encephalopathy, coagulopathy, or hemodynamic instability
- Correct coagulopathy cautiously (only for active bleeding or pre-procedure; INR is prognostic marker)
- Treat cerebral edema (mannitol, hypertonic saline)
- Manage hypoglycemia
- Monitor for and treat infections
- Early transfer to transplant center

**Immune checkpoint inhibitor DILI**: Corticosteroids (prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day) for moderate to severe cases; mycophenolate for steroid-refractory cases

### Chronic/Maintenance

**Post-acute monitoring**: Serial liver function tests until normalization or plateau

**Avoid re-exposure**: Patient education about causative agent and cross-reactive drugs

**Manage chronic sequelae**: If chronic DILI or cirrhosis develops, manage as Chronic Liver Disease or Cirrhosis

### Pharmacology

- **N-Acetylcysteine**: Glutathione precursor; IV or oral administration for acetaminophen toxicity
conditions.nursingPriorities:
**Medication reconciliation is paramount**: Obtain comprehensive medication history including over-the-counter drugs, herbal products, dietary supplements, and vitamins at every encounter

**Acetaminophen safety education**:
- Maximum daily dose 4 grams (lower in liver disease, alcohol use, malnutrition)
- Identify hidden sources in combination products (cough/cold remedies, prescription opioid combinations)
- Counsel patients to read labels and avoid duplicate dosing

**NAC administration**:
- Oral: Dilute in cola or juice to mask sulfur odor; administer via NG tube if patient cannot tolerate oral; monitor for vomiting (repeat dose if within 1 hour)
- IV: Monitor for anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm — reduce rate or temporarily stop infusion)
- Continue full protocol even if acetaminophen level undetectable

**Monitor for hepatic encephalopathy**: Altered mental status, asterixis, confusion — document grade (West Haven criteria)

**Transplant evaluation criteria**: Familiarize with King's College Criteria (acetaminophen: pH <7.3 or INR >6.5 + creatinine >3.4 + grade III-IV encephalopathy; non-acetaminophen: INR >6.5 or 3 of 5 criteria)
pearls: **Ask about herbal and dietary supplements explicitly — patients often don't consider these "real drugs" and won't mention them unless specifically asked.** Supplement-induced DILI is increasing and many products are unregulated with variable composition.

**Acetaminophen toxicity is often "therapeutic misadventure" not intentional overdose.** The patient taking prescription hydrocodone-acetaminophen for back pain who adds OTC acetaminophen for headache and a nighttime cold remedy (also containing acetaminophen) can easily exceed 4g/day without realizing it.

**Tachycardia and altered mental status in a DILI patient signal potential acute liver failure — this is an ICU transfer, not a floor admission.** Early recognition and transplant center evaluation save lives; don't wait for profound coagulopathy or grade III encephalopathy.

**Complete the full NAC protocol even if the patient feels better or acetaminophen level is undetectable.** Hepatotoxicity peaks at 72-96 hours post-ingestion, well after acetaminophen is cleared from serum. NAC works through multiple mechanisms beyond glutathione repletion.

**The absence of jaundice does not rule out severe DILI.** Many patients with dangerous ALT elevations are non-icteric; conversely, isolated hyperbilirubinemia without transaminitis suggests a different etiology.

conditions.title: Dysrhythmias
conditions.overview:
Dysrhythmias are abnormalities of cardiac electrical conduction — the heart's rhythm is too fast, too slow, irregular, or originating from the wrong location. They range from benign incidental findings to immediately lethal cardiac arrest rhythms.

Every dysrhythmia is an Electricity and Excitability problem: either the impulse is **forming abnormally** (automaticity), **traveling abnormally** (re-entry or conduction block), or the **substrate is electrically unstable** (ischemia, electrolyte imbalance).

> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

**Related notes:** Electricity and Excitability · Antiarrhythmics · Cardiac Arrest · Cardiovascular System

---
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Know your monitor.** Artifact mimics VF. Lead disconnection mimics asystole. Always correlate the rhythm on the monitor with the patient in front of you. Check a pulse. Check lead connections
- **12-lead first in stable patients.** A rhythm strip gives you rate and regularity. A 12-lead gives you morphology, axis, intervals (QTc!), ST changes, and diagnostic precision. Get one early
- **Measure the QTc before giving antiarrhythmics.** Amiodarone, procainamide, sotalol, and many non-cardiac drugs prolong QT. If the QTc is already prolonged → risk of Torsades. Magnesium is your friend
- **Pacing pads on early for bradycardia.** Don't wait for the patient to crash. If you see Mobitz II or 3rd degree block → pads on, test capture, have sedation ready
- **Transcutaneous pacing is painful.** Patients need analgesia/sedation (fentanyl + midazolam, or ketamine). Start with minimum milliamps above capture threshold
- **Document rhythm strips.** Print/save the rhythm before, during, and after any intervention. Include the patient's clinical status with each strip. This is medicolegal gold
pearls: **Stable wide-complex tachycardia = VT until proven otherwise.** It's safer to treat SVT with aberrancy as VT (amiodarone won't hurt them) than to treat VT as SVT (adenosine, diltiazem could kill them).
**"Irregularly irregular" = AFib.** No other common rhythm produces this pattern. You can say it before the 12-lead prints.
**A regular narrow-complex tachycardia at exactly 150 bpm = atrial flutter with 2:1 block.** It's a reflex at this point. Atrial rate 300 ÷ 2 = 150.

**Peaked T waves on the monitor → check potassium immediately.** Don't wait for routine labs. [[Hyperkalemia]] can go from peaked T waves to asystole in minutes. Get calcium to the bedside while you wait for the result.

**Torsades gets magnesium, not amiodarone.** This distinction saves lives. Check the QTc — if it's prolonged and the rhythm is polymorphic VT → Torsades → magnesium 2g IV. Amiodarone will make it WORSE.

conditions.title: Dystonia
conditions.overview: A movement disorder characterized by sustained or intermittent muscle contractions causing abnormal, often repetitive postures and/or movements. Dystonic movements are typically patterned (same muscles involved each time), may be twisting, and are often initiated or worsened by voluntary action. Dystonia can range from focal (one body part) to generalized, and from isolated to combined with other movement abnormalities.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — dystonia reflects abnormal basal ganglia circuit activity; dopamine D1/D2 receptor imbalance in the direct/indirect pathways drives excessive and involuntary co-contraction of agonist and antagonist muscles. Electricity and Excitability — the motor symptom IS an action potential problem: excessive, unsuppressed motor neuron firing produces the sustained muscle contractions.*

Dystonia results from dysfunction in the sensorimotor circuits involving the basal ganglia, cerebellum, and cortex:

1. **Basal ganglia circuit dysfunction:** Abnormal output from the globus pallidus internus (GPi) → disrupted inhibition of thalamocortical projections → excessive and inappropriate muscle activation
2. **Loss of surround inhibition:** Normal movement requires activation of agonist muscles AND inhibition of surrounding muscles; in dystonia, this surround inhibition fails → co-contraction of agonists and antagonists
3. **Abnormal sensorimotor integration:** Altered processing of sensory feedback → dysfunctional motor planning
4. **Maladaptive plasticity:** Excessive cortical plasticity in motor and sensory cortex → aberrant motor programs

**"Geste antagoniste" (sensory trick):** Many patients discover that light touch to the affected area reduces dystonic posturing (e.g., touching the chin reduces cervical dystonia); this phenomenon reflects the sensory processing abnormality and is highly suggestive of dystonia.
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Common focal dystonias:**
- **Cervical dystonia (torticollis):** Most common adult focal dystonia; involuntary head turning/tilting/flexion/extension; painful in ~75%; may be rotational (most common), laterocollis, anterocollis, or retrocollis
- **Blepharospasm:** Involuntary bilateral eyelid closure; can be functionally blinding; triggered by bright light, stress, reading
- **Writer's cramp (focal hand dystonia):** Task-specific; abnormal posturing during writing; may spread to other manual tasks
- **Laryngeal dystonia (spasmodic dysphonia):** Strained/strangled voice (adductor type — most common) or breathy voice (abductor type)
- **Oromandibular dystonia:** Involuntary jaw opening, closing, or lateral deviation; interferes with eating and speaking

**Generalized dystonia:**
- Begins in childhood, typically in a leg or foot → spreads to other limbs and trunk
- DYT1 (TOR1A mutation): Most common inherited generalized dystonia; autosomal dominant with ~30% penetrance
- Progressive disability if untreated; cognition is preserved

**Tardive dystonia:**
- Caused by chronic exposure to dopamine-blocking agents (Antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)
- May persist long after offending medication is discontinued
- Can be focal or generalized; often affects trunk and neck

**Acute dystonic reaction:**
- Sudden onset of sustained muscle contraction (usually head/neck — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, trismus, opisthotonos)
conditions.management:
### Acute
**Acute dystonic reaction (medication-induced) — TREAT IMMEDIATELY:**
- **IV diphenhydramine 25-50 mg** — response within 5-15 minutes; first-line
- **OR IV/IM benztropine 1-2 mg** — anticholinergic; equally effective
- Relief is typically rapid and dramatic
- Continue oral anticholinergic (benztropine or diphenhydramine) for 48-72 hours to prevent recurrence
- **Discontinue the causative dopamine-blocking agent** (or switch to lower-risk alternative)
- Secure airway if laryngeal/pharyngeal involvement (rare but life-threatening)

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Focal dystonias — Botulinum toxin injections (FIRST-LINE):**
- OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) or abobotulinumtoxinA (Dysport): Injected directly into affected muscles
- Cervical dystonia: Injections every 12-16 weeks; ~85-90% response rate; most patients achieve significant pain relief and improved posture
- Blepharospasm: Periocular injections every 12 weeks; ~90% response rate
- Laryngeal dystonia: Injected into vocalis muscles (EMG/laryngoscopy-guided)
- Writer's cramp: Targeted forearm/hand muscle injection (challenging — requires expert guidance)
- Side effects: Local weakness (dysphagia with cervical injections — most concerning), bruising; antibody formation (resistance) in 3-5% with long-term use

**Oral medications (adjunctive or when botulinum toxin insufficient):**
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Acute dystonic reactions — recognize and act fast:**
  - Most common in young patients started on antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or prochlorperazine
  - Typically occurs within 24-48 hours of starting the medication
  - Presentation: sudden neck twisting (torticollis), jaw clenching (trismus), eyes rolling upward (oculogyric crisis), arching backward (opisthotonos)
  - **This is frightening for the patient** — they are fully conscious but cannot control their muscles; reassure while treating
  - Administer IV diphenhydramine 25-50 mg or benztropine 1-2 mg STAT; monitor airway
  - Report to prescriber; document reaction prominently in the chart
- **Medication monitoring for chronic patients:**
  - Anticholinergics (trihexyphenidyl): Monitor for cognitive effects (especially in elderly — confusion, hallucinations), urinary retention, constipation; falls risk from sedation
  - Baclofen: Never abruptly discontinue (withdrawal can cause seizures, hallucinations, rebound spasticity); educate patient
  - Benzodiazepines: Fall risk, sedation, cognitive effects; monitor for tolerance
- **Botulinum toxin injection support:**
pearls: **An oculogyric crisis on the psych unit is an acute dystonic reaction until proven otherwise** — if a patient on haloperidol or risperidone suddenly has their eyes locked upward with head tilting back, don't document "patient unresponsive" and wait; give diphenhydramine IV NOW — the relief is dramatic and nearly instantaneous

**Every child with new-onset dystonia deserves a levodopa trial** — dopa-responsive dystonia (DRD/Segawa disease) is one of the most gratifying diagnoses in neurology: a child who can barely walk transforms to normal function with a small dose of levodopa; missing this is unforgivable
**Always check for Wilson disease** — ceruloplasmin, 24h urine copper, slit-lamp exam; it's rare but treatable, and missing it means the patient progresses to irreversible liver and brain damage

**The sensory trick is your diagnostic clue** — when a patient with neck dystonia lightly touches their chin and the posturing improves, that's a geste antagoniste; this is nearly pathognomonic for dystonia and helps distinguish it from other movement disorders

**Metoclopramide (Reglan) is the sneaky cause of tardive dystonia** — it's prescribed casually for nausea and gastroparesis, but it's a potent dopamine blocker; always ask about current or past use in any patient with dystonia; the FDA limits its use to 12 weeks for this reason

conditions.title: Ectopic Pregnancy
conditions.overview:
An ectopic pregnancy is implantation of a fertilized ovum OUTSIDE the uterine cavity. ~97% occur in the fallopian tube (tubal ectopic). As the embryo grows in a space that cannot accommodate it, the tube stretches → pain → eventual rupture → hemorrhage → hemorrhagic shock → death if untreated.

Ectopic pregnancy is the **leading cause of maternal death in the first trimester** and the most common cause of pregnancy-related death in the first 20 weeks.

**Related notes:** Hypovolemic Shock · Reproductive System

---
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — fallopian tube cannot accommodate trophoblast growth; rupture → hemorrhage → hemodynamic decompensation*
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — massive hemoperitoneum reduces preload; hypovolemic shock mechanism*
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Classic Triad (present in <50%)
1. **Abdominal/pelvic pain** (most common — 95%)
2. **Vaginal bleeding** (60-80% — typically light, irregular, dark/"prune juice")
3. **Amenorrhea** (missed period — 75%)

### Unruptured Ectopic
- Unilateral lower abdominal or pelvic pain (dull, intermittent, or crampy)
- Vaginal spotting or light bleeding
- Adnexal tenderness on exam
- Possible palpable adnexal mass (but often not palpable)
- Vitals typically normal

### Ruptured Ectopic
- **Sudden severe abdominal pain** (often unilateral initially → diffuse as blood fills peritoneum)
- **Peritoneal signs** — rebound tenderness, guarding, rigidity
- **Referred shoulder pain** (Kehr's sign — diaphragmatic irritation from hemoperitoneum)
- **Hemorrhagic shock** — tachycardia, hypotension, diaphoresis, altered mental status, pallor
- **Cervical motion tenderness** (chandelier sign — pain so severe patient "reaches for the chandelier")
- Vaginal bleeding may be minimal despite massive intra-abdominal hemorrhage
