| **MRA** (mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) | Spironolactone, eplerenone | Spironolactone 25-50 mg daily | ↓ Aldosterone effects (fibrosis, Na retention, K wasting) | Yes (RALES, EMPHASIS-HF) |
| **SGLT2 inhibitor** | Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), empagliflozin (Jardiance) | Dapa 10 mg; empa 10 mg daily | Osmotic diuresis, cardioprotective mechanisms (multiple); benefit even WITHOUT diabetes | Yes (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced) |

**Additional medications:**
- **Diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide):** For congestion; symptom relief; NO mortality benefit; titrate to euvolemia (see Diuretics)
- **Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate:** Alternative if ACE/ARB/ARNI not tolerated; particularly beneficial in Black patients (A-HeFT trial)
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - **Daily weights** (same time, same scale, same clothing) — most reliable indicator of fluid status; report gain > 2 lbs/day or 5 lbs/week
  - I&O strictly (fluid restriction typically 1.5-2 L/day in advanced HF)
  - Lung sounds (crackles = congestion), JVD, peripheral edema (pitting scale), hepatojugular reflux
  - Hemodynamics if PA catheter: elevated PAWP (> 18 mmHg), decreased CI (< 2.2 L/min/m²)
  - Monitor K+ closely (ACE/ARB/MRA all raise K+; diuretics lower K+; net effect varies)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP trending (decreasing = responding to treatment)
  - Fall risk (orthostatic hypotension from vasodilators/diuretics)
- **Medication management:**
  - Titrate GDMT to target doses (most patients are undertreated — doses too low)
  - Beta-blockers: Start low, go slow; do NOT start during acute decompensation (start when euvolemic)
  - ACE-I/ARNI: Monitor BP, K+, creatinine; 36-hour washout when switching ACE-I → ARNI (angioedema risk)
  - MRA: Monitor K+ within 1 week of starting and after dose changes; hold if K+ > 5.5
  - SGLT2i: Monitor for volume depletion, UTI/yeast; hold during acute illness
- **Patient teaching:**
pearls: **The "Fantastic Four" should be started in ALL HFrEF patients unless contraindicated:** ARNI (or ACE/ARB) + evidence-based beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor. Each has independent mortality benefit. Together they reduce mortality by ~60-70% compared to no treatment. Most patients are UNDER-dosed.
**SGLT2 inhibitors benefit HFrEF patients even WITHOUT diabetes.** DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced proved this definitively. If your HFrEF patient isn't on dapagliflozin or empagliflozin, ask why.

**Don't start beta-blockers during acute decompensation.** They can worsen acute HF by reducing contractility when the patient is already in crisis. Stabilize first (diuresis, vasodilators), then start low-dose beta-blocker when euvolemic.
**S3 gallop in an adult is pathologic until proven otherwise.** It indicates elevated LV filling pressure and ventricular dysfunction. If you hear an S3, think HF.
**Hyponatremia in HF is a BAD prognostic sign.** It reflects severe neurohormonal activation (excess ADH). Fluid restriction is the treatment — not sodium supplementation.

conditions.title: Heat Stroke
conditions.keyFeatures:
### CNS (Most Sensitive Organ to Heat)
- **Altered mental status** — confusion, combativeness, delirium (REQUIRED for diagnosis)
- Seizures
- Ataxia, cerebellar dysfunction
- Coma
- Cerebral edema

### Cardiovascular
- Tachycardia (hyperdynamic early → cardiovascular collapse late)
- Hypotension (vasodilation + volume depletion)
- High-output failure initially → low-output failure if prolonged

### Hepatic
- **Liver is exquisitely heat-sensitive** — transaminases rise 24-72 hours after insult
- AST/ALT can reach >10,000 U/L
- Fulminant hepatic failure in severe cases (2-3 days post-event)
- **Repeat LFTs at 24-48 hours** — peak injury is delayed

### Renal
- AKI from hypovolemia, rhabdomyolysis, direct thermal injury
- Myoglobin from rhabdomyolysis → tubular necrosis
- CK often markedly elevated (especially exertional)

### Hematologic
- **DIC** — temperature-induced endothelial damage activates coagulation cascade
- Check coagulation studies (PT/INR, fibrinogen, D-dimer, platelets)

### Metabolic
- Lactic acidosis (tissue hypoperfusion + increased anaerobic metabolism)
- Hyperkalemia (from rhabdomyolysis and cellular injury)
- Hypoglycemia (hepatic failure, glycogen depletion)

---
conditions.management:
### COOL FIRST — The Only Treatment That Matters

**Target: core temperature <39°C (102.2°F) within 30 minutes**

#### Gold Standard: Cold Water Immersion
- Submerge patient in ice water bath (1-3°C / 34-37°F)
- Cooling rate: ~0.2°C/minute (fastest method)
- Monitor rectal temperature continuously
- **Stop cooling at 38.5-39°C** to prevent overshoot hypothermia
- Most effective for exertional heat stroke
- Logistically difficult in some EDs

#### Alternative Cooling Methods (When Immersion Not Available)
| Method | Effectiveness | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaporative cooling** | Good | Undress patient completely, mist with tepid water, direct fans → evaporation is very effective |
| **Ice packs to axillae, groin, neck** | Moderate | Target areas with large vessels near skin; adjunct to other methods |
| **Cold IV fluids** (4°C NS) | Moderate | Dual purpose: cooling + volume resuscitation |
| **Cooling blankets** | Moderate | Slower; often used as adjunct |
| **Peritoneal/bladder lavage** | Last resort | For refractory hyperthermia |

> [!warning]
> **Antipyretics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) DO NOT WORK for heat stroke.** Heat stroke is NOT a fever — the hypothalamic setpoint is normal, but the body is overwhelmed. Antipyretics reset the setpoint (useful in infection); they cannot enhance heat dissipation. Acetaminophen may also worsen hepatic injury.

### Supportive Care
- **Airway management** — intubation for GCS ≤8 or refractory seizures
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Immediate Actions (Time-Critical)
1. **Rectal temperature** — obtain immediately; do not rely on other methods
2. **Undress completely** — remove all clothing
3. **Begin cooling immediately** — do not wait for physician orders if severely hyperthermic
4. **Two large-bore IVs** — cold NS bolus
5. **Continuous rectal temperature monitoring** — reassess every 5-10 minutes during active cooling
6. **Stop cooling at 38.5-39°C** — overshoot hypothermia is a real risk

### Monitoring
- Continuous cardiac monitoring — arrhythmias from electrolyte derangements and direct thermal injury
- Hourly urine output with Foley — dark urine = rhabdomyolysis
- Neuro checks — improving mental status is the best sign of successful cooling
- Serial labs: CK, LFTs, coags at 6-12 hour intervals

### Prevention Education (Discharge for Heat Exhaustion)
- Hydrate before and during activity — water + electrolyte-containing fluids
- Acclimatization — gradual increase in heat exposure over 10-14 days
- Avoid peak heat hours (10 AM - 4 PM)
- Know the signs: confusion, lack of sweating, headache
- **High-risk medications** — counsel patients on anticholinergics, diuretics, antipsychotics about heat risk
pearls: **Cool first, diagnose second** — every minute above 40°C causes progressive organ damage; do not delay cooling for labs, imaging, or IV access
**Mental status is the dividing line** — hot and confused = heat stroke; hot and alert = heat exhaustion; this distinction drives the urgency
**Sweating does NOT rule out heat stroke** — exertional heat stroke patients are often still sweating; anhidrosis is more classic in non-exertional/elderly heat stroke
**Antipyretics are useless** — this is not a fever; the setpoint is normal; the cooling system is overwhelmed; acetaminophen and ibuprofen do nothing and may cause harm
**Liver injury peaks at 24-72 hours** — a patient cooled successfully may look fine at hour 6 and develop fulminant hepatic failure at hour 48; repeat LFTs

conditions.title: Helicobacter Pylori Infection
conditions.overview: Chronic bacterial infection of the gastric epithelium caused by *Helicobacter pylori*, a gram-negative, microaerophilic, S-shaped bacterium. H. pylori causes chronic gastritis in ALL infected individuals and is the primary etiologic agent for Peptic Ulcer Disease, gastric adenocarcinoma, and gastric MALT lymphoma.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — H. pylori colonizes gastric mucosa via urease (neutralizes acid), flagella (motility through mucus), and adhesins (epithelial attachment); person-to-person transmission via oral-oral or fecal-oral routes. Inflammation and Immune Response — chronic H. pylori infection = persistent neutrophilic then mononuclear infiltration of the gastric mucosa; CagA-positive strains inject the CagA protein into epithelial cells via type IV secretion, triggering intracellular signaling cascades → increased cancer risk.*

H. pylori colonizes the gastric epithelium and survives the acidic environment through urease production (converts urea to ammonia, neutralizing gastric acid). The bacterium induces chronic active gastritis in all infected individuals through multiple virulence factors:

- **CagA pathogenicity island:** Type IV secretion system (T4SS) injects CagA protein into gastric epithelial cells, disrupting cellular signaling and increasing cancer risk
- **VacA (vacuolating cytotoxin A):** Creates membrane channels, induces apoptosis, and disrupts epithelial barrier function
- **BabA (blood group antigen-binding adhesin):** Facilitates bacterial adherence to gastric epithelium

Gastritis phenotypes determine clinical outcomes:
- **Antral-predominant gastritis:** Leads to increased gastrin secretion and acid hypersecretion; associated with duodenal ulcer development
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

Most infected individuals remain asymptomatic throughout their lives. When symptomatic:
- Epigastric pain or discomfort
- Nausea and vomiting
- Bloating and early satiety
- Dyspepsia symptoms
- Signs of complications (see below)

### Complications

- **Peptic Ulcer Disease:** 10-15% lifetime risk in infected individuals; H. pylori causes 70-90% of duodenal ulcers and 60-70% of gastric ulcers
- **Gastric adenocarcinoma:** Approximately 1% lifetime risk; 90% of gastric cancers are attributable to H. pylori infection
- **Gastric MALT lymphoma:** Rare but strongly associated with H. pylori; often regresses with eradication therapy
- **Iron deficiency anemia:** Due to chronic blood loss or impaired iron absorption
- **Vitamin B12 deficiency:** Secondary to atrophic gastritis
conditions.management:
### Acute

**Eradication therapy** is indicated for:
- Active or past Peptic Ulcer Disease
- Gastric MALT lymphoma
- Post-gastric cancer resection
- First-degree relatives of gastric cancer patients
- Dyspepsia (test-and-treat strategy)
- Unexplained iron deficiency anemia
- Long-term Aspirin or NSAID use
- Patient preference after informed consent

### Chronic/Maintenance

**Test of cure:** Mandatory 4+ weeks after completion of eradication therapy using UBT or fecal antigen test (NOT serology). Confirms successful eradication and identifies treatment failures requiring alternative regimens.

**Reinfection prevention:** Reinfection rates <3% per year in developed countries; consider household screening and treatment in endemic areas.

### Pharmacology

**First-line regimens (14-day duration preferred):**

1. **PPI-clarithromycin triple therapy** (in regions with <15% clarithromycin resistance):
   - PPI (standard or double dose) BID
   - Clarithromycin 500mg BID
   - Amoxicillin 1000mg BID

2. **Bismuth quadruple therapy** (first-line in high clarithromycin resistance areas):
   - PPI (standard or double dose) BID
   - Bismuth Subsalicylate 525mg QID
   - Metronidazole 500mg TID-QID
   - Tetracycline 500mg QID

3. **Concomitant therapy** (non-bismuth quadruple):
   - PPI BID
   - Clarithromycin 500mg BID
   - Amoxicillin 1000mg BID
   - Metronidazole 500mg BID

**Second-line regimens** (after first-line failure):
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Medication adherence:** Complex multi-drug regimens with significant pill burden (quadruple therapy = 12-18 pills/day); provide clear written instructions and pill organizers
- **Side effect management:** Nausea, diarrhea, metallic taste (metronidazole), black stools (bismuth) are common; educate patients that black stools from bismuth are harmless
- **Test of cure importance:** Emphasize that negative initial symptoms do NOT confirm eradication; UBT or fecal antigen test required 4+ weeks post-treatment
- **PPI timing:** Take 30-60 minutes before meals for optimal acid suppression during treatment
- **Alcohol avoidance:** Metronidazole causes disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol; strict avoidance during treatment and 48 hours after
- **Clarithromycin interactions:** Multiple drug interactions including statins, warfarin, antiarrhythmics; review medication list
- **Tetracycline precautions:** Avoid dairy products, antacids, and iron supplements within 2 hours; photosensitivity risk
pearls: **The biggest barrier to H. pylori eradication is medication non-adherence, not antibiotic resistance** — complex regimens with 12-18 pills daily, significant GI side effects, and metallic taste lead to premature discontinuation; invest time in pre-treatment education and follow-up check-ins at day 3-5.

**Black stools during bismuth therapy terrify patients who think they have GI bleeding** — proactively educate that bismuth causes harmless black discoloration; this single conversation prevents countless panicked calls and ED visits.

**"Feeling better" after 5 days of antibiotics does NOT mean H. pylori is eradicated** — patients frequently stop treatment early when dyspepsia resolves; emphasize that symptom resolution ≠ bacterial eradication and that incomplete treatment breeds resistance.

**Metronidazole + alcohol = severe illness** — ensure patients understand this is not a mild interaction; even small amounts of alcohol (mouthwash, cough syrup, tiramisu) can cause violent nausea, vomiting, flushing, and tachycardia.

**Test of cure is non-negotiable, not optional** — up to 20% of eradication attempts fail; patients who don't complete test of cure walk around with persistent infection, ongoing ulcer/cancer risk, and false reassurance that they're cured.

conditions.title: Hemophilia
conditions.overview: Hemophilia is a group of X-linked recessive bleeding disorders caused by deficiency or dysfunction of clotting factors in the intrinsic coagulation pathway. Hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency) accounts for ~80-85% of cases, and hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency) accounts for ~15-20%. Both result in impaired thrombin generation and defective fibrin clot formation, leading to prolonged and excessive bleeding, particularly into joints and soft tissues. Severity correlates directly with residual factor activity level.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — factor VIII (hemophilia A) or IX (hemophilia B) deficiency halts the intrinsic pathway at a critical amplification step; without factor X activation, thrombin generation fails and the fibrin clot cannot form.*

1. **Normal coagulation:** Tissue injury → tissue factor pathway activates small amount of thrombin → thrombin activates FVIII and FIX → FVIIIa + FIXa form the tenase complex on platelet surfaces → tenase complex activates FX → massive thrombin burst → stable fibrin clot formation
2. **In hemophilia:** Deficient FVIII (hemophilia A) or FIX (hemophilia B) → impaired tenase complex formation → inadequate thrombin generation → weak, unstable fibrin clot → delayed and prolonged bleeding
3. **Primary hemostasis is intact:** Platelet plug forms normally (platelet count and VWF are normal) → initial bleeding may stop temporarily → but secondary hemostasis fails → rebleeding occurs hours later (classic pattern of delayed hemorrhage after trauma or surgery)
4. **Joint destruction (hemophilic arthropathy):** Repeated hemarthrosis → iron deposition from blood → synovial inflammation (hemosiderin-laden macrophages) → hypertrophic synovitis → neovascularization (friable vessels → more bleeding) → cartilage destruction → end-stage arthropathy; begins in childhood if untreated

### Severity Classification
| Severity | Factor Activity | Bleeding Pattern |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Hemarthrosis (hallmark of severe disease):** Recurrent spontaneous bleeding into joints — knees, elbows, ankles most commonly affected; acute presentation: joint swelling, warmth, severe pain, limited range of motion; "target joints" develop (joints with ≥3 bleeds in 6 months)
- **Muscle/soft tissue hematomas:** Iliopsoas hemorrhage (mimics appendicitis; can compress femoral nerve), compartment syndrome from forearm/calf bleeds
- **Mucosal bleeding:** Oral cavity bleeding (after dental procedures), epistaxis, GI bleeding (less prominent than in Von Willebrand Disease)
- **Intracranial hemorrhage:** Most serious complication; occurs in 3-5% of patients; leading cause of hemophilia-related death; can be spontaneous in severe disease
- **Post-surgical/post-traumatic bleeding:** Excessive, prolonged bleeding — may be the presenting feature in mild hemophilia
- **Pseudotumors:** Encapsulated hematomas in bone or soft tissue from inadequately treated bleeds; can erode bone; require surgical management
- **Neonatal presentation:** Cephalohematoma, prolonged bleeding from circumcision, intracranial hemorrhage; 50% of severe hemophilia diagnosed in neonatal period

### Complications
- Hemophilic arthropathy (chronic joint destruction — major source of disability)
- Intracranial hemorrhage (3-5% lifetime risk; mortality ~20%)
- Compartment syndrome from muscle hemorrhage
- Inhibitor development (anti-factor antibodies — most challenging complication)
conditions.management:
### Acute — Bleeding Episodes
- **Factor replacement (on-demand):**
  - Hemophilia A: Recombinant FVIII concentrate — dose based on target factor level and body weight; 1 IU/kg raises FVIII by ~2%; joint/muscle bleed target: 40-60%; major surgery/ICH target: 80-100%
  - Hemophilia B: Recombinant FIX concentrate — 1 IU/kg raises FIX by ~1% (lower recovery); same target levels
- **RICE protocol** for joint bleeds: Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation — adjunctive to factor replacement
- **Avoid:** Aspirin, NSAIDs (impair platelet function — use COX-2 selective if needed), IM injections, arterial punctures
- **Intracranial hemorrhage:** Treat FIRST, image SECOND — immediately infuse factor to 100% activity before CT scan; any head trauma in severe hemophilia = treat empirically
- **Antifibrinolytics:** Tranexamic acid or aminocaproic acid — adjunctive for mucosal bleeding (oral, dental, epistaxis); do NOT use with prothrombin complex concentrates (thrombosis risk)

### Chronic/Maintenance — Prophylaxis
- **Primary prophylaxis (standard of care for severe disease):**
  - Hemophilia A: FVIII 25-40 IU/kg 3x/week or every other day; goal: maintain trough factor level >1-3%
  - Hemophilia B: FIX 25-40 IU/kg 2x/week (longer half-life)
  - Start before age 2-3 or after first joint bleed → prevents arthropathy
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Venous access:**
  - Many patients with severe hemophilia have central venous access devices (ports) for home factor infusion; maintain port per protocol
  - Avoid arterial punctures and IM injections when possible; apply prolonged pressure (≥5 minutes) to all venipuncture sites
  - Peripheral IVs: use smallest gauge possible; secure well to avoid hematoma from dislodgement
- **Bleeding assessment:**
  - Assess joints every shift: swelling, warmth, ROM, pain level; compare to patient's baseline (chronic arthropathy may have baseline deformity)
  - Head injury assessment: any head trauma in severe hemophilia requires factor infusion BEFORE imaging; neurological checks q1-2h for 24 hours post-injury
  - Monitor for compartment syndrome in limb bleeds: pain out of proportion, paresthesias, pallor, pulselessness, pain with passive stretch → surgical emergency
- **Medication safety:**
  - NEVER administer aspirin or traditional NSAIDs; acetaminophen for pain; COX-2 inhibitors (celecoxib) if anti-inflammatory needed
  - Verify factor product, dose, and reconstitution — factor concentrates require specific reconstitution and infusion rates
pearls: **Treat first, image second for head injuries.** In severe hemophilia, any head trauma — even seemingly minor — requires immediate factor infusion to 100% activity BEFORE obtaining imaging. Waiting for a CT scan result before treating can allow a treatable hemorrhage to become fatal. Infuse factor in the ED, then scan.

**The aPTT is useless in patients on emicizumab.** Emicizumab artificially normalizes the aPTT, making it worthless for monitoring. Standard one-stage FVIII assays also give falsely elevated results. If you need to measure true FVIII activity (e.g., during a breakthrough bleed requiring factor), the lab MUST use a chromogenic bovine assay. Flag this for the lab every single time.

**"I feel a tingle in my knee" is NOT a minor complaint in hemophilia.** Patients with target joints often describe an "aura" — a tingling or warm sensation — before a full hemarthrosis develops. This is the window for early factor infusion that can abort the bleed entirely. Dismissing this complaint leads to a full joint bleed, more iron deposition, and more arthropathy.

**Never give IM injections to a patient with hemophilia.** Intramuscular injection can cause a deep muscle hematoma that may progress to compartment syndrome. All injections (vaccines, medications) should be given subcutaneously or intravenously. Use a small-gauge needle and apply firm pressure for ≥5 minutes.

**Inhibitor patients on emicizumab who need bypassing agents face a clotting risk.** If a patient on emicizumab has a breakthrough bleed requiring FEIBA (aPCC), the combination can cause thrombotic microangiopathy. Avoid aPCC if possible; use rFVIIa (NovoSeven) as the first-line bypassing agent. If aPCC is unavoidable, use the lowest effective dose and monitor closely.

conditions.title: Hepatic Encephalopathy
conditions.overview: Hepatic encephalopathy (HE) is a potentially reversible neuropsychiatric syndrome occurring in patients with acute or chronic liver disease and/or portosystemic shunting. It encompasses a spectrum of cognitive, psychiatric, and motor disturbances ranging from subclinical deficits (minimal HE) to coma.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — ammonia promotes synthesis of false neurotransmitters (octopamine, tyramine) that compete with true neurotransmitters at receptor sites; glutamine accumulation in astrocytes causes osmotic swelling; ammonia also potentiates GABA-A receptor activity (Gi-coupled pathway effects), producing the inhibitory neurological picture of encephalopathy*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — HE represents the brain's response to failed hepatic detoxification; the liver's role as the central homeostatic organ for nitrogen metabolism, drug clearance, coagulation factor synthesis, and glucose buffering means that when hepatic function fails, multiple interdependent feedback loops collapse simultaneously, explaining the multi-system clinical picture*

HE results from multiple interacting mechanisms centered on **ammonia accumulation** and **systemic inflammation**:

**Ammonia hypothesis:**
- Liver failure and portosystemic shunting prevent hepatic detoxification of gut-derived ammonia
- Ammonia crosses blood-brain barrier and is metabolized by astrocytes to glutamine
- Glutamine accumulation causes astrocyte swelling (low-grade cerebral edema), oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction
- Results in altered neurotransmission (increased GABAergic tone, decreased glutamatergic signaling)

**Inflammation and neuroinflammation:**
- Systemic inflammation from bacterial translocation, infections, and immune dysfunction
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Classification by etiology:**
- **Type A:** Associated with acute liver failure (fulminant hepatic failure)
- **Type B:** Associated with portosystemic shunts without intrinsic liver disease (rare)
- **Type C:** Associated with cirrhosis (most common)

**West Haven Criteria (Grades I-IV):**
- **Minimal HE (MHE):** No clinically apparent symptoms; deficits detected only by psychometric testing; impaired quality of life, driving ability, and work productivity
- **Grade I:** Mild confusion, euphoria or depression, shortened attention span, sleep disturbance (reversal of sleep-wake cycle)
- **Grade II:** Lethargy, disorientation to time, personality changes, inappropriate behavior, asterixis present
- **Grade III:** Somnolence to semi-stupor, responsive to stimuli, confusion, gross disorientation, asterixis usually absent
- **Grade IV:** Coma, unresponsive to painful stimuli

**Physical examination findings:**
- **Asterixis (flapping tremor):** Hallmark sign in grades I-II; typically absent in grade III-IV
- Hyperreflexia or hyporeflexia
- Hypertonia or hypotonia
- Fetor hepaticus (musty, sweet breath odor)
- Signs of underlying liver disease (jaundice, ascites, spider angiomata, palmar erythema)

### Complications

- **Falls and trauma:** Impaired coordination and judgment increase injury risk
- **Aspiration pneumonia:** Altered mental status impairs airway protection
- **Pressure ulcers:** Prolonged immobility in higher grades
conditions.management:
### Acute

**Principles:** Identify and treat precipitating factors; reduce ammonia production and absorption; provide supportive care.

1. **Lactulose (first-line):**
   - Lactulose 20-30g (30-45 mL) PO/NG every 1-2 hours until bowel movement, then 15-45 mL BID-TID
   - **Goal:** 2-3 soft, semi-formed stools per day
   - Mechanisms: Acidifies colonic contents (converts ammonia to non-absorbable ammonium ion); acts as cathartic (reduces gut transit time); alters gut microbiome
   - Can be administered as retention enema (300 mL in 700 mL water) in patients unable to take oral medications

2. **Rifaximin:**
   - Rifaximin 550mg PO BID
   - Non-absorbable antibiotic; reduces ammonia-producing gut bacteria
   - **Used as adjunct to lactulose in recurrent HE**, not as monotherapy for acute episodes

3. **Treat precipitating factors:**
   - Broad-spectrum antibiotics for suspected infection (diagnostic paracentesis for ascites)
   - IV fluids and electrolyte repletion
   - Discontinue benzodiazepines, opioids, and other sedating medications
   - Correct hypoglycemia
   - Treat GI bleeding
   - Bowel regimen for constipation

4. **Supportive care:**
   - Airway protection (intubation for grade III-IV)
   - Aspiration precautions
   - Fall precautions
   - Avoid physical restraints (worsen agitation)
   - Frequent reorientation and non-pharmacologic calming measures

### Chronic/Maintenance

**Secondary prophylaxis** (prevent recurrent episodes):
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Mental status assessment:** Frequent neuro checks using standardized tools; monitor for asterixis (have patient extend arms and dorsiflex wrists for 30 seconds); assess orientation to person, place, time, situation
- **Number connection test:** Simple bedside screening for MHE/mild HE; patient connects numbered circles 1-25 as quickly as possible; >50 seconds abnormal
- **Lactulose titration:** Goal is 2-3 soft/semi-formed stools per day, NOT diarrhea; excessive lactulose causes dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, and worsens HE
- **Stool tracking:** Document frequency, consistency; adjust lactulose dose based on stool pattern
- **Fall prevention:** High fall risk due to altered mental status, asterixis, and gait instability; bed alarm, frequent rounding, assistance with ambulation
- **Aspiration precautions:** Elevate head of bed 30-45 degrees; assess swallow safety before oral intake; NPO if grade III-IV until mental status improves
- **Medication reconciliation:** Review home medications; hold or discontinue benzodiazepines, opioids, sedating antihistamines, muscle relaxants; PPIs may increase HE risk
pearls: **Ammonia levels are the most over-ordered and misinterpreted lab in liver disease** — normal ammonia does NOT exclude HE, and elevated ammonia in an alert patient does NOT mandate treatment; diagnose HE clinically, not by lab values.

**The most common cause of "refractory HE" is inadequate lactulose dosing, not treatment failure** — many patients take lactulose once daily or skip doses due to inconvenience; if they're not having 2-3 soft stools per day, they're undertreated.

**Asterixis is specific but not sensitive** — its presence confirms HE, but its absence does NOT exclude it; asterixis typically disappears in grade III-IV as patients become too obtunded to cooperate with testing.

**Protein restriction is harmful, not helpful** — this outdated practice causes sarcopenia, worsens frailty, and paradoxically increases HE risk; cirrhotic patients need MORE protein (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day), not less.

**Physical restraints in HE patients create a dangerous feedback loop** — restraints increase agitation, which increases metabolic demand and ammonia production, which worsens encephalopathy; use verbal de-escalation and family presence instead.

conditions.title: Hepatitis A
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Disease Phases
| Phase | Timing | Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Incubation** | 15-50 days | Asymptomatic; highest viral shedding |
| **Prodrome** | 1-2 weeks | Non-specific: fatigue, malaise, anorexia, nausea, low-grade fever, RUQ discomfort |
| **Icteric phase** | 1-4 weeks | Jaundice, dark urine (bilirubinuria), pale stools (acholic), pruritus |
| **Convalescence** | Weeks to months | Gradual resolution; fatigue may persist |

### Age-Dependent Presentation
| Age | Symptomatic Rate | Jaundice Rate | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Children <6** | ~30% | ~10% | Usually asymptomatic — silent spreaders |
| **Older children/adults** | ~70% | ~70% | More likely to present to ED |
| **Older adults** | >70% | >70% | **Higher morbidity and mortality** |

### Symptoms
- **GI:** Anorexia (often profound), nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, RUQ pain
- **Constitutional:** Fatigue, malaise, low-grade fever, myalgias, arthralgias
- **Hepatic:** Jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stools, hepatomegaly, pruritus
- **Unusual:** Rash, arthralgias (immune-complex mediated)

### Complications
| Complication | Frequency | Features |
|---|---|---|
| **Cholestatic hepatitis** | ~5% | Prolonged jaundice (>3 months), intense pruritus, markedly elevated bilirubin |
| **Relapsing hepatitis** | ~10% | Recurrence of symptoms 1-3 months after initial improvement; self-limited |
conditions.management:
### Supportive Care (Mainstay)
- **No antiviral treatment exists** — management is entirely supportive
- Hydration — IV fluids for patients unable to tolerate PO
- Antiemetics — ondansetron for nausea/vomiting
- Nutrition — small frequent meals as tolerated; no dietary restrictions
- Avoid hepatotoxic substances:
  - **Acetaminophen** — avoid or limit to <2 g/day
  - **Alcohol** — strict avoidance until transaminases normalize
  - Review all medications for hepatotoxicity
- Activity — as tolerated; no need for bed rest
- Monitor PT/INR — **coagulopathy = red flag for fulminant failure**

### Fulminant Hepatic Failure
- **Admit to ICU** — may need transfer to transplant center
- Monitor: mental status (encephalopathy staging), coagulation, glucose, ammonia
- **N-acetylcysteine (NAC)** — may benefit early fulminant failure regardless of cause
- Lactulose and rifaximin for hepatic encephalopathy
- **Liver transplant evaluation** if King's College or MELD criteria met
- Mortality without transplant: 50-80%

### Post-Exposure Prophylaxis
**Within 2 weeks of exposure:**
| Patient | Prophylaxis |
|---|---|
| **Healthy, age 1-40** | **HAV vaccine** (single dose) — preferred |
| **Age >40, immunocompromised, chronic liver disease** | **Immune globulin (IG)** 0.1 mL/kg IM ± vaccine |
| **Infants <12 months** | **IG only** (vaccine not approved <12 months) |

### Vaccination
- **2-dose series** (0 and 6-12 months) — Havrix, Vaqta
- **Combined Hep A/B vaccine** (Twinrix) — 3-dose series
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Assessment
- **Skin and scleral assessment** — jaundice progression/resolution
- **Fluid status** — patients with severe nausea may become significantly dehydrated
- **Mental status** — any confusion or somnolence may indicate encephalopathy → fulminant failure
- **Bleeding assessment** — check for petechiae, ecchymoses, gingival bleeding (coagulopathy)
- Monitor I&Os carefully

### Patient Education
- **Hand hygiene** — most critical intervention to prevent spread
- No food preparation for others during infectious period
- Sexual contact precautions (especially oral-anal)
- Avoid alcohol until cleared by provider
- Inform close contacts — they need PEP within 2 weeks
- Expected recovery timeline: weeks to months, fatigue is the last symptom to resolve
- **Cannot get Hep A again** — lifelong immunity after infection

### Common Patient Questions
- *"Is this the same as Hep B or C?"* — No. Hep A does not cause chronic infection and is spread differently (fecal-oral, not blood/sexual primarily)
- *"Can I go to work?"* — Not during infectious period (1 week after jaundice); food handlers may need health department clearance
pearls: **ALT >1000 with jaundice + recent risk exposure = think acute viral hepatitis** — order the full hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HBc IgM, HCV Ab)
**Patients are most contagious BEFORE they look sick** — by the time they're jaundiced in your ED, viral shedding is already declining
**HAV on top of chronic Hep C = disaster** — vaccination of chronic liver disease patients prevents this entirely preventable catastrophe
**Anti-HAV IgM is the only test you need for acute diagnosis** — total antibody tells you immunity status, not acute infection
**Elevated INR is the alarm bell** — in acute hepatitis, rising INR means the liver is failing; this patient needs close monitoring or transfer

conditions.title: Hepatitis B
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Acute Hepatitis B
- **Incubation:** 6 weeks to 6 months (average 90 days)
- **Prodrome:** Fatigue, anorexia, nausea, RUQ pain, arthralgias, fever
- **Icteric phase:** Jaundice, dark urine, clay-colored stools (same as Hep A)
- **Serum sickness-like syndrome:** Arthralgias, rash, fever (immune-complex mediated, ~10%)
- **Fulminant hepatic failure:** ~1% of acute cases — coagulopathy, encephalopathy, cerebral edema
- Most adults (95%) recover completely and develop anti-HBs immunity

### Chronic Hepatitis B
- Often **asymptomatic for decades** until cirrhosis or HCC develops
- Fatigue is the most common symptom
- Hepatomegaly on exam
- Extrahepatic manifestations: polyarteritis nodosa, glomerulonephritis (immune-complex)
- Diagnosed when **HBsAg persists >6 months**

### HBV Flares and Reactivation
- **Spontaneous flares** — ALT spikes during immune clearance attempts (phase transitions)
- **Immunosuppression-related reactivation** — the most dangerous scenario:
  - **Rituximab** (anti-CD20) — highest risk; screen ALL patients before treatment
  - **High-dose corticosteroids** — suppress immune control → viral rebound → hepatic flare when steroids stopped
  - **Chemotherapy, TNF-alpha inhibitors, other biologics**
  - Can cause **fulminant hepatic failure and death**
  - Prevention: screen HBsAg and anti-HBc before immunosuppression; prophylactic antivirals if positive

---
conditions.management:
### Acute Hepatitis B
- **Supportive care** — same as Hep A (hydration, antiemetics, avoid hepatotoxins)
- Antivirals **not routinely indicated** for uncomplicated acute HBV (95% of adults clear it)
- **Antivirals indicated if:**
  - Fulminant hepatic failure
  - Protracted severe course (coagulopathy, prolonged jaundice >4 weeks)
  - Immunocompromised
- Drug of choice: **Tenofovir** or **Entecavir**

### Chronic Hepatitis B — Treatment Indications
Treatment indicated when:
- HBV DNA >2,000 IU/mL AND elevated ALT AND/OR significant fibrosis
- Cirrhosis with detectable HBV DNA (any level)
- HCC prevention in high-risk patients

### Antiviral Agents
| Drug | Class | Mechanism | Key Points |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tenofovir (TDF/TAF)** | Nucleotide analogue | Inhibits reverse transcriptase | First-line; high barrier to resistance; TAF = less renal/bone toxicity |
| **Entecavir** | Nucleoside analogue | Inhibits reverse transcriptase | First-line; high barrier to resistance; avoid if prior lamivudine resistance |
| **PEG-interferon alfa** | Immunomodulator | Enhances immune clearance of infected hepatocytes | Finite duration (48 weeks); higher HBsAg loss rate; significant side effects |

### Treatment Goals
- **Functional cure** = **HBsAg loss** ± anti-HBs seroconversion (achieved in <10% with NUCs)
- **Virologic suppression** = undetectable HBV DNA (achievable in most patients on NUCs)
- **Biochemical response** = ALT normalization
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Assessment
- **Occupational exposure history** — needlestick, splash; document time, source, exposure type
- **Vaccination status** — critical for determining PEP
- **Risk factors** — IVDU, sexual contacts, birth country (endemic regions), prior HBV testing
- **Liver assessment** — jaundice, RUQ tenderness, hepatomegaly, ascites, spider angiomata
- **Mental status** — encephalopathy screening for patients with acute flares or cirrhosis

### Vaccination in the ED
- **Opportunity for vaccination** — ED visits are often the only healthcare contact for high-risk populations
- Many EDs now stock and administer Hep B vaccine (first dose of series)
- Document and communicate to PCP for series completion

### Needlestick Protocol
1. **Immediately:** Wash site with soap and water (mucous membranes: flush with water)
2. **Do NOT squeeze** — does not help and may increase inoculation
3. Report to charge nurse and employee health
4. **Source patient testing** — HBsAg, HCV Ab, HIV (with consent)
5. **Exposed worker** — HBsAb titer if previously vaccinated
6. **HBIG if indicated** — must be given within **24 hours** (ideally within hours)
7. Document everything
pearls: **HBV serology is the most commonly tested lab interpretation in medicine** — learn the 6-marker table cold; every certification exam tests it
**The window period catches people** — if you only check HBsAg and anti-HBs, both can be negative during acute resolving infection; anti-HBc IgM is the only positive marker
**"Anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative" = vaccinated; "Both positive" = naturally immune** — vaccination produces ONLY anti-HBs, natural infection produces both
**HBV is 50-100× more infectious than HIV by needlestick** — this is why we vaccinate all healthcare workers; take sharps safety seriously
**Screen BEFORE rituximab, always** — HBV reactivation during anti-CD20 therapy can be fatal; this applies to HBsAg-negative/anti-HBc-positive patients too (occult HBV)

conditions.title: Hirschsprung Disease
conditions.overview: Hirschsprung disease (HSCR) is a rare congenital intestinal motility disorder characterized by the absence of ganglion cells in the myenteric (Auerbach) and submucosal (Meissner) plexuses of the distal bowel. This aganglionosis results in functional obstruction due to failure of the affected segment to relax, preventing normal peristalsis and stool passage.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — normal bowel peristalsis requires the enteric nervous system; Hirschsprung = failure of neural crest cell migration during development → absence of ganglion cells in the myenteric plexus → tonic smooth muscle contraction without the relaxation signal → functional obstruction. Genetics and Protein Expression — RET proto-oncogene mutations account for ~50% of familial cases; RET encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase essential for enteric nervous system development.*

**Embryologic defect**: Failure of enteric neural crest cells to migrate completely from the proximal to distal intestine during weeks 5-12 of gestation. Migration proceeds craniocaudally, so aganglionosis always extends proximally from the anus without skip lesions.

**Genetic basis**:
- **RET proto-oncogene** (chromosome 10q11.2): Most common; encodes receptor tyrosine kinase essential for neural crest cell migration and survival; mutations in ~50% of familial cases, 15-20% of sporadic cases
- **EDNRB** (endothelin receptor B) and **EDN3** (endothelin-3): Signaling pathway for neural crest cell migration
- **SOX10**: Transcription factor critical for neural crest development
- Polygenic inheritance in most cases (not simple Mendelian)

**Pathophysiologic consequences**:
- **Absent ganglion cells**: No intrinsic neurons to coordinate relaxation and peristalsis
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Neonatal presentation** (80% diagnosed in neonatal period):
- **Delayed meconium passage**: Beyond 24 hours of life (occurs in 90% of term infants with HSCR); single most important diagnostic clue
- **Abdominal distension**: Progressive after birth
- **Bilious vomiting**: From functional obstruction
- **Feeding intolerance**: Refusal, poor intake
- Failure to thrive if diagnosis delayed

**Infant/child presentation** (late diagnosis):
- Chronic constipation refractory to medical management
- Abdominal distension
- Growth failure, malnutrition
- Enterocolitis (see complications)

**Physical examination**:
- Distended abdomen, visible bowel loops
- Rectal examination: Empty rectal vault (stool retained proximally), explosive passage of gas/stool upon withdrawal of examining finger
- No palpable stool in rectum despite constipation (contrast with functional constipation where rectum full of stool)

### Complications

**Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC)**:
- **Most serious complication**: Can be life-threatening; occurs in 15-30% of patients pre- or post-operatively
- **Presentation**: Fever, abdominal distension, explosive bloody/foul-smelling diarrhea, lethargy, signs of sepsis
- **Pathogenesis**: Stasis, bacterial overgrowth, mucosal barrier disruption, translocation; possibly immune dysregulation
- **Management**: Emergency — aggressive fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, rectal decompression (washouts), may require colostomy
conditions.management:
### Acute

**Neonatal/initial management**:
- **Bowel decompression**:
  - **Short-segment HSCR**: Rectal washouts/irrigations with saline (10-20 mL/kg) 1-2 times daily until definitive surgery
  - **Long-segment HSCR or HAEC**: Diverting colostomy/ileostomy placed proximal to aganglionic segment
- **Supportive care**: IV fluids, correct electrolyte abnormalities, decompress stomach (nasogastric tube), NPO until decompressed
- **Treat enterocolitis if present**: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (covers gram-negative and anaerobes), aggressive fluid resuscitation, rectal decompression, ICU monitoring if septic

**Definitive surgical management**: Pull-through procedure to resect aganglionic segment and anastomose ganglionic bowel to anus

**Pull-through techniques** (all aim to preserve sphincter function):
- **Swenson procedure**: Resection of aganglionic segment with end-to-end anastomosis at dentate line
- **Soave (endorectal) pull-through**: Mucosectomy of distal rectum, pull ganglionic bowel through muscular cuff
- **Duhamel procedure**: Side-to-side anastomosis (retro-rectal); less commonly used
- **Transanal endorectal pull-through (TERPT)**: Minimally invasive approach; single-stage procedure in neonates; shorter hospitalization
- **Laparoscopic-assisted pull-through**: Minimally invasive mobilization, then perineal anastomosis
conditions.nursingPriorities:
**Neonatal assessment**:
- **Meconium passage timing**: Document time of first meconium; passage >24 hours in term infant warrants HSCR evaluation
- Monitor for signs of obstruction: abdominal distension, bilious vomiting, feeding intolerance
- Measure abdominal girth serially

**Pre-operative care**:
- **Rectal washout technique**: Teach parents/caregivers; saline (not tap water — hypotonic, risk of water intoxication) via catheter, gravity or gentle bulb irrigation; goal is decompression, not aggressive cleanout
- Monitor for signs of enterocolitis (fever, explosive diarrhea, abdominal distension, lethargy)
- Ensure adequate hydration and nutrition (may require IV fluids, NG/NJ feeds)

**Recognize HAEC signs — this is an emergency**:
- Fever, abdominal distension, explosive foul-smelling/bloody diarrhea, lethargy, signs of sepsis
- Immediate interventions: IV access, fluid resuscitation, antibiotics, rectal decompression, notify surgeon

**Post-operative care**:
- Standard post-op monitoring: vital signs, pain assessment, surgical site, intake/output
- Monitor first bowel movement post-pull-through
pearls: **Delayed meconium passage beyond 24 hours in a term infant is Hirschsprung disease until proven otherwise.** 90% of healthy term infants pass meconium within 24 hours; 90% of HSCR infants do not. This is the single most important screening question.

**The rectal exam in HSCR is diagnostic before you ever send a biopsy — empty rectal vault despite obvious constipation, followed by explosive stool/gas on finger withdrawal.** This "squirt sign" reflects proximal stool held back by the spastic aganglionic segment. Functional constipation fills the rectum with stool.

**HAEC is the great killer in Hirschsprung disease — fever + distension + diarrhea in an HSCR patient is sepsis until proven otherwise.** Don't wait for "classic" bloody diarrhea; early HAEC may present with just increased stool frequency and fever. Aggressive resuscitation, antibiotics, and decompression save lives.

**Rectal washouts use saline, not tap water.** Tap water is hypotonic; absorption across inflamed bowel mucosa causes hyponatremia and water intoxication (seizures, cerebral edema). Use normal saline or commercial saline products.

**Post-pull-through babies poop constantly at first (10-15 times/day) and parents panic that surgery failed.** Reassure that this is normal; stool frequency gradually decreases over 6-12 months as the pulled-through bowel adapts. This is not diarrhea, it's frequent normal stool.

conditions.title: HIV / AIDS
conditions.overview:
HIV is a retrovirus that selectively destroys CD4+ T-helper lymphocytes — the "generals" of the adaptive immune system. Without treatment, progressive CD4 depletion leads to AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), defined by CD4 <200 cells/μL or the presence of an AIDS-defining illness. With modern antiretroviral therapy (ART), HIV is a manageable chronic disease with near-normal life expectancy.

In the ED, HIV patients present with: opportunistic infections (the hallmark of advanced disease), medication complications, new HIV diagnosis (often during workup for another complaint), occupational exposures (needlesticks), and sexual assault requiring post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
conditions.management:
### New HIV Diagnosis in the ED

- May be discovered incidentally (routine screening, pregnancy testing, workup for other complaints)
- **Do:** Deliver the diagnosis with compassion and privacy. Provide initial counseling. Arrange URGENT outpatient linkage to HIV care (within 1-2 weeks). Do NOT start ART in the ED unless there is institutional protocol for rapid start
- **Do NOT:** Discharge without follow-up plan. This diagnosis changes their life — ensure they have a name, number, and appointment

### Occupational Exposure (Needlestick)

- **Time is critical:** PEP should be started within **2 hours** ideally, no later than **72 hours**
- **Preferred PEP regimen:** Tenofovir/emtricitabine (Truvada or Descovy) + dolutegravir (Tivicay) or raltegravir — 28-day course
- **Baseline labs on exposed person:** HIV test, hepatitis B/C, BMP, CBC, pregnancy test
- **Source patient testing:** Rapid HIV test if status unknown (with consent)
- **Follow-up HIV testing:** At 4-6 weeks and 3 months post-exposure

### Sexual Assault

- PEP should be offered to all sexual assault survivors with potential HIV exposure
- Same regimen as occupational PEP — 28-day course
- Also screen/treat for: gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, hepatitis B, pregnancy (emergency contraception)

### ART Complications in the ED

| Complication | Drugs Involved | Presentation |
|-------------|---------------|-------------|
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Standard precautions are sufficient for routine care of HIV patients.** HIV is NOT transmitted by casual contact, respiratory droplets, or intact skin exposure. Gloves for blood/body fluid contact, standard sharps handling — the same precautions you use for every patient
- **Stigma is the biggest barrier to HIV care.** Patients with HIV experience discrimination in healthcare settings — studies consistently show this. Your language, your body language, and your willingness to provide the same quality of care matters. Don't double-glove. Don't avoid the room. Don't chart "HIV" in a way that broadcasts it to everyone on the unit
- **Medication adherence is everything.** Missing even a few doses of ART allows the virus to replicate and develop resistance. If an HIV patient is admitted and their ART is "held for NPO" or "not on formulary," advocate aggressively to get it restarted. ART should almost never be interrupted
pearls: **Acute HIV seroconversion is one of the most commonly missed diagnoses in medicine.** A young patient with fever, pharyngitis, diffuse rash, and lymphadenopathy is usually diagnosed with "viral syndrome" or "mono." If they have risk factors for HIV, send a viral load (RNA PCR) — the antibody test is often NEGATIVE during acute infection because the antibodies haven't formed yet.

**CD4 count is your roadmap to the differential.** When an HIV patient presents with a new infection, the CD4 count tells you what organisms to consider. Above 200: think community-acquired infections same as anyone else. Below 200: add PJP and candida. Below 100: add toxoplasmosis and cryptococcus. Below 50: add CMV and MAC. This framework is clinically powerful.

**The HIV life cycle explains every drug class.** If you understand the 8 steps above, you understand why we have NRTIs, NNRTIs, integrase inhibitors, protease inhibitors, fusion inhibitors, and CCR5 antagonists. Each drug class blocks a specific step. Combination therapy blocks multiple steps simultaneously, making resistance nearly impossible.

**HIV cannot be cured because of the latent reservoir.** Integrated proviral DNA in resting memory T-cells is invisible to the immune system and unaffected by ART. The "Berlin patient" (Timothy Ray Brown) was cured by receiving a bone marrow transplant from a CCR5-delta32 donor — essentially replacing his immune system with one that HIV couldn't enter. This is not scalable, but it proved cure is theoretically possible.

conditions.title: Hypertension
conditions.overview: Hypertension is a sustained elevation in systemic arterial blood pressure. Per 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines: Normal < 120/80, Elevated 120-129/<80, Stage 1 HTN 130-139 OR 80-89, Stage 2 HTN ≥ 140 OR ≥ 90, Hypertensive Crisis > 180 AND/OR > 120. Diagnosis requires elevated readings on ≥ 2 separate occasions (or ambulatory/home BP monitoring). HTN is the single most important modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke, and chronic kidney disease.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — BP = CO × SVR; HTN is sustained dysregulation of one or both variables. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the baroreceptor set point is reset upward in chronic HTN; the body defends the higher pressure as "normal." Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — angiotensin II (via AT1 GPCR) and catecholamines (via α/β adrenergic GPCRs) are the dominant molecular drivers of vasoconstriction.*

### Primary (Essential) HTN (~90-95% of cases)
- No single identifiable cause; multifactorial
- **Key mechanisms:**
  - Increased sympathetic nervous system activity → vasoconstriction, increased HR
  - RAAS overactivation → angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction + aldosterone-mediated Na+/water retention → increased blood volume (see ACE Inhibitors, ARBs)
  - Endothelial dysfunction → reduced nitric oxide (vasodilator) + increased endothelin (vasoconstrictor)
  - Renal sodium handling abnormalities → impaired pressure natriuresis
  - Arterial stiffening (aging, atherosclerosis) → increased SVR and systolic BP with widened pulse pressure
  - Genetic factors (polygenic; ~30-50% heritability)

### Secondary HTN (~5-10%)
| Cause | Clues |
|-------|-------|
| Renal artery stenosis | Resistant HTN, renal bruit, worsening Cr with ACE/ARB |
| Primary aldosteronism | Hypokalemia, resistant HTN, adrenal mass |
| Pheochromocytoma | Episodic HTN, headache, diaphoresis, palpitations ("spells") |
| Cushing syndrome | Central obesity, striae, moon face |
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Usually asymptomatic** ("silent killer") — most patients are diagnosed on routine screening
- Headache (classically occipital, morning; more common in severe HTN)
- Rarely: dizziness, epistaxis, visual changes, chest pain, dyspnea
- **Target organ damage (long-standing uncontrolled):**
  - **Heart:** LVH, diastolic dysfunction → HFpEF → HFrEF; coronary artery disease; Atrial Fibrillation
  - **Brain:** Stroke (ischemic and hemorrhagic); vascular dementia; hypertensive encephalopathy
  - **Kidneys:** Hypertensive nephrosclerosis → CKD → ESRD; proteinuria
  - **Eyes:** Hypertensive retinopathy (arteriolar narrowing, AV nicking, flame hemorrhages, cotton wool spots, papilledema in malignant HTN)
  - **Vasculature:** Atherosclerosis acceleration; aortic aneurysm/dissection; peripheral arterial disease

### Complications — Hypertensive Emergencies
| Emergency | Features | Treatment |
|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Hypertensive urgency** | SBP > 180 or DBP > 120 WITHOUT target organ damage | Oral medications; gradual reduction over 24-48 hr |
| **Hypertensive emergency** | SBP > 180 or DBP > 120 WITH target organ damage (encephalopathy, ACS, aortic dissection, eclampsia, pulmonary edema, AKI) | IV antihypertensives in ICU; reduce MAP ~25% in first hour; NOT to normal (risk of ischemia) |
conditions.management:
### Lifestyle Modifications (ALL patients, ALL stages)
| Intervention | Expected SBP Reduction |
|-------------|----------------------|
| DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) | ~11 mmHg |
| Sodium restriction (< 1500 mg/day ideal; < 2300 mg/day) | ~5-6 mmHg |
| Weight loss (1 kg lost ≈ 1 mmHg reduction) | ~5 mmHg per 5 kg |
| Regular aerobic exercise (150 min/week moderate intensity) | ~5-8 mmHg |
| Moderate alcohol (≤ 2 drinks/day men; ≤ 1 women) | ~4 mmHg |
| Potassium supplementation (3500-5000 mg/day from diet) | ~4-5 mmHg |

### Pharmacologic Treatment
| Drug Class | First-Line Indication | Key Notes |
|-----------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Thiazide diuretics** (chlorthalidone, HCTZ) | First-line for most; particularly effective in Black patients and elderly | See Diuretics |
| **ACE Inhibitors** (lisinopril, ramipril) | First-line; especially with diabetes, CKD (proteinuria reduction), HF, post-MI | See ACE Inhibitors; cough in 5-20%; angioedema rare but serious |
| **ARBs** (losartan, valsartan) | Alternative to ACE-I (if cough); same indications | See ARBs |
| **CCBs — Dihydropyridine** (amlodipine, nifedipine) | First-line; particularly effective in Black patients and elderly; good for isolated systolic HTN | See Calcium Channel Blockers; peripheral edema; no HFrEF concern |
| **Beta-blockers** (metoprolol, carvedilol) | NOT first-line for uncomplicated HTN; indicated with HF, post-MI, rate control | See Beta-Blockers |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Assessment:**
  - Accurate BP measurement (correct technique, cuff size, positioning)
  - Orthostatic vitals (lying → sitting → standing) especially in elderly and those starting/titrating meds
  - Monitor for target organ damage symptoms: headache, visual changes, chest pain, dyspnea, edema, decreased urine output
  - Medication adherence assessment (most common reason for uncontrolled HTN)
  - Review medication list for BP-raising drugs (NSAIDs, decongestants, OCPs, steroids)
- **Hypertensive emergency nursing:**
  - Continuous arterial line monitoring preferred for IV antihypertensives
  - Reduce MAP ~25% in first hour; NOT to normal (cerebral autoregulation adapted to high pressure → rapid lowering → watershed infarcts)
  - Neuro checks q1h; cardiac monitoring; I&O
- **Patient teaching:**
  - HTN is a LIFELONG condition requiring lifelong treatment
  - Do not stop medications because BP is normal (it's normal BECAUSE of the meds)
  - Home BP monitoring: Same arm, same time, sitting, keep a log, bring to appointments
  - Reduce sodium (read labels; avoid processed foods, restaurant meals)
pearls: **Hypertension is the #1 modifiable risk factor for stroke, MI, HF, CKD, and vascular death worldwide.** Controlling BP prevents more cardiovascular events than any other single intervention.
**Most patients are asymptomatic until organ damage occurs.** This is why screening is critical. Don't wait for headaches -- check BP at every healthcare encounter.
**"Start low, go slow" in elderly, but DO treat.** SPRINT trial showed benefit of intensive BP control (< 120 systolic) even in patients ≥ 75, but watch for orthostatic hypotension, falls, and AKI.

**Resistant hypertension** (uncontrolled on 3 drugs including a diuretic at max doses): Screen for secondary causes, check adherence, and consider adding spironolactone (PATHWAY-2 trial showed spironolactone is the most effective add-on).
**NSAIDs raise BP by ~3-5 mmHg** and blunt the effect of ACE/ARBs and diuretics. Always ask about OTC NSAID use in patients with poorly controlled HTN.

conditions.title: Hypoglycemia
conditions.overview:
Hypoglycemia is defined as an **abnormally low blood glucose concentration** that exposes the individual to potential harm. Severity thresholds:

| Level | Glucose | Clinical Significance |
|-------|---------|----------------------|
| **Alert value** | <70 mg/dL (3.9 mmol/L) | Sufficiently low to trigger treatment; counterregulatory hormones activate |
| **Clinically significant** | <54 mg/dL (3.0 mmol/L) | Serious, clinically important hypoglycemia; neuroglycopenic symptoms likely |
| **Severe** | <40 mg/dL (2.2 mmol/L) | Requires assistance of another person; seizure, coma, death risk |

### Whipple Triad

The classic diagnostic triad confirming true hypoglycemia:

1. **Symptoms** consistent with hypoglycemia
2. **Low plasma glucose** measured at the time of symptoms
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — glucose regulation IS a classic negative feedback loop: ↓ glucose → α cells → glucagon → hepatic glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis → ↑ glucose; counterregulatory response also includes epinephrine, cortisol, GH. Nutrition and Metabolism — the brain is obligate glucose-dependent (cannot use fatty acids); hypoglycemia-induced neuroglycopenia (confusion, seizure, coma) reflects impaired neuronal ATP production.*

### Normal Glucose Counterregulation

When glucose falls, the body activates a **hierarchical counterregulatory response:**

1. **~80 mg/dL** — Insulin secretion decreases (first defense)
2. **~65-70 mg/dL** — Glucagon secretion increases (most important acute counterregulation)
3. **~65-70 mg/dL** — Epinephrine secretion increases (drives adrenergic symptoms)
4. **~60 mg/dL** — Growth hormone and cortisol secretion increase (slower, sustained response)
5. **~50-55 mg/dL** — Neuroglycopenic symptoms appear (brain relies on glucose as primary fuel)
6. **<40 mg/dL** — Seizures, coma, death if untreated

### Why Diabetics Lose Counterregulation

In longstanding diabetes (especially T1DM but also advanced T2DM):
- **Glucagon response is lost** early (alpha-cell dysfunction)
- **Epinephrine response is blunted** after recurrent hypoglycemia
- This creates **hypoglycemia-associated autonomic failure (HAAF)**
- The result: **hypoglycemia unawareness** — the patient feels no warning symptoms before neuroglycopenic crisis
conditions.management:
### Immediate Treatment — Severity-Based

#### Mild-Moderate (Patient is ALERT, Able to Swallow)

**Rule of 15:**
1. Give **15 grams of fast-acting glucose** orally:
   - 4 oz (120 mL) fruit juice
   - 4 oz regular soda (not diet)
   - 3-4 glucose tablets
   - 1 tablespoon honey or sugar
2. **Recheck glucose in 15 minutes**
3. If still <70 mg/dL → repeat 15g glucose
4. Once glucose >70 mg/dL → give a **complex carb + protein snack** (crackers with peanut butter, cheese and crackers) to prevent recurrence
5. Continue monitoring q15-30 min until stable

#### Moderate-Severe (Patient is ALTERED but has IV Access)

**Dextrose 50% (D50W) — The Standard ED Treatment:**
- **Dose:** 25 grams IV push = one ampule (50 mL of D50W)
- **Onset:** Seconds to minutes
- **Administration technique:**
  - Use a large-bore IV in a large vein (antecubital preferred)
  - D50W is **highly hypertonic** (2,525 mOsm/L) and extremely irritating to veins
  - **Flush the line well** before and after — causes vein sclerosis and tissue necrosis if extravasation occurs
  - Push slowly over 2-3 minutes if possible (rapid push increases phlebitis risk)
  - If peripheral IV only → consider D10W infusion instead (less caustic; 250 mL of D10W = 25g dextrose)
- **Recheck glucose 5-10 minutes after administration**
- May repeat if glucose remains <70 mg/dL
- Follow with D10W drip (100-150 mL/hr) if recurrent or prolonged cause anticipated
conditions.nursingPriorities:
### Rapid Assessment Protocol

1. **ABCs first** — protect airway in the unconscious patient
2. **Immediate POC glucose** — before ANY other workup in an altered patient
3. If glucose <70 mg/dL → treat immediately (don't wait for lab confirmation)
4. Establish IV access if not already present
5. Place patient on continuous cardiac monitoring (hypoglycemia can cause arrhythmias)
6. Ongoing neurological assessment

### D50W Administration Best Practices

- **Always verify patency** of IV line before pushing D50W
- Use the **largest bore IV** available in the **largest vein** available
- If only small peripheral IV available → use D10W instead (250 mL = 25g glucose, less caustic)
- **Aspirate for blood return** before and during push
- Push slowly (2-3 minutes) when patient condition allows
- **Flush with 20-30 mL normal saline** immediately after
- Document IV site assessment before and after
- Monitor IV site for infiltration/extravasation — D50 extravasation can cause tissue necrosis
- If extravasation occurs: stop immediately, aspirate residual, elevate extremity, warm compress, notify provider

### Glucagon Administration
pearls: **"Any altered patient gets a glucose check."** This is the single most important pearl in emergency medicine. Hypoglycemia mimics stroke, intoxication, psychiatric crisis, and seizure. A 5-second fingerstick can save a life and avoid an unnecessary CT, intubation, or psychiatric hold.

**Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia needs 24-hour admission — no exceptions.** The half-life of glipizide is 2-5 hours, glimepiride 5-8 hours, and glyburide up to 24 hours. The patient who "feels fine" after D50 in the ED will crash again at 3 AM. Octreotide is the real treatment — start it early.

**"Feed after you treat."** Dextrose and glucagon provide a temporary glucose bridge. If you don't follow up with complex carbohydrates and protein, the glucose will drop again. A sandwich matters as much as the D50.

**Thiamine before dextrose in alcoholics — always.** Giving glucose without thiamine in a chronically malnourished patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia). Thiamine is cheap, fast, and safe. Make it a reflex.

**Glucagon does not work if glycogen is depleted.** In the alcoholic, the starved patient, or the patient who has been hypoglycemic for hours, there are no glycogen stores left for glucagon to mobilize. These patients need IV dextrose.

conditions.title: Hypovolemic Shock
conditions.overview:
Hypovolemic shock is inadequate tissue perfusion caused by **insufficient intravascular volume** — either from blood loss (hemorrhagic) or from non-hemorrhagic fluid losses. It is the most common type of shock in trauma and one of the most common in the ED overall.

The DO2 problem: Cardiac output drops because there isn't enough volume returning to the heart (↓ preload → ↓ stroke volume → ↓ CO → ↓ DO2). The pump works fine — it just has nothing to pump.

**Related notes:** Shock · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops · Blood Products and Transfusion · Blood Administration

---
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — hemorrhage reduces preload → reduced SV → reduced CO → reduced BP; the Frank-Starling curve shifts leftward with hypovolemia. Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — hypovolemic shock = DO2 failure from reduced CO; tissues compensate by increasing extraction (↑O2ER), but once extraction maxes out, lactate rises. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — compensatory tachycardia, vasoconstriction (catecholamines), and RAAS activation maintain BP temporarily but sacrifice peripheral DO2.*

Through the Oxygen Delivery and Consumption lens:

1. **Volume loss** → ↓ venous return (preload) → ↓ stroke volume → ↓ cardiac output → ↓ DO2
2. **Compensatory response** (see Homeostasis and Feedback Loops):
   - **Baroreceptor reflex** → ↑ HR, ↑ SVR (vasoconstriction) → maintains MAP (initially)
   - **RAAS activation** → Na+/water retention → attempts to restore volume
   - **ADH release** → water retention + vasoconstriction
   - **Catecholamine surge** → tachycardia, diaphoresis, pallor, anxiety
   - **Blood flow redistribution** → shunts away from skin, gut, kidneys → preserves brain, heart
3. **Decompensation** → compensatory mechanisms exhausted → MAP drops → tissue ischemia → anaerobic metabolism → lactate → metabolic acidosis → organ failure → cardiac arrest

### Hemodynamic Profile

| Parameter | Expected Finding | Why |
|-----------|-----------------|-----|
| **CO/CI** | ↓ | Not enough volume to generate output |
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Early (Compensated — Class I-II)
- Tachycardia (earliest and most reliable sign)
- Anxiety, restlessness
- Cool, pale skin (peripheral vasoconstriction)
- Mild tachypnea
- Normal blood pressure (compensated)
- Narrowing pulse pressure (SV dropping → lower systolic)
- Delayed capillary refill (>2 sec)

### Late (Decompensated — Class III-IV)
- Hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <65)
- Altered mental status → confusion → obtundation
- Oliguria → anuria
- Marked tachycardia (>120) or paradoxical bradycardia (pre-arrest)
- Mottled skin, cool extremities
- Weak/thready pulses
- Metabolic acidosis (rising lactate)
- Coagulopathy developing (hypothermia + acidosis + hemodilution = lethal triad in trauma)

### The Lethal Triad (Trauma)

In hemorrhagic shock, three factors create a self-reinforcing death spiral:

1. **Hypothermia** — massive volume resuscitation with cold fluids + exposure + impaired thermogenesis
2. **Acidosis** — anaerobic metabolism from hypoperfusion → lactic acidosis
3. **Coagulopathy** — dilution of clotting factors, hypothermia-impaired enzyme function, acidosis-impaired platelet function → Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy → MORE bleeding → MORE volume loss

> **Damage control resuscitation** targets all three: warm fluids/products, permissive hypotension (to reduce ongoing hemorrhage), and balanced blood product resuscitation (to replace clotting factors alongside RBCs).

---
conditions.management:
### Non-Hemorrhagic Hypovolemia

**Crystalloid resuscitation** is the mainstay:
- **Balanced crystalloid** (Lactated Ringer's or Plasmalyte) preferred over normal saline
  - NS has supraphysiologic chloride → hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis → renal vasoconstriction → AKI risk (SMART trial, SALT-ED trial)
- **Initial bolus:** 1-2 L IV, then reassess. Titrate to MAP ≥65, HR <100, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate clearance
- **Identify and treat the cause:** DKA → insulin + fluids. GI losses → antiemetics + PO/IV repletion. Third-spacing → treat underlying (pancreatitis, obstruction)

### Hemorrhagic Shock — Damage Control Resuscitation

This is fundamentally different from non-hemorrhagic management:

#### 1. Control the Bleeding (Source Control)
- **Direct pressure** to compressible hemorrhage
- **Tourniquet** for extremity hemorrhage (apply early, don't hesitate)
- **Emergent surgery** for non-compressible torso hemorrhage (OR or ED thoracotomy)
- **Interventional radiology** for embolization (pelvic fractures, GI bleeding)
- **Endoscopy** for upper GI hemorrhage

#### 2. Massive Transfusion Protocol (MTP)

Activate MTP early — don't wait for labs. Clinical indicators:
- Hemodynamic instability despite initial crystalloid
- Obvious massive hemorrhage
- ABC Score ≥2 (penetrating mechanism, SBP ≤90, HR ≥120, positive FAST)
- Estimated blood loss >1500 mL or expected to require >10 units pRBC

**Balanced resuscitation ratios:**

| Product | Ratio | Purpose |
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **Two large-bore IVs (16G or larger)** — flow rate is proportional to catheter RADIUS to the fourth power (Poiseuille's law). A short, fat 16G delivers much more volume per minute than a long, thin 20G. Consider IO access if IV difficult
- **Rapid infuser and fluid warmer** — for MTP, products must be warmed and can be delivered at >300 mL/min through a rapid infuser
- **Type and crossmatch immediately** — but give O-negative (or O-positive for males) uncrossmatched blood if patient is unstable. Don't wait for crossmatch in hemorrhagic shock
- **Serial labs** — CBC, BMP, coags (INR, PTT, fibrinogen), lactate, ionized calcium, ABG/VBG — repeat q30-60 min during active resuscitation
- **Trendelenburg or passive leg raise** — temporary measure to augment preload while preparing volume. Passive leg raise is also a diagnostic tool: if BP improves with leg raise → the patient is volume responsive
- **Watch for transfusion reactions** — see Transfusion Reactions. Slow down or stop if fever, rigors, urticaria, hemodynamic instability during transfusion
pearls: **Tachycardia is the canary in the coal mine.** A young trauma patient with HR 120 and BP 118/80 is NOT stable — they're compensating for significant blood loss. By the time BP drops, they've lost >30% of blood volume.

**Crystalloid is a bridge, not a destination.** In hemorrhagic shock, the patient needs BLOOD (RBCs for O2 carrying, plasma for clotting factors, platelets for hemostasis). Excessive crystalloid dilutes everything and worsens the lethal triad. Limit to 1-2L.
**Early Hgb is a liar.** Fresh hemorrhage doesn't change Hgb concentration because you're losing whole blood. The Hgb drops hours later after hemodilution. Treat the clinical picture, not the number.

**1:1:1 saves lives.** Balanced transfusion (pRBCs:FFP:platelets) addresses the full spectrum of hemorrhagic shock — oxygen carrying capacity AND hemostasis. Don't give 6 units of packed cells before thinking about clotting factors.

**Give TXA early or not at all.** Tranexamic acid must be given within 3 hours of injury (CRASH-2). After 3 hours, it may actually INCREASE mortality. Don't wait for labs — if you're activating MTP, give TXA.

conditions.title: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
conditions.overview: A chronic, progressive, fibrosing interstitial pneumonia of unknown cause, occurring primarily in older adults and limited to the lungs. Characterized by the histopathological and/or radiological pattern of usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP). It carries a poor prognosis with median survival of 3-5 years from diagnosis.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Wound Healing — IPF is aberrant wound healing: repeated microinjury to alveolar epithelium → abnormal fibroblast activation → myofibroblast persistence → collagen deposition → architectural destruction; fails to resolve (as normal wound healing does) and instead progresses. Oxygenation and Ventilation — fibrotic thickening of the alveolar-capillary membrane directly impairs O₂ diffusion; restrictive pattern on PFTs, reduced DLCO, and exertional hypoxia are the direct functional consequences.*

IPF results from aberrant wound healing in the alveolar epithelium, NOT a primarily inflammatory process:
1. Repetitive microinjury to alveolar epithelial cells (AECs) from unknown triggers
2. Dysfunctional repair: AECs undergo apoptosis rather than normal regeneration
3. Fibroblast activation and differentiation into myofibroblasts
4. Excessive extracellular matrix (collagen) deposition forming "fibroblastic foci"
5. Progressive architectural distortion → honeycombing → loss of gas exchange surface area
6. Temporal heterogeneity: areas of normal lung adjacent to established fibrosis adjacent to active fibroblastic foci (hallmark of UIP)

Key molecular mediators: TGF-β (master profibrotic cytokine), platelet-derived growth factor, endothelin-1.

Progressive fibrosis → restrictive physiology → impaired gas exchange → hypoxemia (initially exertional, then at rest) → pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure.
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Insidious onset** — symptoms often present 1-2 years before diagnosis
- Progressive exertional dyspnea (cardinal symptom)
- Chronic dry, nonproductive cough
- **Bibasilar "Velcro" crackles** on auscultation (present in >90%) — fine, dry, end-inspiratory
- Digital clubbing (25-50% of patients)
- Fatigue and exercise intolerance
- Late: cyanosis, weight loss, signs of right heart failure

### Complications
- Acute exacerbation of IPF (rapid deterioration over days-weeks; mortality >50%)
- Progressive respiratory failure
- Pulmonary hypertension (30-40% of patients)
- Lung cancer (incidence 10-15%; increased risk independent of smoking)
- Depression and anxiety
- GERD worsening and aspiration risk
conditions.management:
### Acute
- **Acute exacerbation of IPF:**
  - Rule out infection, PE, heart failure as causes of rapid decline
  - High-dose pulse Systemic Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 1g/day x 3 days) — limited evidence but widely used
  - Supportive care with supplemental O2
  - ICU admission often needed; mechanical ventilation carries very poor prognosis
  - Mortality >50% per exacerbation episode

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Antifibrotic therapy (disease-modifying — started at diagnosis):**
  - **Pirfenidone:** Reduces rate of FVC decline by ~50%; anti-fibrotic, anti-inflammatory
  - **Nintedanib:** Tyrosine kinase inhibitor; similar reduction in FVC decline
  - Neither reverses existing fibrosis — they slow progression
- **Supplemental oxygen:** For resting SpO2 <88% or exertional desaturation
- **Pulmonary rehabilitation:** Improves exercise tolerance, dyspnea, and quality of life
- **GERD management:** Aggressive acid suppression (controversial but commonly done)
- **Lung transplant evaluation:** Refer early (at diagnosis for suitable candidates); single or bilateral lung transplant is the only intervention that improves survival
- **Palliative care:** Early integration; opioids for refractory dyspnea; advance care planning
- **Avoid:** Steroids and immunosuppressants for chronic IPF (PANTHER trial showed HARM with prednisone + azathioprine + NAC)

### Pharmacology
- Pirfenidone — antifibrotic; take with food to reduce GI side effects; photosensitivity
conditions.nursingPriorities:
- **O2 titration is critical** — many patients need higher flow rates with activity than at rest; ensure ambulatory O2 is prescribed and titrated to SpO2 ≥88-90%
- Monitor for antifibrotic side effects: pirfenidone (GI upset, photosensitivity rash, elevated LFTs) and nintedanib (diarrhea — often dose-limiting, hepatotoxicity)
- LFT monitoring: baseline, then monthly x 3 months, then periodically for both antifibrotics
- Teach energy conservation and pacing techniques
- Fall risk assessment — exertional hypoxemia causes lightheadedness
- Psychosocial support: IPF carries heavy emotional burden; screen for depression/anxiety
- **Advance care planning conversations should happen early** — IPF trajectory is unpredictable; acute exacerbations can be fatal
- Vaccination: influenza annually, pneumococcal, COVID-19
- Teach patients to report any rapid worsening (new/worsened dyspnea over days) — may signal acute exacerbation
- Lung transplant education for eligible patients: process, expectations, immunosuppression
pearls: **"Velcro crackles" are the auscultatory signature of IPF** — fine, bibasilar, end-inspiratory crackles that sound like pulling apart Velcro; if you hear them on a patient with chronic dyspnea, advocate for HRCT
**Never put an IPF patient on chronic steroids** — the PANTHER trial proved that prednisone + azathioprine + NAC increased mortality; this is fibrosis, not inflammation
**FVC decline is the single most important number to track** — a drop of ≥10% over 6-12 months predicts poor survival; serial PFTs are essential
**Acute exacerbations are the "heart attacks" of IPF** — rapid, unpredictable deterioration with >50% mortality; counsel patients and families early about prognosis and goals of care
**Oxygen needs change dramatically with activity** — a patient who sats 92% at rest may drop to 78% walking to the bathroom; always assess exertional oxygenation

conditions.title: Infective Endocarditis
conditions.overview: Infective endocarditis is an infection of the endocardial surface of the heart, most commonly involving the heart valves. Organisms (usually bacteria) colonize damaged or prosthetic endocardium, forming vegetations — masses of platelets, fibrin, microorganisms, and inflammatory cells. IE can destroy valve tissue, cause embolic phenomena to virtually any organ, and trigger immune-complex disease. Despite advances in diagnosis and treatment, mortality remains 15-30%.
conditions.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — endocardial injury (turbulent flow, intracardiac device) → platelet-fibrin thrombus on valve → bacterial seeding → vegetation (platelet-fibrin-bacteria complex). Infection and Infectious Disease — bacteremia is the proximate cause; portal of entry (dental, IV, skin) determines organism; adhesin proteins mediate valve colonization. Inflammation and Immune Response — embolic phenomena (stroke, renal infarct) = fragments of infected vegetation; immune complex deposition (Osler's nodes, Roth spots) = type III hypersensitivity.*

1. **Endothelial damage** (from turbulent flow, prosthetic material, degenerative changes) → sterile platelet-fibrin deposition (nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis — NBTE)
2. **Bacteremia** (from dental procedures, IV access, skin breakdown, GI/GU procedures) → organisms adhere to damaged endothelium or NBTE
3. **Vegetation formation:** Bacteria proliferate within platelet-fibrin matrix → forms vegetation (relatively protected from host immune defenses and antibiotics)
4. **Local valve destruction:**
   - Leaflet perforation or destruction → acute valvular regurgitation → heart failure
   - Abscess formation (periannular/root abscess) → conduction abnormalities (especially aortic valve IE → AV block)
   - Fistula formation (aorta-to-cardiac chamber)
5. **Embolic phenomena:** Vegetations fragment and embolize:
conditions.keyFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Subacute IE (Strep viridans, Enterococcus):**
- Insidious onset over weeks to months
- Low-grade fever, night sweats, malaise, weight loss
- Anorexia, fatigue, arthralgias, myalgias
- New or changing heart murmur (~85%)

**Acute IE (Staph aureus):**
- Abrupt onset, high fever, rigors
- Rapid valve destruction → acute heart failure
